topics.title: Strategy of Experimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strategy of Experimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Strategy of Experimentation** refers to the systematic, planned, and statistically rigorous procedure adopted by an engineer or scientist to investigate the behaviour of a process or system by deliberately manipulating one or more input variables (called **factors**) and observing the resulting changes in one or more output variables (called **responses**).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Design and Analysis of Experiments (DOE)*, the strategy of experimentation is defined as the structured methodology that governs:

- **What** variables to study,
- **How many** experimental runs to perform,
- **In what order** the runs should be executed, and
- **How** the resulting data should be analysed to obtain valid, reliable, and actionable conclusions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT865 – Module 1):**
> The official KTU 2024 syllabus frames "Strategy of Experimentation" as the *bridge between ad-hoc, intuition-driven testing and a formal, statistically-valid experimental campaign*. It is the **first cognitive step** before any concrete design (CRD, RBD, LSD, Factorial) is constructed.

### 1.2 Need for a Strategy of Experimentation

Without a proper strategy, experiments tend to be:

1. **Inefficient** – Too many runs are performed for too little information.
2. **Biased** – Conclusions are contaminated by uncontrolled nuisance variables.
3. **Irreproducible** – Other engineers cannot replicate the results.
4. **Non-optimal** – Sub-optimal settings of factors are selected as the "best".

A good strategy ensures **minimum cost, maximum information, and valid inference**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Masterchef's Recipe Development**
>
> Imagine a chef trying to perfect a new biryani recipe. She has **5 candidate ingredients** (basmati rice type, oil quantity, spice level, cooking time, salt) — each can be set at **3 different levels** (low, medium, high).
>
> - **No Strategy (One-Factor-At-A-Time / OFAT):** She changes salt today, observes, then tomorrow changes oil, observes… This takes **3 × 5 = 15 days minimum**, and she may completely miss that *spice level interacts with cooking time*.
> - **With a Proper DOE Strategy:** She plans a **L₉ Taguchi design** or a **2⁵ factorial**, runs all combinations in a randomized order over **just 2 days**, statistically extracts the **main effects and interactions**, and identifies the optimum combination with a quantifiable confidence level.
>
> The "Strategy of Experimentation" is exactly the **master-plan** the chef uses — choosing the design, the run order, the blocking, and the analytical tool — to ensure her biryani is perfected using the **fewest trials with the strongest evidence**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology — Standard KTU Metrics

The following **bolded** terms form the standard vocabulary of every KTU examination answer:

- **Factor (Independent Variable):** A controllable input variable whose effect on the response is being investigated.
- **Level:** The specific value (or setting) assigned to a factor in a given run.
- **Response (Dependent Variable):** The measured output whose variation is to be explained.
- **Treatment:** A unique combination of factor levels applied in a run.
- **Experimental Unit:** The physical entity (specimen, batch, batch of material) that receives a treatment.
- **Replication:** Independent repetition of a treatment under identical conditions.
- **Error (Residual):** The unexplained random variation in the response.
- **Replication vs. Repetition:** *Replication* involves independent experimental units; *repetition* is the same unit measured multiple times.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Historical Context — Why Fisher?

The foundation of modern experimental strategy was laid by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher** in the early 1920s at the **Rothamsted Agricultural Experimental Station** in England. He demonstrated that valid inference about cause-and-effect relationships requires three simultaneous principles:

1. **Randomization** – Allocation of treatments to experimental units by a chance mechanism.
2. **Replication** – Independent repetition of treatments to estimate experimental error.
3. **Blocking** – Grouping homogeneous units together to control known sources of nuisance variation.

Later, **Box, Hunter, and Hunter (1978)** extended this foundation into the realm of industrial and chemical experimentation through *Statistics for Experimenters* — the de-facto reference style for KTU 2024 DOE pedagogy.

### 2.2 The Conventional Approach vs. The DOE Strategy

This is the **highest-yield comparison** for KTU Part-A questions (3 marks).

| Aspect | **Conventional (OFAT) Strategy** | **DOE (Statistical) Strategy** |
|---|---|---|
| Variable change | One variable at a time | Many variables simultaneously |
| Total runs | Very high ($3^k$ for $k$ factors at 3 levels) | Drastically reduced (Taguchi OAs) |
| Interaction detection | **Impossible** (by design) | Built-in detection of interactions |
| Optimum obtained | Local optimum only | Global optimum likely |
| Statistical validity | Weak / qualitative | Strong / quantitative (p-values, CIs) |
| Engineer effort | Trial-and-error feel | Planned, documented, reproducible |
| Confidence in result | Low | High (with controlled error) |

### 2.3 The Eight-Step Strategy of Experimentation (KTU High-Yield Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames experimentation as a **recursive, eight-step strategy**:

1. **Problem Definition** — Clearly state the objective in measurable terms.
2. **Response Variable Selection** — Choose *y* that meaningfully captures the outcome.
3. **Factor & Level Identification** — Brainstorm and *screen* potential factors (using prior knowledge, fishbone diagrams, etc.).
4. **Choice of Experimental Design** — CRD, RBD, LSD, Full/Fractional Factorial, Taguchi OA, RSM, etc.
5. **Performing the Runs** — Execute experiments in **randomized order** with proper **replication** and **blocking**.
6. **Data Collection & Statistical Analysis** — ANOVA, regression, residual diagnostics.
7. **Conclusions & Recommendations** — Identify significant factors and optimal settings.
8. **Confirmation Run** — Perform independent verification experiments at the predicted optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> Step 8 — the **Confirmation Run** — is the most-missed step in KTU answers. Examiners repeatedly deduct marks when students jump from "ANOVA says p < 0.05" directly to "therefore set factor A at level 2" without verifying the optimum with fresh data.

### 2.4 Logical Pillars Behind the Strategy

- **Why Randomize?** — It averages out the effect of *uncontrollable nuisance variables* and validates the assumptions of ANOVA (independence of errors).
- **Why Replicate?** — It provides the **degrees of freedom** needed to estimate the experimental error variance $\sigma^2$. Without replication, no test of significance is possible.
- **Why Block?** — It removes known systematic variability (e.g., shift, operator, batch) from the error term, *increasing precision*.
- **Why Factorial instead of OFAT?** — Because in real processes, factors **interact** — the effect of temperature may depend on the level of pressure. OFAT cannot detect this.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total number of treatment combinations | $N = L^k$ | $L$ levels, $k$ factors |
| 2 | Single-factor OFAT runs | $N_{\text{OFAT}} = 1 + L(k-1)$ | For $k$ factors at $L$ levels each |
| 3 | Degrees of freedom for error | $df_E = N - a$ | $a$ = number of treatments |
| 4 | Sample variance of response | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n-1}$ | Unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$ |
| 5 | Standard error of mean | $SE(\bar{y}) = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Variability of $\bar{y}$ |
| 6 | Replication impact on SE | $SE \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$ | Quadrupling runs halves SE |
| 7 | Relative efficiency (2 designs) | $RE = \dfrac{\text{Var of design B}}{\text{Var of design A}}$ | Higher RE = better precision |
| 8 | Contrast sum (full factorial) | $\text{Contrast}_A = \sum (\pm 1)\, y_i$ | Sign-coded effect estimation |
| 9 | Sum of Squares Total | $SS_T = \sum y_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y_i)^2}{N}$ | Total variability |
| 10 | Sum of Squares Treatment | $SS_{\text{Trt}} = \sum \dfrac{T_j^2}{n_j} - \dfrac{(\sum y_i)^2}{N}$ | Between-treatment variability |
| 11 | Experimental error | $SS_E = SS_T - SS_{\text{Trt}}$ | Within-treatment variability |
| 12 | F-statistic | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{\text{Trt}}}{MS_E}$ | Test statistic for treatment effect |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown-Table Safety Note:** Where an absolute-value sign appears in a formula above (e.g., a contrast magnitude), it is intentionally rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve table integrity. **Never** use a literal pipe `|` inside a markdown table row.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Strategy of Experimentation is the **backbone of modern Quality Engineering**, with applications in:

- **Manufacturing:** Optimizing CNC machining parameters (cutting speed, feed, depth) for minimum surface roughness.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Maximising yield of a reactor while minimising by-product formation.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Formulation development using mixture designs.
- **Electronics:** Robust design of microchip lithography processes.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete mix optimisation — strength vs. workability vs. cost.
- **Software Engineering:** A/B testing and performance benchmarking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the **analytical foundation** that justifies why a particular strategy (e.g., factorial over OFAT) is mathematically superior.

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Replication Reduces Standard Error

Let $y_{ij}$ be the $j^{\text{th}}$ observation under treatment $i$, where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, a$ and $j = 1, 2, \ldots, r$ (replications).

$$
\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}
$$

The sample mean of treatment $i$ is $\bar{y}_{i\cdot}$. The **variance of this sample mean** is:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Linearity of variance:** Because all observations under treatment $i$ are i.i.d. with variance $\sigma^2$,

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\text{Var}(y_{ij}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\,(r\,\sigma^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Simplification:**

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard error:**

$$
SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}
$$

**Conclusion / Engineering Insight:** To **halve** the standard error of the treatment mean, the engineer must perform **four times** as many replications. This non-linear cost is the *driving economic reason* behind DOE strategy: it is far cheaper to add a few well-chosen factorial points than to replicate a one-factor-at-a-time campaign.

### 3.2 Derivation: Decomposition of Total Variability (SS_T)

For any experimental observation $y_{ij}$, the linear model is:

$$
y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}
$$

where $\mu$ is the grand mean, $\tau_i$ is the effect of treatment $i$ (with $\sum \tau_i = 0$), and $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

Define the correction factor:

$$
CF = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}\right)^{2}}{N}, \quad N = a \cdot r
$$

**Step 1 — Total Sum of Squares (corrected):**

$$
SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}^{2} \;-\; CF
$$

**Step 2 — Treatment Sum of Squares:**

$$
SS_{\text{Trt}} = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\frac{T_i^{2}}{r} \;-\; CF
$$

where $T_i = \sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}$ is the $i^{\text{th}}$ treatment total.

**Step 3 — Error Sum of Squares (by subtraction):**

$$
SS_E = SS_T - SS_{\text{Trt}}
$$

**Step 4 — Partition of degrees of freedom:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
df_T &= N - 1 \\
df_{\text{Trt}} &= a - 1 \\
df_E &= N - a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mean Squares:**

$$
MS_{\text{Trt}} = \frac{SS_{\text{Trt}}}{a - 1}, \qquad MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{N - a}
$$

**Step 6 — F-test:**

$$
F_0 = \frac{MS_{\text{Trt}}}{MS_E} \;\;\sim\;\; F_{(a-1,\, N-a)} \text{ under } H_0
$$

**Conclusion:** If $F_0 > F_{\alpha,\, a-1,\, N-a}$, we **reject** $H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_a$ and conclude that at least one treatment mean differs. This single F-test is the **statistical backbone** of every CRD/RBD/LSD and every KTU ANOVA problem.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right DOE Strategy

The following matrix guides an engineer from *problem type* to *design choice*. This is exactly the kind of decision-tree KTU examiners expect in 14-mark Part-B answers.

| If the problem looks like… | Dominant strategy | KTU Design Code |
|---|---|---|
| One factor, homogeneous units, no nuisance variable | **Completely Randomized Design (CRD)** | Single-factor ANOVA |
| One factor, one nuisance variable known | **Randomized Block Design (RBD)** | Two-way ANOVA (without interaction) |
| Two factors, complete interaction study desired | **Full Factorial** ($2^k$ or $3^k$) | Factorial ANOVA |
| Many factors, only main effects of interest | **Fractional Factorial / Taguchi OA** | $2^{k-p}$ design |
| Optimum is in a *curved* region, response is a continuous surface | **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** — CCD, BBD | Second-order polynomial fit |
| Hard-to-change factors present | **Split-Plot / Split-Split-Plot Design** | Mixed-model ANOVA |
| Mixture problem (ingredients summing to 100 %) | **Mixture Design** (simplex-centroid, simplex-lattice) | Scheffé polynomial |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Anchor — Run-Count Comparison

Suppose an engineer wishes to study $k = 4$ factors at $L = 3$ levels each.

**Strategy A — One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT):**

$$
N_{\text{OFAT}} = 1 + L(k-1) = 1 + 3(4-1) = 1 + 9 = 10 \text{ runs}
$$

**Strategy B — Full Factorial (3⁴):**

$$
N_{\text{FF}} = L^{k} = 3^{4} = 81 \text{ runs}
$$

**Strategy C — Taguchi L₉ Orthogonal Array (saturated for 4 factors):**

$$
N_{\text{L9}} = 9 \text{ runs}
$$

**Engineering Inference:** The Taguchi strategy delivers **interaction detection at 11 % of the cost** of the full factorial and only **one run less than OFAT** — yet it captures *both* main effects *and* selected two-factor interactions that OFAT entirely misses. This is precisely the **economic and informational superiority** of a well-planned experimental strategy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Eight-Step Strategic Loop of Experimentation

The following **Mermaid flow-diagram** captures the recursive, eight-step strategy as taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each node is named with alphanumeric identifiers to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword safety rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1: PROBLEM DEFINITION"] --> A2["STEP 2: RESPONSE SELECTION"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3: FACTOR AND LEVEL IDENTIFICATION"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4: CHOICE OF EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5: PERFORM RANDOMIZED RUNS"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6: DATA COLLECTION AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7: CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS"]
    A7 --> A8{"STEP 8: CONFIRMATION RUN PASSES?"}
    A8 -- NO --> A3
    A8 -- YES --> A9["DEPLOY OPTIMUM TO PRODUCTION"]
```

**Interpretation of the loop:** If the confirmation run fails to reproduce the predicted optimum, the engineer must return to **Step 3** (re-screen the factors or re-choose the levels) — making this a true *iterative strategy*, not a linear checklist.

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Inputs and Outputs of an Experimental Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["ENGINEERING OBJECTIVE"] --> B2["FACTOR SCREENING TOOLS"]
    B2 --> B3["DESIGN SELECTION ENGINE"]
    B3 --> B4["RUN-ORDER RANDOMIZER"]
    B4 --> B5["PHYSICAL EXPERIMENTAL RUNS"]
    B5 --> B6["DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM"]
    B6 --> B7["ANOVA AND REGRESSION ENGINE"]
    B7 --> B8["OPTIMUM FACTOR SETTINGS"]
    B8 --> B9["ENGINEERING DECISION"]
```

**Interpretation:** Each block represents a functional sub-system of the strategy. The *Design Selection Engine* and *ANOVA Engine* together constitute the statistical core of the strategy, while the *Randomizer* guarantees validity through Fisher's principle of randomization.

### 4.3 Conventional vs. DOE — Conceptual Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONV["CONVENTIONAL OFAT APPROACH"]
        C1["Fix all factors at baseline"] --> C2["Vary Factor A only"]
        C2 --> C3["Record best A"]
        C3 --> C4["Vary Factor B at best A"]
        C4 --> C5["Record best B"]
        C5 --> C6["Repeat for all k factors"]
    end

    subgraph DOE["STATISTICAL DOE APPROACH"]
        D1["Choose design (e.g. 2^k factorial)"] --> D2["Randomize run order"]
        D2 --> D3["Execute all N runs"]
        D3 --> D4["Compute main effects"]
        D4 --> D5["Test interactions via ANOVA"]
        D5 --> D6["Predict and confirm optimum"]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** The OFAT architecture is *serial and sequential*; the DOE architecture is *parallel and integrated*. The DOE architecture detects interactions that the OFAT architecture is structurally incapable of revealing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**

**Q1. Define the term "Strategy of Experimentation" as applicable to the Design of Experiments. Why is a systematic strategy preferred over a one-factor-at-a-time approach?**

**Model Answer:**

The *Strategy of Experimentation* is the **systematic, statistically-planned procedure** used to investigate the effect of one or more input variables (factors) on an output variable (response). It encompasses:

- Defining the **objective** and **response**,
- Identifying the relevant **factors and levels**,
- Selecting an appropriate **experimental design** (CRD, RBD, factorial, etc.),
- Performing **randomized, replicated runs**,
- Analysing the data using **ANOVA / regression**, and
- **Confirming** the predicted optimum with a verification run.

It is preferred over the one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) approach because:

1. It **detects interactions** between factors, which OFAT cannot,
2. It yields a **global optimum**, not just a local one,
3. It provides a **quantitative measure of confidence** (p-values, confidence intervals), and
4. It uses **fewer experimental runs** for the same information content (especially with Taguchi OAs).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**

**Q2. State and briefly explain the three fundamental principles of experimental strategy laid down by Fisher.**

**Model Answer:**

The three fundamental principles introduced by **Sir R. A. Fisher** are:

1. **Randomization:** The allocation of treatments to experimental units and the order in which the runs are performed must be governed by a *chance mechanism* (e.g., a random-number table). This averages out the effect of *uncontrolled* nuisance variables and validates the independence assumption of ANOVA.

2. **Replication:** Each treatment must be applied to *more than one experimental unit* independently. Replication provides the *degrees of freedom* required to estimate the experimental error variance $\sigma^2$ and thus to perform any test of statistical significance.

3. **Blocking:** A *known* source of nuisance variability (operator, shift, batch, day) is grouped into blocks so that units within a block are as homogeneous as possible. Blocking removes this systematic variation from the error term and *increases the precision* of treatment comparisons.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 1, 14 marks]**

### ⭐ Question A — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the conventional one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) approach to experimentation. Discuss, with suitable illustrations, **three major limitations** of this approach in the context of modern industrial experimentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **eight-step Strategy of Experimentation** recommended in the KTU 2024 DOE syllabus. For each step, briefly indicate its purpose. Why is the *confirmation run* (Step 8) considered essential even after a statistically significant ANOVA result? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Conventional OFAT Approach — Limitations**

The OFAT approach keeps all factors constant except one; that single factor is varied across its levels to find its "best" setting. The procedure is then repeated for the next factor, and so on.

**Three Major Limitations:**

1. **Failure to detect interactions:** OFAT assumes factors act *independently*. In reality, the effect of factor $A$ may depend on the level of factor $B$ (an $A \times B$ interaction). OFAT, by varying only one factor at a time, **cannot reveal such interactions**, leading to incorrect or incomplete conclusions.

2. **Local optimum problem:** Because OFAT explores one dimension at a time, it converges to a *local* optimum, not the *global* optimum. It may settle on a factor setting that is sub-optimal when interactions exist.

3. **Large number of runs, low information density:** For $k$ factors at $L$ levels each, OFAT requires $1 + L(k-1)$ runs. For $k = 5$ and $L = 3$, that is 11 runs — yet only main effects are estimated, and no interactions are detected. A properly designed $2^5$ factorial would yield *all* 5 main effects *and* all 10 two-factor interactions in just 32 runs.

**(b) The Eight-Step Strategy of Experimentation**

| Step | Activity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Problem Definition | Translate the engineering objective into a measurable, unambiguous statement. |
| 2 | Response Selection | Choose the output variable(s) that genuinely capture the desired outcome. |
| 3 | Factor & Level Identification | List all candidate factors; assign realistic, operationally-feasible levels. |
| 4 | Design Selection | Choose CRD / RBD / Factorial / Taguchi OA based on factors and objective. |
| 5 | Randomized Runs | Execute the runs in randomized order with replication and blocking. |
| 6 | Statistical Analysis | ANOVA, regression, residual diagnostics to identify significant effects. |
| 7 | Conclusions & Recommendations | Identify the optimal factor settings and quantify the expected improvement. |
| 8 | Confirmation Run | Perform independent runs at the predicted optimum to verify the result. |

**Why is the Confirmation Run Essential?**

- ANOVA tells us whether *differences exist*, not whether the *predicted optimum* is achievable in practice.
- The confirmation run provides **independent verification** outside the original experimental error estimate.
- It catches errors due to *model misspecification*, *uncontrolled drift*, or *factor-level interpolation* issues.
- It transforms a *statistically* significant result into an *engineering* certainty — a critical step before scale-up or production deployment.

---

### ⭐ Question B — (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Differentiate between *replication* and *repetition* in the context of experimental strategy. With a clear mathematical derivation, show how replication reduces the standard error of a treatment mean. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical engineer wishes to study the effect of **4 factors**, each at **3 levels**, on the yield of a reaction. Compare the number of experimental runs required under (i) the OFAT approach, (ii) the full $3^4$ factorial, and (iii) the Taguchi L₉ orthogonal array. Comment on the information obtained in each case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Replication vs. Repetition, and Reduction of Standard Error**

**Replication** means performing the treatment on *independent* experimental units (separate specimens, separate batches). It captures variation *between* units and provides genuine degrees of freedom for error estimation.

**Repetition** means measuring the *same* experimental unit multiple times. It captures only *measurement* variability, not unit-to-unit variability, and therefore yields a *biased* (under-estimated) error variance.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Let $y_{ij}$ be the $j^{\text{th}}$ replicate observation under treatment $i$, with $j = 1, \ldots, r$. The sample mean of treatment $i$ is

$$
\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}.
$$

Because each $y_{ij}$ is i.i.d. with variance $\sigma^2$,

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\text{Var}(y_{ij}) = \frac{r\,\sigma^2}{r^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}.
$$

Therefore

$$
SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}.
$$

The standard error is **inversely proportional to the square root of the number of replications**, demonstrating that replication *substantially* improves precision.

**(b) Run-Count Comparison**

Let $k = 4$ factors and $L = 3$ levels.

**(i) OFAT Approach:**

$$
N_{\text{OFAT}} = 1 + L(k-1) = 1 + 3(4-1) = 1 + 9 = 10 \text{ runs}.
$$

**Information obtained:** Only *main effects*, one factor at a time. **No interactions detected.**

**(ii) Full $3^4$ Factorial:**

$$
N_{\text{FF}} = L^{k} = 3^{4} = 81 \text{ runs}.
$$

**Information obtained:** All main effects + all two-factor + all three-factor + the four-factor interaction. Highly precise, but expensive.

**(iii) Taguchi L₉ Orthogonal Array:**

$$
N_{\text{L9}} = 9 \text{ runs}.
$$

**Information obtained:** All 4 main effects clearly estimated; selected two-factor interactions *confounded* but partially recoverable with interaction columns.

**Inference:** The L₉ OA achieves **90 % run-reduction compared to full factorial** and **only 1 fewer run than OFAT**, while providing a **balanced estimate of all main effects** and partial interaction information — a textbook example of the **economy and efficiency** of a well-planned DOE strategy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse replication with repetition** — most students lose 2–3 marks in Part-B on this distinction alone. Replication = independent units; Repetition = same unit, multiple readings.
> 2. **Never skip the confirmation run** — examiners explicitly deduct marks if Step 8 is omitted from the eight-step strategy.
> 3. **Do not write "$p < 0.05$ therefore the factor is significant"** without specifying the *null hypothesis being tested* (e.g., $H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2$).
> 4. **Taguchi OAs do not magically eliminate confounding** — interactions can still be confounded with each other; state this explicitly in 14-mark answers to earn full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Strategy of Experimentation = the master-plan**, not a single test. It governs *what* to vary, *how many* runs to perform, *in what order*, and *how* to analyse.
- **Fisher's three principles** — *Randomization, Replication, Blocking* — are non-negotiable in any valid DOE strategy.
- The **eight-step strategy** is *iterative*: a failed confirmation run sends the engineer back to Step 3 (factor screening).
- **OFAT cannot detect interactions** — always state this when contrasting OFAT with DOE.
- **Run-count formula for OFAT:** $N_{\text{OFAT}} = 1 + L(k-1)$.
- **Run-count formula for full factorial:** $N_{\text{FF}} = L^k$.
- **Taguchi Orthogonal Arrays** (e.g., L₄, L₉, L₁₆, L₂₇) are saturated or near-saturated designs that maximise information per run.
- **Standard error shrinks as $1/\sqrt{r}$** — quadrupling replications halves the SE.
- **Confirmation run (Step 8)** transforms statistical significance into engineering certainty.
- **Replication ≠ Repetition** — replication uses independent units; repetition only re-measures the same unit.
- **Choice of design depends on** the *number of factors*, *number of levels*, *presence of nuisance variables*, and the *objective* (screening, characterisation, or optimisation).
- **Randomization order** must be generated *before* the experiment begins — never after seeing the data.
- **Blocking** removes the effect of *known* nuisance variables; it cannot rescue an experiment with *unknown* confounders.
- **Degrees of freedom for error** must be ≥ 2 for a meaningful F-test; plan replication accordingly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Applications of Experimental Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Typical Applications of Experimental Design # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a systematic, statistical methodology for planning, conducting, analyzing, and interpreting controlled tests to evaluate the effects of one or more input variables (called *factors*) on a measured response variable. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT865 syllabus), the **Typical Applications of Experimental Design** refer to the diverse engineering, scientific, and industrial problem domains where structured experimentation is applied to optimize processes, improve product quality, reduce variability, and accelerate innovation cycles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Experimental Design is NOT about running "trials randomly." It is a *structured, statistically valid, and economically efficient* method of investigation pioneered by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher (1925)** at the Rothamsted Agricultural Experimental Station, England. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cooking Experiment" Imagine a chef (the experimenter) trying to perfect a new dish. The chef has many controllable inputs: | Kitchen Variable | DOE Terminology | |---|---| | Oven Temperature | **Factor A (Independent Variable)** | | Baking Time | **Factor B (Independent Variable)** | | Amount of Sugar | **Factor C (Independent Variable)** | | Taste Rating | **Response Variable (Y)** | If the chef changes all variables haphazardly (one trial high temperature + long time + more sugar), the result is **confounded** — the chef will never know *which* input caused the change in taste. **DOE** is the scientific cookbook: it tells the chef how to vary one (or more) ingredients *at a time, in a planned pattern*, so that the contribution of each ingredient to the final taste is mathematically isolable. --- ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Any Experimental Application Regardless of the application domain, every DOE study must satisfy **three foundational pillars**: > [!NOTE] > **Pillar 1 — Randomization:** Allocation of experimental runs to experimental units using a random mechanism (e.g., lottery, RNG). Eliminates systematic bias. > > **Pillar 2 — Replication:** Independent repetition of experimental runs under identical conditions. Provides the **estimate of experimental error (σ²)**. > > **Pillar 3 — Blocking:** Grouping of homogeneous experimental units together to control for known sources of nuisance variability. --- ## 1.4 Why is Experimental Design Important? (KTU Syllabus Motivation) | S.No. | Problem Without DOE | Solution Provided by DOE | |---|---|---| | 1 | Trial-and-error approach is costly & slow | Efficient, minimal-run screening | | 2 | One-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) misses interactions | Factorial designs capture **interactions** | | 3 | Cannot quantify uncertainty | Provides **confidence intervals** and **p-values** | | 4 | Confounded effects lead to wrong conclusions | Orthogonal designs **deconfound** effects | > [!TIP] > **Real-World KTQ Tip:** In KTU exams, whenever asked "why DOE?", always write: *"DOE provides a structured framework to simultaneously study multiple factors, detect interactions, quantify noise, and arrive at optimal operating conditions with statistical confidence — using fewer runs than trial-and-error."* --- ## 1.5 Typical Applications — Classification Overview The applications of Experimental Design are typically classified into **six broad domains**: 1. **Comparative Experiments** — comparing two or more treatments/processes. 2. **Screening / Factor-Identification Experiments** — identifying the "vital few" factors from the "trivial many." 3. **Optimization Experiments** — finding the optimum operating conditions (e.g., Response Surface Methodology). 4. **Robust Design Experiments** — making products/processes insensitive to uncontrollable noise factors (Taguchi Methods). 5. **Mixture Experiments** — optimizing the *proportions* of components in a blend. 6. **Evolutionary Operation (EVOP)** — continuous on-line process optimization in manufacturing plants. Each of these is mapped to a specific design in the upcoming sections. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Typical Applications The KTU 2024 PECHT865 syllabus emphasizes the following typical application contexts. Let us break them down one by one. ### Application Category 1: Industrial Process Optimization **Goal:** Maximize yield, minimize defects, reduce cycle time. | Application | Factors Studied | Response | |---|---|---| | Semiconductor Wafer Etching | RF Power, Pressure, Gas Flow Rate | Etch Rate (nm/min) | | Injection Molding | Temperature, Pressure, Cooling Time | Tensile Strength | | CNC Machining | Spindle Speed, Feed Rate, Depth of Cut | Surface Roughness (Ra) | | Heat Treatment | Soaking Temp., Holding Time, Quench Medium | Hardness (HRC) | **Recommended DOE Strategy:** **2^k Factorial Design** → **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** for fine-tuning. --- ### Application Category 2: Comparative Studies (CRD / RBD / LSD) Used when the *primary objective* is to compare **t means** (where t = number of treatments). **Three Principal Designs:** $$\text{Completely Randomized Design (CRD)} \rightarrow \text{Single factor, homogeneous units}$$ $$\text{Randomized Block Design (RBD)} \rightarrow \text{Single factor, one blocking variable}$$ $$\text{Latin Square Design (LSD)} \rightarrow \text{Single factor, two blocking variables}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Rule for KTU Exams:** > - 1 source of nuisance variability → use **RBD** > - 2 sources of nuisance variability → use **LSD** > - No nuisance source + small number of treatments → use **CRD** --- ### Application Category 3: Screening — The 2^k Factorial Design When the number of potential factors (k) is large (e.g., 5–15) and we want to identify which factors significantly influence the response, a **2^k (Two-Level) Full Factorial Design** is employed. **Total runs required:** $$N = 2^k$$ | k (factors) | N (runs) | Application | |---|---|---| | 2 | 4 | Pilot study | | 3 | 8 | Initial process study | | 4 | 16 | Multi-factor screening | | 5 | 32 | Industrial screening | For k ≥ 6, **Fractional Factorial Designs (2^(k-p))** are used to economize on runs. --- ### Application Category 4: Response Surface Methodology (RSM) When the goal is **optimization** (find the maximum or minimum response), RSM is applied *after* screening has identified the key factors. **Two Most Common RSM Designs:** | Design Type | When to Use | |---|---| | **Central Composite Design (CCD)** | Sequential optimization; allows estimation of full quadratic model | | **Box-Behnken Design (BBD)** | Avoids extreme corner points (axial runs); safer for industrial processes | The fitted second-order model is: $$Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Rule:** In all KTU answer sheets, avoid writing absolute values with `|x|`. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX or write `mod(x)` in plain text. This avoids confusion with determinants in valuation scripts. --- ## 2.3 Why These Applications Matter in Engineering | Engineering Field | Critical Application | |---|---| | Chemical Engineering | Reactor yield optimization, distillation column design | | Mechanical Engineering | Welding parameter optimization, fatigue life testing | | Civil Engineering | Concrete mix design (mixture experiments!), structural testing | | Electrical Engineering | Antenna design optimization, semiconductor yield | | Biotechnology | Fermentation process optimization, bio-reactor design | | Pharmaceutical | Tablet formulation, drug stability studies | | Computer Science | Algorithm benchmarking, A/B testing, ML hyperparameter tuning | > [!TIP] > **For KTU Project Viva:** When asked *"Give one real-world example of DOE"*, always quote an example from **your branch of engineering** with specific factors and a response. Example for Chemical: *"In a chemical reactor, I would study Temperature, Pressure, and Catalyst Concentration (factors) on Yield% (response) using a 2³ factorial design with 2 center point replicates."* # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom for Common Designs ### Derivation 1 — Completely Randomized Design (CRD) ANOVA Table Let $t$ = number of treatments, $r$ = number of replicates per treatment, $N = t \times r$ = total observations. **Total Sum of Squares (SST):** $$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$$ **Treatment Sum of Squares (SS_Tr):** $$SS_{Tr} = r\sum_{i=1}^{t}(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$$ **Error Sum of Squares (SSE):** $$SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})^2$$ **Fundamental Partition (Identity):** $$SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_E$$ **Degrees of Freedom Partition:** $$(N - 1) = (t - 1) + (t)(r - 1)$$ Verification: $$N - 1 = tr - 1$$ $$t - 1 + tr - t = tr - 1 \quad \checkmark$$ **Mean Squares:** $$MS_{Tr} = \frac{SS_{Tr}}{t - 1}, \quad MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{t(r-1)}$$ **F-Test Statistic:** $$F_0 = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$$ Decision Rule: Reject $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \dots = \mu_t$ if $F_0 > F_{\alpha, \, t-1, \, t(r-1)}$. --- ### Derivation 2 — Randomized Block Design (RBD) ANOVA Let $t$ = treatments, $b$ = blocks, $N = tb$. $$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$$ $$SS_{Tr} = b\sum_{i=1}^{t}(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$$ $$SS_{Bl} = t\sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{y}_{.j} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$$ $$SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{.j} + \bar{y}_{..})^2$$ **Identity (with blocking):** $$SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_{Bl} + SS_E$$ **df Partition:** $$tb - 1 = (t - 1) + (b - 1) + (t - 1)(b - 1)$$ Verification: $$tb - 1 = t - 1 + b - 1 + tb - t - b + 1 = tb - 1 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### Derivation 3 — Latin Square Design (LSD) ANOVA For an $n \times n$ Latin square, with $n$ treatments, $n$ rows, and $n$ columns. $N = n^2$. $$SS_T = SS_{Row} + SS_{Col} + SS_{Tr} + SS_E$$ **df Partition:** $$n^2 - 1 = (n-1) + (n-1) + (n-1) + (n-1)(n-2)$$ Verification: $$n^2 - 1 = 3n - 3 + n^2 - 3n + 2 = n^2 - 1 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### Derivation 4 — 2^k Factorial Design — Effect Calculation For a 2-level factorial with $k$ factors, each factor has levels coded as **−1 (low)** and **+1 (high)**. The design matrix is constructed by combining all sign combinations. **Main Effect of Factor A:** $$A = \frac{\sum [y \cdot \text{(contrast vector for A)}]}{N \cdot 2^{k-2}}$$ Equivalently, using contrast $C_A$: $$C_A = \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i y_i \quad \text{where } a_i = \pm 1$$ $$A = \frac{2 C_A}{N}$$ **Two-Factor Interaction Effect (AB):** $$AB = \frac{2 C_{AB}}{N}, \quad \text{where } C_{AB} = \sum a_i b_i y_i$$ **Variance of an Effect:** $$V(\text{Effect}) = \frac{4 \sigma^2}{N} = \frac{4 \cdot MS_E}{N}$$ **Standard Error of an Effect:** $$SE(\text{Effect}) = \sqrt{\frac{4 MS_E}{N}}$$ --- ### Derivation 5 — Taguchi S/N Ratio (Larger-the-Better Case) Suppose we have $n$ observations $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$ from a treatment combination, and we want this combination to *maximize* the response. **Step 1:** The Total Sum of Squares (assuming target = 0): $$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2$$ **Step 2:** Mean Sum of Squares (per observation): $$MS_0 = \frac{S}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2$$ **Step 3:** Convert to decibels (logarithmic scale, base 10): $$\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$$ > This is because for "larger-the-better," the loss function is $L = k / y^2$, hence the signal-to-noise ratio becomes: $$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{Mean Square of Signal}}{\text{Mean Square of Noise}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}(L)$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A 2² factorial design is used to study the effect of Temperature (A) and Pressure (B) on Yield. Data: | Run | A | B | Yield | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | − | − | 30 | | 2 | + | − | 40 | | 3 | − | + | 50 | | 4 | + | + | 80 | Compute the main effects A, B and interaction AB. **Step 1 — Compute Contrast Coefficients:** The contrast vectors are: - A: (−1, +1, −1, +1) - B: (−1, −1, +1, +1) - AB: (+1, −1, −1, +1) **Step 2 — Compute Contrast Sums:** $$C_A = (-1)(30) + (+1)(40) + (-1)(50) + (+1)(80) = -30 + 40 - 50 + 80 = 40$$ $$C_B = (-1)(30) + (-1)(40) + (+1)(50) + (+1)(80) = -30 - 40 + 50 + 80 = 60$$ $$C_{AB} = (+1)(30) + (-1)(40) + (-1)(50) + (+1)(80) = 30 - 40 - 50 + 80 = 20$$ **Step 3 — Compute Effects (with N = 4):** $$A = \frac{2 C_A}{N} = \frac{2 \times 40}{4} = 20$$ $$B = \frac{2 C_B}{N} = \frac{2 \times 60}{4} = 30$$ $$AB = \frac{2 C_{AB}}{N} = \frac{2 \times 20}{4} = 10$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** Increasing Temperature from low to high raises Yield by **20 units** on average. Increasing Pressure raises Yield by **30 units**. The AB interaction effect of **10** indicates a *positive synergistic interaction* — when both A and B are increased together, the yield gains are amplified beyond what is predicted by individual effects alone. --- ## 3.3 Application-to-Design Mapping Matrix (Analytical Summary) | Application Type | Recommended Design | Number of Factors | Key Assumption | |---|---|---|---| | Comparing 3 catalysts | CRD | 1 (treatment) | Units homogeneous | | Crop yield with field variability | RBD | 1 (treatment) + 1 (block) | Blocks homogeneous within | | 4 machines × 4 operators × 4 days | LSD | 1 (treatment) + 2 (nuisance) | No interaction | | 3 factors, all interactions | 2³ Factorial | 3 | Factors independent | | 6 factors, screen main effects | 2^(6-2) Fractional | 6 | Sparsity-of-effects | | Optimize 2 factors | CCD or BBD | 2 | Curvature exists | | Robust to noise | Taguchi L9 / L18 | 3–7 + noise | Noise uncontrollable | | Blend optimization | Simplex-Lattice | q (mixture) | Proportions sum to 1 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DOE Methodology Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Step 1 Identify Problem
and Objective] --> node2[Step 2 Identify
Response Variable Y] node2 --> node3[Step 3 List Potential
Factors X1 X2 ... Xk] node3 --> node4[Step 4 Choose
Experimental Design] node4 --> node5[Step 5 Conduct
Runs with Replication] node5 --> node6[Step 6 Statistical
Analysis ANOVA] node6 --> node7[Step 7 Validate
Conclusions] node7 --> node8[Step 8 Implement
Optimal Settings] style node1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style node2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333 style node3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style node4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style node5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style node6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 style node7 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style node8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Experimental Designs ```mermaid flowchart TD root[Experimental Designs] --> basic[Basic Designs] root --> advanced[Advanced Designs] basic --> CRD[Completely Randomized
Design CRD] basic --> RBD[Randomized Block
Design RBD] basic --> LSD[Latin Square
Design LSD] advanced --> FF[Factorial Designs
2k Full] advanced --> FracF[Fractional Factorial
2k-p] advanced --> RSM[Response Surface
CCD / BBD] advanced --> Taguchi[Taguchi Robust
Design] advanced --> Mix[Mixture
Experiments] advanced --> EVOP[Evolutionary
Operation EVOP] CRD --> CRDapp[Application Homogeneous
units small N] RBD --> RBDapp[Application One
nuisance source] LSD --> LSDapp[Application Two
nuisance sources] FF --> FFapp[Application Screening
all interactions] FracF --> FracFapp[Application Large k
resource constrained] RSM --> RSMapp[Application
Optimization] Taguchi --> Tagapp[Application Robust
to noise] Mix --> Mixapp[Application
Blends formulations] EVOP --> EVOPapp[Application
Online continuous] style root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style basic fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style advanced fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Application-to-Design Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Domain [Engineering and Science Domains] D1[Chemical Reactor
Yield] D2[Pharma Tablet
Formulation] D3[Agricultural
Crop Trials] D4[Mechanical CNC
Machining] D5[Civil Concrete
Mix Design] D6[Software A or B
Testing] end subgraph Design [Recommended DOE Strategy] S1[2k Factorial
plus RSM] S2[Mixture Design
Simplex] S3[RBD or Split
Plot] S4[Taguchi L9
plus S/N Ratio] S5[Simplex Lattice
Mixture] S6[Two Sample
Test or CRD] end subgraph Outcome [Achieved Outcome] O1[Optimum Yield] O2[Optimum Blend] O3[Best Variety] O4[Low Ra Surface] O5[Target Strength] O6[Max Conversion] end D1 --> S1 --> O1 D2 --> S2 --> O2 D3 --> S3 --> O3 D4 --> S4 --> O4 D5 --> S5 --> O5 D6 --> S6 --> O6 style Domain fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333 style Design fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style Outcome fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Taguchi Inner-Outer Array Concept ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InnerArray [Inner Array Control Factors] IA1[A Level 1] IA2[A Level 2] IA3[A Level 3] end subgraph OuterArray [Outer Array Noise Factors] OA1[N1 Low] OA2[N2 High] end InnerArray --> Run[Experimental Run] OuterArray --> Run Run --> SN[Compute SN Ratio
per control setting] SN --> ANOVA[ANOVA on
Mean SN] ANOVA --> Opt[Pick Best Control
Level Combination] style InnerArray fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333 style OuterArray fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style Opt fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Q: List any three typical applications of Experimental Design in engineering with one example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three typical applications of Design of Experiments (DOE) in engineering are: **1. Process Optimization (Industrial Manufacturing):** DOE is used to identify the optimal operating conditions of a manufacturing process. *Example:* In an injection molding process, Temperature, Pressure, and Cooling Time (factors) are studied to maximize the Tensile Strength (response) using a 2³ factorial design followed by Response Surface Methodology. **2. Comparative Studies (Agricultural and Pharmaceutical):** DOE is used to compare the effectiveness of multiple treatments. *Example:* Four varieties of paddy are compared across five fields using a Randomized Block Design (RBD), where each field acts as a block to control soil variability. **3. Robust Product Design (Taguchi Methods):** DOE is used to design products that are insensitive to uncontrollable noise factors. *Example:* An automotive engineer uses a Taguchi L9 orthogonal array to design a brake pad whose wear rate (response) is robust against ambient humidity and road conditions (noise factors). The Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio is maximized. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper] **Q: Differentiate between Comparative Experiments and Screening Experiments in DOE.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Comparative Experiment | Screening Experiment | |---|---|---| | **Objective** | Compare means of 2 or more existing treatments | Identify which of many factors significantly affect the response | | **Number of Factors** | Single factor (treatment) | Multiple factors (typically 3–10) | | **Typical Designs** | CRD, RBD, LSD | 2^k or 2^(k-p) Factorial | | **Example** | Comparing 4 fertilizers on wheat yield | Screening 7 process parameters for yield in semiconductor fab | | **Outcome** | Best treatment identified | Vital few factors identified for further study | | **Statistical Test** | One-way / Two-way ANOVA | ANOVA + Normal probability plot of effects | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **A chemical engineer wants to study the effect of Reaction Temperature (A: 60°C / 80°C) and Catalyst Concentration (B: 2% / 4%) on the % Yield of a product. A 2² factorial design with 2 replicates is conducted. The yields obtained are:** | Run | A | B | Replicate 1 | Replicate 2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | − | − | 30 | 32 | | 2 | + | − | 45 | 47 | | 3 | − | + | 55 | 53 | | 4 | + | + | 75 | 77 | **(a)** Compute the main effects A, B and the interaction effect AB. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Set up and complete the ANOVA table. Comment on the significance at α = 0.05. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Part (a) — Effect Computation (7 marks) **Step 1: Compute Treatment Totals (Sum of both replicates):** | Run | A | B | Rep1 | Rep2 | Total | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | − | − | 30 | 32 | 62 | | 2 | + | − | 45 | 47 | 92 | | 3 | − | + | 55 | 53 | 108 | | 4 | + | + | 75 | 77 | 152 | **Step 2: Apply Contrast Method.** With N = 8 (total observations): $$C_A = -(62) + (92) - (108) + (152) = -62 + 92 - 108 + 152 = 74$$ $$C_B = -(62) - (92) + (108) + (152) = -62 - 92 + 108 + 152 = 106$$ $$C_{AB} = +(62) - (92) - (108) + (152) = 62 - 92 - 108 + 152 = 14$$ **Step 3: Compute Effects** using Effect = 2 × Contrast / N: $$A = \frac{2 \times 74}{8} = 18.5$$ $$B = \frac{2 \times 106}{8} = 26.5$$ $$AB = \frac{2 \times 14}{8} = 3.5$$ **Interpretation:** Temperature increases yield by 18.5%, Catalyst by 26.5%, and the AB interaction contributes an additional 3.5% (minor positive interaction). --- #### Solution to Part (b) — ANOVA Table (7 marks) **Step 1: Grand Total and Correction Factor (CF):** $$\text{Grand Total} = 62 + 92 + 108 + 152 = 414$$ $$CF = \frac{(414)^2}{8} = \frac{171396}{8} = 21424.5$$ **Step 2: Total SS:** $$SS_T = (30^2 + 32^2 + 45^2 + 47^2 + 55^2 + 53^2 + 75^2 + 77^2) - CF$$ Compute each square: $$30^2 + 32^2 + 45^2 + 47^2 + 55^2 + 53^2 + 75^2 + 77^2$$ $$= 900 + 1024 + 2025 + 2209 + 3025 + 2809 + 5625 + 5929 = 23546$$ $$SS_T = 23546 - 21424.5 = 2121.5$$ **Step 3: SS for Effects:** $$SS_A = \frac{C_A^2}{N} = \frac{74^2}{8} = \frac{5476}{8} = 684.5$$ $$SS_B = \frac{C_B^2}{N} = \frac{106^2}{8} = \frac{11236}{8} = 1404.5$$ $$SS_{AB} = \frac{C_{AB}^2}{N} = \frac{14^2}{8} = \frac{196}{8} = 24.5$$ **Step 4: Error SS (by subtraction):** $$SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_B - SS_{AB} = 2121.5 - 684.5 - 1404.5 - 24.5 = 8.0$$ **Step 5: Degrees of Freedom:** - $df_A = 1$ - $df_B = 1$ - $df_{AB} = 1$ - $df_E = 8 - 4 = 4$ - $df_T = 7$ **Step 6: ANOVA Table:** | Source | SS | df | MS | F₀ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A (Temperature) | 684.5 | 1 | 684.5 | 342.25 | | B (Catalyst) | 1404.5 | 1 | 1404.5 | 702.25 | | AB Interaction | 24.5 | 1 | 24.5 | 12.25 | | Error | 8.0 | 4 | 2.0 | — | | **Total** | **2121.5** | **7** | — | — | **Step 7: Decision at α = 0.05:** Critical values: $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$ - $F_A = 342.25 > 7.71$ → **A is highly significant** ✓ - $F_B = 702.25 > 7.71$ → **B is highly significant** ✓ - $F_{AB} = 12.25 > 7.71$ → **AB interaction is significant** at α = 0.05 ✓ **Conclusion:** Both Temperature and Catalyst Concentration have significant effects on Yield. The interaction AB is also significant (though smaller in magnitude). The recommended operating condition is **A = + (80°C)** and **B = + (4%)**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **A researcher uses a Completely Randomized Design (CRD) to compare 4 fertilizers (A, B, C, D) on wheat yield (kg/plot). Five replicate plots are used for each fertilizer.** **(a)** Set up the linear statistical model for CRD and explain the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the following partial ANOVA information: $SS_{\text{Total}} = 720$, $SS_{\text{Treatment}} = 480$, $SS_{\text{Error}} = 240$, with $t = 4$ treatments and $r = 5$ replicates. Complete the ANOVA table, state the null hypothesis, and test it at α = 0.05 using $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Part (a) — CRD Model (7 marks) **Linear Statistical Model for CRD:** $$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$$ Where: - $y_{ij}$ = yield observation from the $j$-th replicate of the $i$-th treatment - $\mu$ = overall (grand) mean yield - $\tau_i$ = effect of the $i$-th treatment (with $\sum \tau_i = 0$) - $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = random experimental error **Index ranges:** - $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$ (treatments) - $j = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ (replicates) **Assumptions of CRD:** 1. **Additivity:** The treatment effect and error are additive (no interaction between treatments and replicates). 2. **Independence:** Errors $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are statistically independent. 3. **Normality:** $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ — errors are normally distributed with mean zero. 4. **Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity):** All treatments share a common variance $\sigma^2$. 5. **Randomization:** Treatments are randomly assigned to experimental units. **Hypotheses:** $$H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \tau_3 = \tau_4 = 0 \quad \text{(All treatments have equal mean yield)}$$ $$H_1: \tau_i \neq 0 \quad \text{for at least one } i$$ --- #### Solution to Part (b) — ANOVA Computation (7 marks) **Step 1: Compute Degrees of Freedom:** - $N = tr = 4 \times 5 = 20$ (total observations) - $df_{\text{Total}} = 20 - 1 = 19$ - $df_{\text{Treatment}} = t - 1 = 3$ - $df_{\text{Error}} = t(r - 1) = 4 \times 4 = 16$ **Step 2: Compute Mean Squares:** $$MS_{\text{Treatment}} = \frac{480}{3} = 160$$ $$MS_{\text{Error}} = \frac{240}{16} = 15$$ **Step 3: Compute F-statistic:** $$F_0 = \frac{MS_{\text{Treatment}}}{MS_{\text{Error}}} = \frac{160}{15} = 10.67$$ **Step 4: Completed ANOVA Table:** | Source | SS | df | MS | F₀ | F-critical | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Treatment | 480 | 3 | 160 | 10.67 | 3.24 | | Error | 240 | 16 | 15 | — | — | | **Total** | **720** | **19** | — | — | — | **Step 5: Decision:** Since $F_0 = 10.67 > F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$, we **reject $H_0$**. **Conclusion:** There is statistically significant evidence at the 5% level to conclude that the mean wheat yields differ among the four fertilizers. To identify which specific fertilizers differ, a post-hoc test such as **Tukey's HSD** or **LSD (Least Significant Difference)** would be required. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Grand Mean Interpretation** in CRD — always state the model $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ explicitly. (Lose 1 mark if missing) > 2. **Confusing "interaction AB" with "two-way ANOVA interaction"** in factorial design — AB here is a *cross-product term*, not a layout. (Lose 2 marks) > 3. **Not mentioning replication & randomization** in the assumptions section — KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for randomization. > 4. **Wrong df partition** — students frequently write $df_E = N - t$ instead of $df_E = t(r-1)$. (Lose 1 mark) > 5. **Not concluding the F-test** — always end with a clear *"Reject/Accept H₀"* statement with justification. > 6. **Skipping effect-to-response interpretation** — after computing effects, always give a one-line physical interpretation. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Typical Applications of Experimental Design"** ### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize - [ ] **DOE definition** (Fisher, 1925; Rothamsted Station) — structured, statistical, efficient. - [ ] **Three pillars:** Randomization, Replication, Blocking. - [ ] **Six application categories:** Comparative, Screening, Optimization, Robust, Mixture, EVOP. - [ ] **Design matrix mapping** (CRD → RBD → LSD for comparative; 2^k → RSM for screening/optimization). ### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize Symbolically) - [ ] $N = 2^k$ — full factorial runs - [ ] $N = 2^{k-p}$ — fractional factorial runs - [ ] $N = 2^k + 2k + n_c$ — CCD runs (where $n_c$ = center points) - [ ] Effect = $2C/N$ for 2-level factorial - [ ] $\eta = -10 \log_{10}(\text{MSD})$ — Taguchi S/N ratio - [ ] $\sum x_i = 1$ — mixture constraint - [ ] $F = MS_{\text{Treatment}}/MS_{\text{Error}}$ ### ✅ Decision Tree for Selecting a Design 1. **How many factors?** 1 → CRD/RBD/LSD; Many → 2^k Factorial 2. **What is the objective?** Compare → CRD/RBD; Screen → 2^k; Optimize → RSM (CCD/BBD) 3. **Any nuisance sources?** Yes → Block it (RBD/LSD) 4. **Are factors proportions?** Yes → Mixture Design 5. **Is noise uncontrollable?** Yes → Taguchi Robust Design ### ✅ Real-World Example Bank (For Viva) - Chemical: 2³ factorial for reactor yield - Mechanical: Taguchi L9 for surface roughness - Civil: Simplex-lattice for concrete mix - CS: A/B test for website conversion - Agricultural: RBD for crop variety comparison ### ✅ Common KTU Exam Traps - "What if there is no replication?" → Use Normal Probability Plot of Effects (Lenth's method). - "Why prefer Factorial over OFAT?" → Captures interactions; more efficient (no. of runs ∝ factors, not factors²). - "Difference between CRD and RBD?" → CRD has no blocking; RBD has one blocking variable. --- > **End of Module 1 Notes — Typical Applications of Experimental Design** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PECHT865 Syllabus — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Compliant*

topics.title: Basic Principles of Experimental Design: Replication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Experimental Design: Replication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, **Replication** is defined as the **independent repetition of the basic experimental run** — that is, the application of each treatment to more than one **experimental unit** under identical conditions, such that each repetition constitutes an independent observation of that treatment's effect.

Formally, if a treatment $T_i$ is applied to $n_i$ experimental units, the number of replications for that treatment is $r_i = n_i$. In a **balanced design**, every treatment has the same number of replications, denoted as $r$ (or sometimes $n$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> *"Replication is the repetition of the basic experiment. It is required to provide an estimate of the experimental error and to obtain more precise estimates of the treatment means."*

Replication provides two essential statistical advantages:
1. **Estimation of Experimental Error** $\sigma^2$ from the sample variance.
2. **Increased Precision** of treatment mean estimates by reducing the standard error.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Weighing Scale Analogy (Plain English):**
>
> Imagine you want to find the true weight of a bag of rice using a faulty weighing scale. If you weigh it **once** and the scale reads 5.1 kg, you cannot tell if it is really 5 kg or if the scale drifted. However, if you weigh it **5 times** independently and get readings 5.1, 5.0, 5.2, 4.9, 5.05, you can now:
> - **Average** the readings to get a more accurate estimate ($\bar{x} = 5.05$ kg).
> - **Measure the spread** of the readings to estimate the **uncertainty / experimental error** of the scale.
>
> The repeated weighings are the **replications**. The variability among them captures the random error, and the average is more precise than any single reading. This is exactly what replication does in any engineering experiment — it lets us *separate the true effect from the noise*.

### 1.3 Replication vs. Repetition (Common Confusion)

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Replication** | The **entire experiment** is repeated from scratch on a new experimental unit. Each replicate is an **independent run**. | Baking 3 separate cakes using 3 different batches of dough, each baked at 180 °C. |
| **Repetition (Repeated Measures)** | The **same experimental unit** is measured multiple times **without resetting** the treatment. | Measuring the temperature of the same cake 3 times using the same thermometer. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Repetition of measurements on the same unit gives information about the **measurement error** only — it does **NOT** provide an estimate of the **experimental error** (which includes unit-to-unit variability). Only **true replication** captures both. Students often lose marks by confusing these terms in definitions.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The **standard error of a treatment mean** is $SE(\bar{y}_i) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$, where **$r$** is the number of replications and **$\sigma$** is the standard deviation of an individual observation.
- The **confidence interval half-width** for a mean is $H = t_{\alpha/2, \, df} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$.
- The **relative efficiency** of two designs with replications $r_1$ and $r_2$ is $E = \dfrac{r_1}{r_2}$ for precision of the mean.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Purposes of Replication

Replication is **not** done arbitrarily. It serves three well-defined statistical purposes:

**Purpose 1 — Estimation of Experimental Error (Variance)**
- With only one observation per treatment, the variation due to **chance / uncontrolled factors** is confounded with the treatment effect.
- Replication provides $n - t$ degrees of freedom (where $n$ = total observations, $t$ = number of treatments) to compute a **pure error estimate** $S^2 = \dfrac{SS_E}{df_E}$.
- This $S^2$ is the denominator of the **F-test** in ANOVA.

**Purpose 2 — Increased Precision of Mean Estimates**
- The variance of a sample mean computed from $r$ replications is:

$$Var(\bar{y}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}$$

- Doubling $r$ from $r = 2$ to $r = 4$ **halves** the variance of the mean — a powerful precision gain.
- The standard error of the mean decreases by a factor of $\sqrt{r}$.

**Purpose 3 — Wider Inferential Scope**
- Replication allows construction of **confidence intervals** and **hypothesis tests** for treatment means and differences.
- A design with $r = 1$ cannot statistically test whether treatments differ (zero error degrees of freedom).

### 2.2 Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Random Error is Inherent.** Every measurement $y_{ij}$ contains a random component $\varepsilon_{ij}$ (i.e., $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$).
2. **One Observation is Unreliable.** A single $y_{ij}$ cannot be distinguished from noise.
3. **Multiple Replications Sample the Error.** Repeated measurements under identical treatment reveal the **distribution** of $\varepsilon_{ij}$.
4. **Averaging Cancels Random Fluctuations.** Random errors tend to cancel out when averaged over $r$ replications, leaving the true treatment effect visible.
5. **Sample Variance Estimates Population Variance.** $S^2 = \dfrac{\sum (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i)^2}{r-1}$ provides an unbiased estimate of $\sigma^2$.
6. **Degrees of Freedom for Error.** With $t$ treatments each replicated $r$ times, $df_E = t(r-1)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-valuable. Memorize the table.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Model | $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ | $i = 1, \ldots, t$ treatments; $j = 1, \ldots, r$ replications |
| 2 | Standard Error of Mean | $SE(\bar{y}_i) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$ | $r$ = number of replications; $\sigma$ = true std. deviation |
| 3 | Variance of Mean | $Var(\bar{y}_i) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{r}$ | Inversely proportional to $r$ |
| 4 | Error Degrees of Freedom | $df_E = t(r-1)$ | $t$ = number of treatments; balanced design |
| 5 | Sample Variance | $S^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij}-\bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2}{t(r-1)}$ | Pooled estimate of $\sigma^2$ |
| 6 | CI Half-Width | $H = t_{\alpha/2,\,t(r-1)} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{r}}$ | For two-sided CI on a treatment mean |
| 7 | SE of Difference of Means | $SE(\bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_k) = S\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{r}}$ | For comparing two treatment means |
| 8 | Required $r$ for Precision $H^*$ | $r \geq \left( \dfrac{t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot \sigma}{H^*} \right)^2$ | Round up to integer; $df$ depends on $r$ |
| 9 | Relative Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{r_2}{r_1}$ | Design 2 is $E$ times more efficient than Design 1 |
| 10 | Total Observations | $N = t \cdot r$ | For a completely randomized design (CRD) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Trials:** A new drug's efficacy is tested on $r$ patients per dose level to estimate individual response variability and demonstrate statistical significance against placebo.
- **Materials Testing:** Tensile strength of a new alloy is measured on $r$ specimens to estimate the within-batch variability and establish a reliable mean strength.
- **Chemical Reactor Yield:** A catalyst is tested at $r$ independent runs to separate true catalytic effect from random fluctuations in feed concentration, temperature drift, and catalyst aging.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** A wafer is processed under $r$ runs of identical recipe to compute wafer-to-wafer variation — the experimental error used to detect process drift.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Variance of a Treatment Mean

**Starting Point — Single Observation Model:**
Assume each observation follows the model:
$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$$
where $\varepsilon_{ij} \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 1 — Take the Mean over $r$ Replications:**
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r} \sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Model:**
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r} \sum_{j=1}^{r} (\mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij})$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the Summation:**
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r} \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{r}\mu + \sum_{j=1}^{r}\tau_i + \sum_{j=1}^{r}\varepsilon_{ij} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify (since $\mu$ and $\tau_i$ are constants over $j$):**
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \mu + \tau_i + \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Variance Operator:**
$$Var(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = Var\left(\frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\varepsilon_{ij}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Factor Out the Constant $1/r$:**
$$Var(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\,Var\left(\sum_{j=1}^{r}\varepsilon_{ij}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Apply Independence of the $\varepsilon_{ij}$:**
$$Var(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\sum_{j=1}^{r}Var(\varepsilon_{ij}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\sigma^2$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{Var(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The variance of the mean is reduced by a factor of $r$ compared to the variance of a single observation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Replication and Standard Error

**Given:** $SE(\bar{y}_i) = \sqrt{Var(\bar{y}_i)} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$

**Question:** How much does the SE decrease if we increase replications from $r_1$ to $r_2$?

**Step 1 — Write the Ratio:**
$$\frac{SE_2}{SE_1} = \frac{\sigma / \sqrt{r_2}}{\sigma / \sqrt{r_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{r_1}{r_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Case: $r_2 = 4r_1$ (Quadrupling Replications):**
$$\frac{SE_2}{SE_1} = \sqrt{\frac{r_1}{4r_1}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

> **Result:** Quadrupling the replications **halves** the standard error. The precision gain is a square-root function, not linear.

**Step 3 — Case: $r_2 = 2r_1$ (Doubling Replications):**
$$\frac{SE_2}{SE_1} = \sqrt{\frac{r_1}{2r_1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$$

> **Result:** Doubling replications reduces SE by only ~29.3 %.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Effect of Replication

**Problem:** A chemical engineer measures yield $y$ of a process. Each yield reading has true $\sigma = 4$ g. The process is repeated with $r = 1, 4, 16$ replications. Compute the SE of the mean in each case.

**Step 1 — Case $r = 1$:**
$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{1}} = 4.000 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Case $r = 4$:**
$$SE = \frac{4}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.000 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Case $r = 16$:**
$$SE = \frac{4}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.000 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Tabulated Summary:**

| Replications $r$ | Standard Error $SE$ | SE reduction vs. $r=1$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.000 g | 0 % |
| 4 | 2.000 g | 50 % |
| 16 | 1.000 g | 75 % |
| 64 | 0.500 g | 87.5 % |

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Diminishing returns set in rapidly. Going from $r=1$ to $r=4$ cuts SE in half, but going from $r=16$ to $r=64$ only halves it again. Replication cost (time, materials) must be balanced against precision benefit.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Required Sample Size for a Target Confidence Half-Width

**Goal:** Find the minimum $r$ such that the two-sided $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ has half-width $\leq H^*$.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Inequality:**
$$H = t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}} \leq H^*$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\sqrt{r}$:**
$$\sqrt{r} \geq \frac{t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot \sigma}{H^*}$$

**Step 3 — Square Both Sides:**
$$r \geq \left( \frac{t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot \sigma}{H^*} \right)^2$$

> **Note:** The $t$-value itself depends on $df = r - 1$. Solve **iteratively**: start with $z_{\alpha/2}$, compute $r$, update $df = r-1$, re-solve.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Suppose $\sigma = 4$, $\alpha = 0.05$, target $H^* = 1.5$ g.

- **Iteration 1 (use $z_{0.025} = 1.96$):**
$$r_1 = \left(\frac{1.96 \cdot 4}{1.5}\right)^2 = (5.227)^2 = 27.32 \Rightarrow r_1 = 28$$

- **Iteration 2 (use $t_{0.025,\,27} = 2.052$):**
$$r_2 = \left(\frac{2.052 \cdot 4}{1.5}\right)^2 = (5.472)^2 = 29.94 \Rightarrow r_2 = 30$$

- **Iteration 3 (use $t_{0.025,\,29} = 2.045$):**
$$r_3 = \left(\frac{2.045 \cdot 4}{1.5}\right)^2 = (5.453)^2 = 29.74 \Rightarrow r_3 = 30$$

> **Converged:** Minimum $r = 30$ replications needed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Why Replication Matters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single Observation y_ij]:::start --> B{Is it Reliable?}
    B -- No, contains random error --> C[Apply Treatment r Times]
    C --> D[Collect r Observations per Treatment]
    D --> E[Compute Sample Mean y_bar_i]
    D --> F[Compute Sample Variance S squared]
    E --> G[Improved Estimate of Treatment Effect]
    F --> H[Estimate of Experimental Error sigma squared]
    G --> I[ANOVA F-Test & Confidence Intervals]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Valid Statistical Inferences]:::goal

    classDef start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Replication Architecture in a Completely Randomized Design (CRD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRT1["Treatment 1"]
        Y11[Obs y11]:::obs
        Y12[Obs y12]:::obs
        Y13[Obs y13]:::obs
        Y1R[Obs y1r]:::obs
    end
    subgraph TRT2["Treatment 2"]
        Y21[Obs y21]:::obs
        Y22[Obs y22]:::obs
        Y23[Obs y23]:::obs
        Y2R[Obs y2r]:::obs
    end
    subgraph TRT3["Treatment t"]
        YT1[Obs yt1]:::obs
        YT2[Obs yt2]:::obs
        YT3[Obs yt3]:::obs
        YTR[Obs ytr]:::obs
    end

    M1[Mean y_bar_1 dot]:::mean
    M2[Mean y_bar_2 dot]:::mean
    M3[Mean y_bar_t dot]:::mean
    SS[Total SS Error]:::ss

    Y11 --> M1
    Y12 --> M1
    Y13 --> M1
    Y1R --> M1

    Y21 --> M2
    Y22 --> M2
    Y23 --> M2
    Y2R --> M2

    YT1 --> M3
    YT2 --> M3
    YT3 --> M3
    YTR --> M3

    Y11 --> SS
    Y12 --> SS
    Y21 --> SS
    YT1 --> SS

    classDef obs fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef mean fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ss fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Standard Error vs. Replication

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input: sigma fixed, r variable]:::input --> FORMULA[Apply SE equals sigma over sqrt r]:::proc
    FORMULA --> OUT1[r equals 1, SE equals sigma]:::out
    FORMULA --> OUT2[r equals 4, SE equals sigma over 2]:::out
    FORMULA --> OUT3[r equals 9, SE equals sigma over 3]:::out
    FORMULA --> OUT4[r equals 16, SE equals sigma over 4]:::out
    OUT1 --> TREND[Diminishing Precision Return]:::trend
    OUT2 --> TREND
    OUT3 --> TREND
    OUT4 --> TREND
    TREND --> DECISION[Choose r to balance Cost vs Precision]:::decision

    classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333399,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef trend fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Replication" in the context of Design of Experiments. State any two reasons why replication is necessary.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Replication refers to the **independent repetition of a basic experimental run**, i.e., applying the same treatment to more than one experimental unit under identical conditions, yielding multiple observations $y_{i1}, y_{i2}, \ldots, y_{ir}$ for treatment $i$.

> **Reasons (2 Marks — Any Two):**
> 1. **Estimation of Experimental Error:** Replication provides a within-treatment sum of squares $SS_E$ with $df_E = t(r-1)$ degrees of freedom, yielding an unbiased estimate $S^2$ of the error variance $\sigma^2$. Without replication, $S^2$ cannot be computed.
> 2. **Increased Precision of Treatment Means:** The standard error of a mean is $SE(\bar{y}_i) = \sigma / \sqrt{r}$. Increasing $r$ shrinks the SE, producing more precise (less variable) treatment mean estimates.
> 3. **Statistical Inference Validity:** Replication is required to construct confidence intervals and conduct F-tests in ANOVA. With $r = 1$, the error variance is undefined and no valid inference is possible.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between "Replication" and "Repetition" in experimental design. Why is repetition not a substitute for replication?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Replication | Repetition |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** (1 Mark) | Independent repetition of the entire experimental run on a new experimental unit. | Multiple measurements on the **same** experimental unit without resetting the treatment. |
| **What it captures** (1 Mark) | Captures **unit-to-unit variability** (true experimental error). | Captures **measurement error** only. |
| **Substitutability** (1 Mark) | — | **Repetition cannot replace replication** because it ignores the variability between experimental units, leading to a biased (underestimated) error variance. Statistical tests based on repetition-only data are anti-conservative and overstate significance. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module in Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two complete, independent alternative questions — the student answers **either** A **or** B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)*
*Explain in detail the three main purposes of replication in a designed experiment. How does replication reduce the standard error of a treatment mean? Derive the relationship between the number of replications and the standard error.*

**(b)** *(7 Marks)*
*A textile engineer measures the breaking strength of a fabric. The standard deviation of a single measurement is known to be $\sigma = 5$ N. How many replications are required so that the $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean breaking strength has a half-width of at most $1.5$ N? Use $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ for the first iteration and refine using the $t$-distribution.*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Three purposes of replication (3 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Estimation of Experimental Error $\sigma^2$:** When $r \geq 2$ replications per treatment exist, the within-treatment sum of squares $SS_E = \sum_i \sum_j (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2$ is computed. This sum, divided by $df_E = t(r-1)$, gives $S^2$, an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$. This estimate is the **denominator** in the F-statistic of ANOVA.

2. **Increasing Precision of Mean Estimates:** Because the variance of a mean equals $\sigma^2 / r$, replication shrinks variability of treatment means. The confidence interval half-width $H = t_{\alpha/2,df} \cdot \sigma / \sqrt{r}$ decreases with larger $r$.

3. **Enabling Statistical Inference:** Replication is a prerequisite for valid hypothesis tests and confidence intervals. With $r = 1$, the F-test cannot be performed (zero error df).

**Derivation of SE vs. $r$ (4 Marks):**

Starting from the linear model $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ with $\varepsilon_{ij} \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)$:

$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\varepsilon_{ij}$$

Applying the variance operator and using independence:

$$Var(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\sum_{j=1}^{r}Var(\varepsilon_{ij}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}$$

$$SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$$

> **Key conclusion:** $SE$ decreases as $1/\sqrt{r}$. To halve the SE, $r$ must be **quadrupled**. (2 Marks for the derivation, 2 Marks for the conclusion and the $1/\sqrt{r}$ relationship.)

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $\sigma = 5$ N, target half-width $H^* = 1.5$ N, two-sided 95% CI.

**Step 1 — Write the precision formula (1 Mark):**
$$H = t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}} \leq H^*$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 1 using $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ (2 Marks):**
$$\sqrt{r_1} \geq \frac{z_{0.025} \cdot \sigma}{H^*} = \frac{1.96 \times 5}{1.5} = 6.5333$$
$$r_1 \geq 6.5333^2 = 42.685$$
$$\Rightarrow r_1 = 43$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 2 using $t_{0.025,\,42} = 2.0181$ (2 Marks):**
$$\sqrt{r_2} \geq \frac{2.0181 \times 5}{1.5} = 6.7270$$
$$r_2 \geq 6.7270^2 = 45.25$$
$$\Rightarrow r_2 = 46$$

**Step 4 — Iteration 3 using $t_{0.025,\,45} = 2.0141$ (1 Mark):**
$$\sqrt{r_3} \geq \frac{2.0141 \times 5}{1.5} = 6.7137$$
$$r_3 \geq 45.07 \Rightarrow r_3 = 46$$

**Step 5 — Conclude (1 Mark):**
> **Answer:** A minimum of $r = 46$ replications is required to ensure the 95% CI half-width is at most 1.5 N.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)*
*Define "Experimental Error" in the context of DOE. Explain how replication provides a measure of experimental error. Why is it impossible to estimate the experimental error when $r = 1$?*

**(b)** *(7 Marks)*
