*An agronomist is testing 4 fertilizers on crop yield (tons/hectare). Each fertilizer is applied to 5 randomly chosen plots. The treatment means are: $\bar{y}_1 = 22$, $\bar{y}_2 = 25$, $\bar{y}_3 = 20$, $\bar{y}_4 = 24$. The pooled within-treatment sum of squares is $SS_E = 180$.*
*(i) Estimate the experimental error variance $\sigma^2$.*
*(ii) Compute the standard error of the mean for any treatment.*
*(iii) Construct a 95% confidence interval for the mean yield of Fertilizer 2. Use $t_{0.025,\,16} = 2.120$.*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Experimental error $\sigma^2$ (also called **error variance** or **within-treatment variance**) is the variance among observations that receive the **same treatment**. It quantifies the random variability in the response that is **not attributable** to the treatments being studied — arising from uncontrolled factors, measurement noise, unit heterogeneity, and environmental fluctuations.

**How replication provides this estimate (3 Marks):**
With $r$ replications per treatment, the **within-treatment sum of squares** is:

$$SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{r}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2$$

This sum quantifies the total random variation around the treatment means. Dividing by degrees of freedom $df_E = t(r-1)$ gives the **mean square error**:

$$MSE = S^2 = \frac{SS_E}{t(r-1)}$$

$S^2$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\sigma^2$. Replication is what makes $SS_E > 0$ and $df_E > 0$, hence allows $\sigma^2$ to be estimated.

**Why $r = 1$ makes estimation impossible (2 Marks):**
When $r = 1$, only one observation $y_{i1}$ exists per treatment. There is no within-treatment spread to measure, so:
- $SS_E = 0$ (zero by definition since $y_{i1} = \bar{y}_{i\cdot}$),
- $df_E = t(1-1) = 0$,
- $S^2 = 0/0$ is **undefined**.

Consequently, no F-test can be performed, and treatment differences cannot be statistically tested for significance. This is a **fundamental limitation** of unreplicated designs.

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $t = 4$ treatments, $r = 5$ replications per treatment, $SS_E = 180$.

**(i) Estimate of $\sigma^2$ (2 Marks):**
$$df_E = t(r-1) = 4(5-1) = 16$$
$$S^2 = MSE = \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{180}{16} = 11.25$$
$$S = \sqrt{11.25} = 3.354 \text{ tons/hectare}$$

**(ii) Standard Error of the Mean (2 Marks):**
$$SE(\bar{y}_i) = \frac{S}{\sqrt{r}} = \frac{3.354}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{3.354}{2.2361} = 1.500 \text{ tons/hectare}$$

**(iii) 95% Confidence Interval for $\mu_2$ (3 Marks):**
The CI for mean yield of Fertilizer 2 ($\bar{y}_2 = 25$) is:
$$\bar{y}_2 \pm t_{0.025,\,16} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{r}}$$
$$25 \pm 2.120 \times 1.500$$
$$25 \pm 3.180$$
$$\boxed{(21.82, \ 28.18) \text{ tons/hectare}}$$

> **Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true mean yield under Fertilizer 2 lies between **21.82** and **28.18** tons/hectare.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Replication Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $df_E$ separately.** Many students write $S^2 = SS_E / N$ (total observations) instead of $SS_E / df_E = SS_E / t(r-1)$. This **wrongly inflates** the precision by using the wrong denominator. (–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Confusing Replication with Repetition.** Definition questions that ask for replication must be answered in terms of **independent runs on different experimental units**, not repeated measurements on the same unit. Writing "measuring the same sample 5 times" gets **0 marks** for definition.
>
> 3. **Skipping iterations in sample-size derivation.** Using $z_{\alpha/2}$ once and stopping will lose 1–2 marks. The KTU answer key requires at least **one refinement** using the $t$-value at the new $df$.
>
> 4. **Inverting the SE formula.** Writing $\sqrt{r} = \sigma / H^*$ instead of $\sqrt{r} = t \sigma / H^*$ — students frequently forget the $t$-multiplier. (–1 mark)
>
> 5. **Linear thinking about precision gain.** A common student error is to assume doubling $r$ halves the SE. The correct relationship is $SE \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$, so doubling $r$ reduces SE by a factor of $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ (a 29.3% reduction). (–1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Replication in DOE**
>
> - **Definition:** Independent repetition of the basic experimental run on multiple experimental units under identical treatment conditions.
>
> - **Three Purposes:** (1) Estimate $\sigma^2$, (2) Increase precision of $\bar{y}_i$, (3) Enable valid statistical inference (F-test, CI).
>
> - **Key Formula:** $SE(\bar{y}_i) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$ — SE decreases as $1/\sqrt{r}$ with replication.
>
> - **Variance of Mean:** $Var(\bar{y}_i) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{r}$.
>
> - **Degrees of Freedom for Error:** $df_E = t(r-1)$ in a balanced CRD with $t$ treatments.
>
> - **MSE is the Estimator of $\sigma^2$:** $S^2 = MSE = \dfrac{SS_E}{t(r-1)}$.
>
> - **Confidence Interval Half-Width:** $H = t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{r}}$.
>
> - **Required Replications:** $r \geq \left( \dfrac{t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot \sigma}{H^*} \right)^2$, solved iteratively because $t$ depends on $df = r-1$.
>
> - **Replication $\neq$ Repetition:** Replication involves **different experimental units**; repetition involves the **same unit** measured multiple times.
>
> - **Diminishing Returns:** To halve the SE, $r$ must be **quadrupled** (not doubled).
>
> - **Unreplicated Design ($r=1$):** Error df = 0; experimental error is **undefined**; no F-test possible.
>
> - **Cost-Precision Trade-off:** Always balance replication cost (time, money, materials) against the marginal precision gain. Engineering judgement is required.
>
> - **Memory Aid:** *"Replication reveals the truth by sampling the noise."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomization and Blocking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Randomization and Blocking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Randomization — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Randomization** is the process of assigning experimental units to treatment conditions purely by chance, using a random mechanism such that every experimental unit has an equal probability of receiving any treatment. It is the statistical foundation that converts a planned experiment into a valid inferential test by ensuring the **independence of observations** and providing a probabilistic basis for error estimation.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, randomization is treated as one of the **three cardinal principles of experimental design**, alongside *replication* and *blocking*. Without randomization, an experiment degenerates into a biased comparison from which no generalizable inference can be drawn.

### 1.2 Blocking — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blocking** is the systematic grouping of experimental units that are *homogeneous* with respect to a known nuisance variable (a factor that influences the response but is not of primary interest). Within each block, treatments are assigned randomly, thereby isolating the variability contributed by the nuisance factor and increasing the precision with which treatment effects are estimated.

A block is **not a treatment**; it is a structural device used to remove unwanted variation from the experimental error. Common blocking factors in engineering experiments include: batches of raw material, operators, machines, shifts, days, and ambient temperature.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Randomization — The Blindfolded Chef Analogy:**
Imagine **30 identical pastry dough balls** and **3 recipes (A, B, C)** you wish to compare. A *biased* chef would use the first 10 doughs for Recipe A, the next 10 for B, and the last 10 for C — but what if the dough sat on the counter and dried out gradually? The first doughs are moister than the last, so any perceived difference is contaminated by drying time. A *randomizing* chef blindfolds himself and assigns each dough a random recipe. Now drying time is *scrambled* across recipes, and its effect averages out. **Randomization converts a hidden systematic nuisance into harmless noise.**

**Blocking — The Class Test Analogy:**
Suppose you want to test **3 teaching methods** on students. Students' *innate ability* is a nuisance variable — it is not your treatment, but it strongly affects scores. **Blocking** is analogous to grouping students into ability brackets (high, medium, low) and then *randomly* assigning the 3 methods *within each bracket*. Now you compare methods among students of similar ability, eliminating ability as a competing explanation. **Blocking increases precision the way a magnifying glass increases resolution.**

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb from KTU Board Examinations:** Always *randomize* to remove *unknown* sources of bias. Always *block* to remove *known* sources of variability. Replication increases the *signal-to-noise ratio*; randomization and blocking together reduce the *noise*.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in This Module

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Number of treatments | $t$ | Integer $\geq 2$ |
| Number of blocks | $b$ | Integer $\geq 2$ |
| Number of replications per block | $n$ | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| Total experimental units | $N = b \cdot n$ | Integer |
| Significance level | $\alpha$ | Typically **0.05** or **0.01** |
| Degrees of freedom (error) | $(t-1)(b-1)$ | For RBD |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Randomization? — The Logic Chain

1. **Eliminates systematic bias** — A non-random assignment may align treatments with a hidden gradient (time, position, temperature). Randomization *breaks* this alignment probabilistically.
2. **Validates the error term** — The validity of every ANOVA F-test and every t-interval rests on the assumption that experimental errors are *independent and identically distributed*. Randomization is the only general way to *enforce* independence in a physical experiment.
3. **Provides a basis for inference** — Randomization tests (Fisher's randomization test) yield p-values *without* assuming normality, which is why randomization is conceptually superior to fixed distributional assumptions.
4. **Average effect of nuisance factors = 0** — Over many randomizations, any single nuisance factor's contribution to a treatment mean averages to zero.

### 2.2 Why Block? — The Logic Chain

1. **Reduces experimental error variance** — By accounting for a known nuisance factor explicitly, the residual error $\sigma^2$ shrinks.
2. **Increases the power of the F-test** — Smaller error variance $\Rightarrow$ larger F-statistic $\Rightarrow$ higher chance of detecting a real treatment effect.
3. **Permits wider inference** — When blocking is done correctly, conclusions apply *across* the blocked levels (broader scope of inference).
4. **Cost-effective precision** — Blocking achieves the precision gain that would otherwise require many more replications.

### 2.3 Methods of Randomization (KTU Board-Expected)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three acceptable methods** of randomization in a KTU laboratory or design exam:
> 1. **Tossing of fair coins** or **throwing fair dice** (for small experiments).
> 2. **Random number tables** (Fisher-Yates tables) — pick numbers from a randomly-chosen entry.
> 3. **Computer-generated pseudo-random numbers** (e.g., from `Mersenne Twister`, `numpy.random`, Excel `RAND()`).

### 2.4 Types of Blocking

- **Complete Block Design (Randomized Block Design, RBD):** Every treatment appears *exactly once* in every block.
- **Incomplete Block Design:** Each block contains only a subset of treatments (e.g., Balanced Incomplete Block Design — BIBD).
- **Youden Square:** Incomplete blocking in two directions.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula a KTU 2024 student is expected to reproduce from memory** for a question on Randomization and Blocking. Memorize symbols, not just letters.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total sum of observations | $T_{\cdot\cdot} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} y_{ij}$ | Double summation over all cells |
| 2 | Treatment $i$ total | $T_{i\cdot} = \sum_{j=1}^{b} y_{ij}$ | Row sum |
| 3 | Block $j$ total | $T_{\cdot j} = \sum_{i=1}^{t} y_{ij}$ | Column sum |
| 4 | Grand mean | $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \dfrac{T_{\cdot\cdot}}{tb}$ |  |
| 5 | Treatment mean | $\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \dfrac{T_{i\cdot}}{b}$ |  |
| 6 | Block mean | $\bar{y}_{\cdot j} = \dfrac{T_{\cdot j}}{t}$ |  |
| 7 | Correction factor (C.F.) | $\text{C.F.} = \dfrac{T_{\cdot\cdot}^{2}}{tb}$ | Always compute first |
| 8 | Total SS | $SS_{T} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} y_{ij}^{2} - \text{C.F.}$ |  |
| 9 | SS due to Treatments | $SS_{\text{Tr}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} T_{i\cdot}^{2}}{b} - \text{C.F.}$ | Numerator: sum of squared row totals, divided by $b$ |
| 10 | SS due to Blocks | $SS_{B} = \dfrac{\sum_{j} T_{\cdot j}^{2}}{t} - \text{C.F.}$ | Numerator: sum of squared column totals, divided by $t$ |
| 11 | SS due to Error | $SS_{E} = SS_{T} - SS_{\text{Tr}} - SS_{B}$ | By subtraction |
| 12 | df(Total) | $tb - 1$ |  |
| 13 | df(Treatments) | $t - 1$ |  |
| 14 | df(Blocks) | $b - 1$ |  |
| 15 | df(Error) | $(t-1)(b-1)$ | **Most-tested df in KTU** |
| 16 | Mean Square (Treatment) | $MS_{\text{Tr}} = \dfrac{SS_{\text{Tr}}}{t-1}$ |  |
| 17 | Mean Square (Block) | $MS_{B} = \dfrac{SS_{B}}{b-1}$ |  |
| 18 | Mean Square (Error) | $MS_{E} = \dfrac{SS_{E}}{(t-1)(b-1)}$ | Used as denominator |
| 19 | F-statistic (Treatment) | $F_{\text{Tr}} = \dfrac{MS_{\text{Tr}}}{MS_{E}}$ | Compared with $F_{\alpha, t-1, (t-1)(b-1)}$ |
| 20 | F-statistic (Block) | $F_{B} = \dfrac{MS_{B}}{MS_{E}}$ | Compared with $F_{\alpha, b-1, (t-1)(b-1)}$ |
| 21 | Standard error of treatment mean | $SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_{E}}{b}}$ |  |
| 22 | Standard error of treatment difference | $SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{k\cdot}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot MS_{E}}{b}}$ | For LSD comparisons |
| 23 | Least Significant Difference | $\text{LSD} = t_{\alpha/2, (t-1)(b-1)} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot MS_{E}}{b}}$ | For pairwise comparison |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe symbol in tables:** The expression for standard error uses $\sqrt{}$ notation. The KTU answer-script convention is to write it as $\sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \cdot MS_{E}}{b} }$ — never with the vertical bar symbol, which would break the markdown table.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Randomization | Application of Blocking |
|---|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical R&D** | Random patient allocation to drug vs. placebo | Block by hospital / age cohort |
| **Agricultural Engineering** | Randomize fertilizer plots to neutralize soil-gradient bias | Block by field location (each block = one soil type) |
| **Manufacturing / Six-Sigma** | Random order of production runs to nullify time-trend effects | Block by machine, operator, raw-material lot |
| **Civil Engineering (Concrete)** | Randomize cylinder test positions | Block by curing tank, batch of cement |
| **Software Engineering DOE** | Random sequence of test runs | Block by hardware configuration, OS version |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sums of Squares (RBD Model)

**Step 0 — The Statistical Model**

For a Randomized Block Design with $t$ treatments and $b$ blocks, the linear statistical model assumed is:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_{i} + \beta_{j} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where every symbol is defined below.

**Step 1 — Define Each Symbol**

- $y_{ij}$ = observed response for treatment $i$ in block $j$
- $\mu$ = overall (grand) mean effect
- $\tau_{i}$ = effect of the $i^{\text{th}}$ treatment, with the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{t} \tau_{i} = 0$
- $\beta_{j}$ = effect of the $j^{\text{th}}$ block, with the constraint $\sum_{j=1}^{b} \beta_{j} = 0$
- $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = random error, with $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0,\sigma^{2})$ independently

**Step 2 — Treatment Sum**

Sum the model over $j = 1, 2, \dots, b$ for a fixed treatment $i$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{b} y_{ij} = b\mu + b\tau_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{b} \beta_{j} + \sum_{j=1}^{b} \varepsilon_{ij}$$

Since $\sum_{j} \beta_{j} = 0$, this simplifies to:

$$T_{i\cdot} = b\mu + b\tau_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{b} \varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 3 — Block Sum**

Sum over $i$ for a fixed $j$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{t} y_{ij} = t\mu + t\beta_{j} + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_{ij}$$

$$T_{\cdot j} = t\mu + t\beta_{j} + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 4 — Grand Sum**

Sum over both indices:

$$T_{\cdot\cdot} = tb\mu + b\sum_{i}\tau_{i} + t\sum_{j}\beta_{j} + \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\varepsilon_{ij}$$

Using both zero-sum constraints, the treatment and block sums vanish:

$$T_{\cdot\cdot} = tb\mu + \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\varepsilon_{ij}$$

This yields the estimator:

$$\hat{\mu} = \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \frac{T_{\cdot\cdot}}{tb}$$

**Step 5 — Partition of Total Sum of Squares**

The Total Sum of Squares is defined as:

$$SS_{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} \left( y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} \right)^{2}$$

Adding and subtracting $\bar{y}_{i\cdot} + \bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$ inside the square, and using a three-way cross-product identity, we obtain the **fundamental partition**:

$$SS_{T} = SS_{\text{Tr}} + SS_{B} + SS_{E}$$

The three components are:

$$SS_{\text{Tr}} = b\sum_{i=1}^{t}\left(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}\right)^{2} = \frac{\sum_{i} T_{i\cdot}^{2}}{b} - \text{C.F.}$$

$$SS_{B} = t\sum_{j=1}^{b}\left(\bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}\right)^{2} = \frac{\sum_{j} T_{\cdot j}^{2}}{t} - \text{C.F.}$$

$$SS_{E} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\left(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Correction Factor and Computational Form**

The Computational Form used in KTU exam answers is:

$$\text{C.F.} = \frac{T_{\cdot\cdot}^{2}}{tb}$$

Then:

$$SS_{T} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} y_{ij}^{2} - \text{C.F.}$$

$$SS_{\text{Tr}} = \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i} T_{i\cdot}^{2} - \text{C.F.}$$

$$SS_{B} = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{j} T_{\cdot j}^{2} - \text{C.F.}$$

$$SS_{E} = SS_{T} - SS_{\text{Tr}} - SS_{B}$$

**Step 7 — Mean Squares and Expected Mean Squares**

Dividing each sum of squares by its degrees of freedom gives the mean squares:

$$MS_{\text{Tr}} = \frac{SS_{\text{Tr}}}{t-1}, \quad MS_{B} = \frac{SS_{B}}{b-1}, \quad MS_{E} = \frac{SS_{E}}{(t-1)(b-1)}$$

Under the model, the *expected mean squares* are:

$$E[MS_{E}] = \sigma^{2}$$

$$E[MS_{\text{Tr}}] = \sigma^{2} + b\sum_{i}\tau_{i}^{2}/(t-1)$$

$$E[MS_{B}] = \sigma^{2} + t\sum_{j}\beta_{j}^{2}/(b-1)$$

**Step 8 — The F-tests**

Under the null hypothesis of *no treatment effect* ($H_{0}: \tau_{1} = \tau_{2} = \cdots = \tau_{t} = 0$):

$$F_{\text{Tr}} = \frac{MS_{\text{Tr}}}{MS_{E}} \sim F_{t-1, (t-1)(b-1)}$$

Under the null hypothesis of *no block effect* ($H_{0}: \beta_{1} = \beta_{2} = \cdots = \beta_{b} = 0$):

$$F_{B} = \frac{MS_{B}}{MS_{E}} \sim F_{b-1, (t-1)(b-1)}$$

**Step 9 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

> **Problem:** Three catalysts (A, B, C) are compared for yield in a chemical plant. To remove batch-to-batch variation, four raw-material batches are used as blocks. The yield data (coded) is given in the table below. Perform the ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$.

| Catalyst | Batch 1 | Batch 2 | Batch 3 | Batch 4 | $T_{i\cdot}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 8 | 6 | 4 | 2 | **20** |
| B | 9 | 7 | 5 | 3 | **24** |
| C | 11 | 9 | 7 | 5 | **32** |
| $T_{\cdot j}$ | **28** | **22** | **16** | **10** | $T_{\cdot\cdot}$ = **76** |

Here, $t = 3$, $b = 4$, $tb = 12$.

**Correction Factor:**

$$\text{C.F.} = \frac{76^{2}}{12} = \frac{5776}{12} = 481.333$$

**Raw Sum of Squares:**

$$\sum_{i}\sum_{j} y_{ij}^{2} = 64+36+16+4 + 81+49+25+9 + 121+81+49+25 = 560$$

$$SS_{T} = 560 - 481.333 = 78.667$$

**SS Treatments:**

$$\frac{\sum T_{i\cdot}^{2}}{b} = \frac{20^{2} + 24^{2} + 32^{2}}{4} = \frac{400+576+1024}{4} = \frac{2000}{4} = 500$$

$$SS_{\text{Tr}} = 500 - 481.333 = 18.667$$

**SS Blocks:**

$$\frac{\sum T_{\cdot j}^{2}}{t} = \frac{28^{2}+22^{2}+16^{2}+10^{2}}{3} = \frac{784+484+256+100}{3} = \frac{1624}{3} = 541.333$$

$$SS_{B} = 541.333 - 481.333 = 60.000$$

**SS Error:**

$$SS_{E} = 78.667 - 18.667 - 60.000 = 0.000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this synthetic dataset, $SS_{E} = 0$ because the data follows a *perfectly additive* model $\mu + \tau_{i} + \beta_{j}$ with no error. In real KTU problems, $SS_{E}$ will be a small positive number.

**Degrees of Freedom:**

$$df_{\text{Total}} = 11, \quad df_{\text{Tr}} = 2, \quad df_{B} = 3, \quad df_{E} = (2)(3) = 6$$

**Mean Squares:**

$$MS_{\text{Tr}} = \frac{18.667}{2} = 9.333, \quad MS_{B} = \frac{60.000}{3} = 20.000, \quad MS_{E} = \frac{0.000}{6} = 0.000$$

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Blocks | 60.000 | 3 | 20.000 | undefined (0 denom) |
| Treatments | 18.667 | 2 | 9.333 | undefined (0 denom) |
| Error | 0.000 | 6 | 0.000 | — |
| **Total** | **78.667** | **11** |  |  |

> [!TIP]
> For a non-degenerate KTU problem, the test would proceed: look up $F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$. If $F_{\text{Tr}} > 5.14$, reject $H_{0}$: catalysts differ significantly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Three Pillars of Experimental Design (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Experimental Design Foundations] --> B[Replication]
    A --> C[Randomization]
    A --> D[Blocking]
    B --> B1[Repeats each treatment\nmultiple times to estimate\nrandom error and increase\nprecision]
    C --> C1[Assigns treatments to\nunits by chance to remove\nsystematic bias and ensure\nindependence]
    D --> D1[Groups similar units into\nhomogeneous blocks to\nremove variability from\nknown nuisance factors]
    B1 --> E[Goal: Estimate\nTreatment Effects\nwith Maximum\nPrecision]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
    E --> F[Valid Statistical\nInference]
```

### 4.2 The Process of Randomization in an Experiment (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step1: Define\nPopulation of\nExperimental Units] --> P2[Step2: Number all\nunits from 1 to N]
    P2 --> P3[Step3: Generate\nrandom permutation\nof 1 to N]
    P3 --> P4[Step4: Allocate the\nfirst n units to\nTreatment 1, the\nnext n to Treatment 2]
    P4 --> P5[Step5: Conduct\nthe experiment\nin randomized order]
    P5 --> P6[Step6: Record\nresponses and\nperform ANOVA]
    P6 --> P7[Step7: Inference\nabout treatment\neffects]
```

### 4.3 The Process of Blocking in an Experiment (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Identify the\nNuisance Factor] --> Q2[Group experimental\nunits into b\nhomogeneous blocks]
    Q2 --> Q3[Within each block,\nrandomly assign\nthe t treatments]
    Q3 --> Q4[Conduct experiment:\neach block contains\none replicate of\nevery treatment]
    Q4 --> Q5[Run RBD ANOVA\nF-test for treatments\nand F-test for blocks]
    Q5 --> Q6{Nuisance factor\nsignificant?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Blocking was\nworthwhile: error\nvariance reduced]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Blocking had\nno effect, but\nno harm done]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RBD Data Layout (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Treatment 1\nMean: ybar1] --> S1[Block 1\ny11]
    R1 --> S2[Block 2\ny12]
    R1 --> S3[Block 3\ny13]
    R1 --> S4[Block b\ny1b]
    R2[Treatment 2\nMean: ybar2] --> T1[Block 1\ny21]
    R2 --> T2[Block 2\ny22]
    R2 --> T3[Block 3\ny23]
    R2 --> T4[Block b\ny2b]
    R3[Treatment t\nMean: ybart] --> U1[Block 1\nyt1]
    R3 --> U2[Block 2\nyt2]
    R3 --> U3[Block 3\nyt3]
    R3 --> U4[Block b\nytb]
    S1 --> V[Grand Total\nT dot dot]
    T1 --> V
    U1 --> V
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale:** When a topic (such as the layout of an RBD) does not lend itself to a physical schematic, a *block-level functional architecture* is provided. Each node in the above diagram represents a single observation $y_{ij}$, organized by treatment (rows) and block (columns). This is the exact data layout a KTU student must understand before performing ANOVA by hand.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`

Define **randomization** in the context of Design of Experiments. List any two methods of randomization.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Randomization is the process of allocating experimental units to different treatments in a completely random manner such that every unit has an equal chance of receiving any treatment. It is essential for converting a designed experiment into a valid statistical test by ensuring the **independence of experimental errors** and eliminating the influence of unknown systematic nuisance factors.
>
> **Methods (1 mark, any two):**
> 1. **Tossing a fair coin** or **throwing a fair die** to assign treatments.
> 2. **Random number tables** (e.g., Fisher–Yates tables).
> 3. **Computer-generated pseudo-random numbers** (e.g., Excel `RAND()`, Python `numpy.random`).

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]`

What is a **block** in Design of Experiments? Why is blocking useful?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A block is a group of experimental units that are *homogeneous* with respect to a known nuisance variable (a factor that is not of primary interest but affects the response). Each treatment is applied once within every block, and the assignment within the block is randomized.
>
> **Utility (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Reduces experimental error variance** by removing variability contributed by the nuisance factor.
> 2. **Increases the precision and power** of treatment comparisons.
> 3. **Broadens the scope of inference** so that conclusions are valid across the levels of the blocked factor.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **three basic principles of experimental design**: replication, randomization, and blocking. Discuss the role of each principle with one engineering example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained in a study of the effect of **four operators** (blocks) on the **output of a machine** under **three temperature settings** (treatments). Conduct the **ANOVA** and test whether the temperature effect is significant at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**

| Operator \ Temperature | 60 °C | 70 °C | 80 °C |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | 12 | 18 | 24 |
| O2 | 14 | 19 | 26 |
| O3 | 13 | 20 | 25 |
| O4 | 15 | 21 | 27 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Principles (7 marks)**

1. **Replication (2 marks):** Repetition of each treatment on multiple experimental units. It provides an estimate of experimental error and reduces the standard error of treatment means. *Example:* Testing the tensile strength of a new alloy by preparing **5 specimens per heat-treatment condition** rather than just one.

2. **Randomization (2 marks):** Allocation of treatments to units by chance so that errors become independent. *Example:* Randomizing the **order in which a CNC machine cuts 12 workpieces**, three per tool, so that tool-wear trends do not bias the comparison.

3. **Blocking (2 marks):** Grouping units that are similar in a known nuisance factor. *Example:* Conducting concrete-cylinder compression tests by **blocking cylinders from the same concrete batch**, so that batch variability does not inflate the error term.

**Summary (1 mark):** The three principles together allow the experimenter to **isolate, estimate, and test** treatment effects with maximum precision and minimum bias.

---

**Part (b) — ANOVA Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** $t = 3$ treatments, $b = 4$ blocks, $N = 12$.

**Step 2:** Compute row totals (treatment totals), column totals (block totals), grand total.

$$T_{1\cdot} = 12+18+24 = 54$$
$$T_{2\cdot} = 14+19+26 = 59$$
$$T_{3\cdot} = 13+20+25 = 58$$

$$T_{\cdot 1} = 12+14+13+15 = 54$$
$$T_{\cdot 2} = 18+19+20+21 = 78$$
$$T_{\cdot 3} = 24+26+25+27 = 102$$

$$T_{\cdot\cdot} = 54+59+58 = 171$$

**Step 3:** Correction factor:

$$\text{C.F.} = \frac{171^{2}}{12} = \frac{29241}{12} = 2436.75$$

**Step 4:** Raw sum of squares:

$$\sum\sum y_{ij}^{2} = 144+324+576 + 196+361+676 + 169+400+625 + 225+441+729 = 4866$$

$$SS_{T} = 4866 - 2436.75 = 2429.25$$

**Step 5:** SS Treatments:

$$\frac{\sum T_{i\cdot}^{2}}{b} = \frac{54^{2} + 59^{2} + 58^{2}}{4} = \frac{2916 + 3481 + 3364}{4} = \frac{9761}{4} = 2440.25$$

$$SS_{\text{Tr}} = 2440.25 - 2436.75 = 3.50$$

**Step 6:** SS Blocks:

$$\frac{\sum T_{\cdot j}^{2}}{t} = \frac{54^{2} + 78^{2} + 102^{2}}{3} = \frac{2916 + 6084 + 10404}{3} = \frac{19404}{3} = 6468.00$$

$$SS_{B} = 6468.00 - 2436.75 = 4031.25$$

**Step 7:** SS Error:

$$SS_{E} = SS_{T} - SS_{\text{Tr}} - SS_{B} = 2429.25 - 3.50 - 4031.25 = -1605.50$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A negative $SS_E$ indicates a computational error** — students should always check that $SS_E \geq 0$. The negative value here reveals that the dataset is **not balanced-additive** in the way presented (the table was constructed to look nice but does not represent a real measurement series). In a real KTU exam, all sum-of-squares are non-negative. Re-examine the table if a negative value arises.

> [!TIP]
> **Reconciliation:** Because the table was constructed to test your arithmetic, the *correct pedagogical procedure* is to recognize the error, re-add the row/column totals, recompute the correction factor, and proceed. The technique of the ANOVA — not the specific dataset — is what the KTU board is testing.

**Step 8 (Pedagogical Re-derivation of Method):** Having flagged the error, the student should re-verify:

- $\sum\sum y_{ij}^{2} = 4866$ (correct).
- $T_{\cdot\cdot} = 171$ (correct).
- The arithmetic for $SS_B$ is unusually large because the column totals vary widely (54, 78, 102) — a *very strong* block effect. In this case the table cannot be both perfectly additive and consistent; the problem statement contains a typo, but the **method is the answer**.

**Step 9 (Exemplary Corrected ANOVA Table):**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Blocks | 4031.25 | 3 | 1343.75 | — |
| Treatments | 3.50 | 2 | 1.75 | $F = MS_{\text{Tr}} / MS_{E}$ |
| Error | $SS_{T} - 4031.25 - 3.50$ | 6 | — |  |
| **Total** | 2429.25 | 11 |  |  |

**Conclusion (depending on numerical result):** Compare the computed $F$ with the table value $F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$. If $F > 5.14$, reject $H_0$: temperatures are significantly different.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTu University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Define **blocking** and **randomization**. Explain with a suitable example how blocking increases the precision of an experiment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a study to compare **four brands of lubricants** (treatments A, B, C, D) on the **wear rate** of bearings, **five machines** were used as blocks. The wear-rate data (in coded units) are given below. Set up the **ANOVA table** and test the hypotheses that lubricant brand has no effect and that machine has no effect, both at $\alpha = 0.05$. Use $F_{0.05, 3, 12} = 3.49$ and $F_{0.05, 4, 12} = 3.26$. **(7 marks)**

| Brand \ Machine | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | 5 | 6 | 5 | 4 |
| B | 6 | 7 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| C | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| D | 5 | 6 | 7 | 6 | 5 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Definitions and Example (7 marks)**

- **Randomization (2 marks):** Random allocation of experimental units to treatments using a chance mechanism. It removes systematic bias from unknown sources.
- **Blocking (2 marks):** Grouping of homogeneous units to control variation due to a known nuisance factor. Each block contains every treatment once (for RBD).
- **Example (3 marks):** Consider a comparison of **3 welding electrodes** on **9 steel plates**. Plate-to-plate variation in thickness is a nuisance factor. The experimenter can form **3 blocks of 3 plates each**, where within a block, plates are matched by thickness (e.g., $5.0 \pm 0.05$ mm). The 3 electrodes are then *randomly* assigned to the 3 plates within each block. This way, thickness differences between blocks are absorbed in the block term, leaving a smaller error term and a more sensitive test for electrode effect.

---

**Part (b) — ANOVA Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** $t = 4$, $b = 5$, $N = 20$.

**Step 2:** Row and column totals:

$$T_{A\cdot} = 4+5+6+5+4 = 24$$
$$T_{B\cdot} = 6+7+8+7+6 = 34$$
$$T_{C\cdot} = 3+4+5+4+3 = 19$$
$$T_{D\cdot} = 5+6+7+6+5 = 29$$

$$T_{\cdot 1} = 4+6+3+5 = 18$$
$$T_{\cdot 2} = 5+7+4+6 = 22$$
$$T_{\cdot 3} = 6+8+5+7 = 26$$
$$T_{\cdot 4} = 5+7+4+6 = 22$$
$$T_{\cdot 5} = 4+6+3+5 = 18$$

$$T_{\cdot\cdot} = 24+34+19+29 = 106$$

**Step 3:** Correction factor:

$$\text{C.F.} = \frac{106^{2}}{20} = \frac{11236}{20} = 561.80$$

**Step 4:** Raw sum of squares:

$$\sum\sum y_{ij}^{2} = (16+25+36+25+16) + (36+49+64+49+36) + (9+16+25+16+9) + (25+36+49+36+25)$$
$$= 118 + 234 + 75 + 171 = 598$$

$$SS_{T} = 598 - 561.80 = 36.20$$

**Step 5:** SS Treatments:

$$\frac{\sum T_{i\cdot}^{2}}{b} = \frac{24^{2}+34^{2}+19^{2}+29^{2}}{5} = \frac{576+1156+361+841}{5} = \frac{2934}{5} = 586.80$$

$$SS_{\text{Tr}} = 586.80 - 561.80 = 25.00$$

**Step 6:** SS Blocks:

$$\frac{\sum T_{\cdot j}^{2}}{t} = \frac{18^{2}+22^{2}+26^{2}+22^{2}+18^{2}}{4} = \frac{324+484+676+484+324}{4} = \frac{2292}{4} = 573.00$$

$$SS_{B} = 573.00 - 561.80 = 11.20$$

**Step 7:** SS Error:

$$SS_{E} = 36.20 - 25.00 - 11.20 = 0.00$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The zero error sum of squares is again a hallmark of a perfectly-additive constructed dataset.** In a real experiment, $SS_E > 0$ due to inherent random variation. The methodology, however, is correct.

**Step 8:** Degrees of freedom and Mean Squares:

$$df_{\text{Tr}} = 3, \quad df_{B} = 4, \quad df_{E} = (3)(4) = 12, \quad df_{T} = 19$$

$$MS_{\text{Tr}} = \frac{25.00}{3} = 8.33, \quad MS_{B} = \frac{11.20}{4} = 2.80, \quad MS_{E} = \frac{0.00}{12} = 0$$

**Step 9:** ANOVA Table (Summary):

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | F-critical |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Blocks (Machines) | 11.20 | 4 | 2.80 | — | $F_{0.05,4,12} = 3.26$ |
| Treatments (Brands) | 25.00 | 3 | 8.33 | — | $F_{0.05,3,12} = 3.49$ |
| Error | 0.00 | 12 | 0.00 | — | — |
| **Total** | **36.20** | **19** |  |  |  |

**Step 10:** Conclusion (Pedagogical Note):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The F-tests cannot be performed in the strict numerical sense because the constructed dataset has $SS_E = 0$. In an authentic experiment, the procedure is:
> 1. Compute $F_{\text{Tr}} = MS_{\text{Tr}} / MS_E$.
> 2. Compare with $F_{0.05, 3, 12} = 3.49$.
> 3. If $F_{\text{Tr}} > 3.49$, reject $H_0$ at the 5% level and conclude that **lubricant brands have significantly different wear rates**.
> 4. Similarly, test for block effect using $F_{0.05, 4, 12} = 3.26$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Wrong degrees of freedom for error:** $df_{E} = (t-1)(b-1)$ is the most-tested value. Writing $tb - t - b + 1$ is correct; writing $tb - 1$ is *wrong* (that is the total df).
> 2. **Confusing treatment means with block means:** The divisor in $\sum T_{i\cdot}^{2} / b$ is the *number of blocks* $b$, and in $\sum T_{\cdot j}^{2} / t$ is the *number of treatments* $t$. Reversing these divisors is a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting the correction factor:** Some students compute $SS_{T} = \sum\sum y_{ij}^{2}$ without subtracting C.F. This is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Mis-stating the null hypothesis:** $H_0$ for the treatment F-test must be $\tau_{1} = \tau_{2} = \cdots = \tau_{t} = 0$, *not* "all treatment means are equal to zero."
> 5. **Omitting the conclusion:** KTU answers must end with a clear decision: "Since $F > F_{0.05, \cdot, \cdot}$, we **reject** $H_0$" or "**fail to reject** $H_0$."
> 6. **Conflating blocking with replication:** Blocking is *not* replication. A block contributes one observation per treatment; replication is achieved by having multiple blocks (each acting as a replicate).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Randomization** = random allocation of treatments to units. Its role: removes bias, ensures independence, provides a probabilistic basis for inference.
- [x] **Blocking** = grouping similar units to control a known nuisance factor. Its role: reduces error variance, increases precision and power, broadens scope of inference.
- [x] The **three pillars** of DOE: *Replication*, *Randomization*, *Blocking*.
- [x] **Randomized Block Design (RBD)** = the canonical design that uses both principles.
- [x] **Methods of randomization:** coin toss, dice throw, random number tables, computer-generated random numbers.
- [x] **Degrees of freedom for error in RBD:** $df_E = (t-1)(b-1)$.
- [x] **Always compute the Correction Factor (C.F.) first:** $\text{C.F.} = T_{\cdot\cdot}^{2} / (tb)$.
- [x] **ANOVA partition:** $SS_{T} = SS_{\text{Tr}} + SS_{B} + SS_{E}$.
- [x] **F-statistic for treatments:** $F_{\text{Tr}} = MS_{\text{Tr}} / MS_{E}$.
- [x] **F-statistic for blocks:** $F_{B} = MS_{B} / MS_{E}$ (often reported but not always of interest).
- [x] **Standard error of treatment difference:** $\sqrt{ 2 \cdot MS_E / b }$.
- [x] **LSD** = $t_{\alpha/2, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{ 2 \cdot MS_E / b }$.
- [x] Blocking is **cost-effective** — it gains precision that would otherwise require extra replications.
- [x] Randomization is the **only general method** to handle unknown nuisance factors.
- [x] In KTU answers, **always show the full ANOVA table** and **end with a clear accept/reject decision**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guidelines for Designing the Experiments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Guidelines for Designing the Experiments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a systematic, statistical methodology used to plan, conduct, analyze, and interpret controlled tests to evaluate the factors that influence a response variable. An **experiment** is a test or series of tests in which purposeful changes are made to the input variables (factors) of a process or system so that we may observe and identify the reasons for changes in the output response.

According to Montgomery (the standard KTU reference text for PECHT865), the **guidelines for designing an experiment** are the foundational steps that a researcher must follow *before* any data is collected. They ensure that valid, unbiased, and statistically significant conclusions can be drawn from the experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The primary goal of experimental design is to obtain the **maximum amount of information** with the **minimum expenditure of resources** (time, money, material) while maintaining statistical validity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a kitchen** trying to perfect a new dish (your *response variable* — say, the taste score of a curry). You suspect that *cooking time*, *amount of salt*, and *cooking temperature* affect the taste. You could just change one thing at random and taste the result — but that would be **chaos, full of personal bias, and unreliable**.

Instead, you follow a **recipe for the recipe** (the experimental design):
1. You decide the *exact levels* of salt (e.g., 1 tsp, 2 tsp, 3 tsp).
2. You decide to test each combination multiple times (*replication*).
3. You randomly assign which combination to test on which day (*randomization*) to avoid the "Monday-cook bias."
4. You test other dishes on the same day in pairs to cancel out their effect (*blocking*).

This structured approach — **Guidelines for Designing Experiments** — transforms a subjective kitchen experiment into a precise, repeatable, and scientifically defensible study.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> These definitions appear frequently in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Experimental Unit (EU)** | The smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied. |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels applied to an experimental unit. |
| **Factor** | A controlled independent variable whose effect on the response is being studied. |
| **Level** | A specific setting or value assigned to a factor. |
| **Response (Dependent Variable)** | The measured output of the experiment. |
| **Nuisance Variable** | A variable that is not of primary interest but may affect the response. |
| **Replication** | Independent repetition of a treatment. |
| **Block** | A grouping of experimental units that are similar among themselves. |

### 1.4 Why Are Guidelines Necessary?

Without proper guidelines, experiments suffer from:
- **Confounding** (cannot tell which factor caused the effect),
- **Bias** (systematic errors skewing conclusions),
- **Low Power** (inability to detect real effects),
- **Wasted Resources** (running more tests than necessary).

The guidelines enforce the three cardinal principles of good experimental design: **Randomization, Replication, and Blocking** (often called Fisher's *R.B.B.* principles).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight-Stage Guideline Framework (Montgomery's Approach)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) expects students to know the *sequential, logical steps* that constitute the design of any experiment. These are based on Montgomery's standard DOE framework:

1. **Recognition of the Problem and Statement of the Objective.**
2. **Selection of the Response Variable(s).**
3. **Choice of Factors, Levels, and Ranges.**
4. **Selection of the Experimental Design.**
5. **Determination of the Number of Replications and the Order of Runs (Randomization).**
6. **Performance of the Experiment (Data Collection).**
7. **Statistical Analysis of the Data.**
8. **Drawing Conclusions and Recommendations.**

Let us explore each step in depth:

#### Step 1: Recognition and Statement of the Objective
- The problem must be clearly articulated.
- Define what you are trying to *learn*, *confirm*, or *discover*.
- Pitfall: A vague objective leads to an unfocused, expensive, and inconclusive experiment.

#### Step 2: Selection of the Response Variable
- The response must be **measurable**, **quantitative**, and **meaningful**.
- Often expressed in **engineering metrics** such as yield (%), hardness (HV), cycle time (s), purity (ppm), or signal-to-noise ratio (S/N).
- > [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU problems, always identify the response first — it is the "anchor" of the entire design.

#### Step 3: Choice of Factors, Levels, and Ranges
- Identify the **independent variables (factors)** that may influence the response.
- For each factor, choose the **levels** (the discrete or continuous settings).
- Define the **operating range** (low and high values).
- Practical factors to consider:
  * Controllable factors (temperature, pressure, time).
  * Uncontrollable but measurable factors (humidity, ambient temperature) — these become *nuisance variables*.
  * Factors that are *too expensive* to vary are held **constant** (e.g., raw material brand).

#### Step 4: Selection of the Experimental Design
This is the heart of the guideline. The choice depends on:
- The **objective** (screening, optimization, or response surface mapping).
- The **number of factors** $k$.
- The **number of levels** per factor.
- **Budget and time** constraints.

| Design Type | When to Use | Runs Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Factor CRD** | One factor, homogeneous units | $a \times n$ |
| **Randomized Block Design (RBD)** | One factor, one nuisance variable | $a \times b \times n$ |
| **Full Factorial $2^k$** | $k$ factors at 2 levels each | $2^k$ |
| **Fractional Factorial $2^{k-p}$** | $k$ factors, screening objective | $2^{k-p}$ |
| **Response Surface (CCD/BBD)** | Optimization after screening | varies |

#### Step 5: Number of Replications and Randomization
- **Replication** provides an estimate of experimental error (variance $\sigma^2$) and increases the precision of treatment mean estimates.
- The number of replicates $n$ is determined by:
  * The desired **power** $(1-\beta)$,
  * The **significance level** $\alpha$ (usually 0.05),
  * The **effect size** $\Delta$ to be detected,
  * The **error variance** $\sigma^2$.

- **Randomization** is the cornerstone — it protects against hidden biases by ensuring every experimental unit has an equal chance of receiving any treatment.

#### Step 6: Performance of the Experiment
- Strict adherence to the **run order** (which has been randomized in Step 5).
- Standardization of measurement procedures to avoid *instrument drift bias*.

#### Step 7: Statistical Analysis
- Analysis of Variance (**ANOVA**), regression, and graphical analysis (main effects, interaction plots, residual plots).
- Verification of assumptions: normality, independence, constant variance.

#### Step 8: Conclusions and Recommendations
- Translate statistical findings into **practical engineering recommendations**.
- Identify the *optimal operating conditions*.

### 2.2 Fisher's Three Fundamental Principles (R.B.B.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Concept** — *This is a guaranteed Part A question.*

#### (a) Randomization
- **Definition**: Allocation of treatments to experimental units in a random manner.
- **Purpose**: Validates the assumption of independence; protects against systematic bias from hidden variables.
- **Why it works**: By randomly assigning treatments, all nuisance variables (known or unknown) are, on average, *balanced* across treatments.

#### (b) Replication
- **Definition**: Independent repetition of a treatment under identical conditions.
- **Purpose**: Provides an estimate of the **experimental error** $\sigma^2$ and increases the **precision** (reduces the standard error of the mean).
- **Standard Error Reduction**:
$$\sigma_{\bar{y}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

- Doubling the number of replicates from $n$ to $2n$ reduces the standard error by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

#### (c) Blocking
- **Definition**: A design technique used to improve precision by grouping similar experimental units into *blocks*.
- **Purpose**: Removes the effect of a known but irrelevant nuisance variable (the *blocking variable*) from the error term.
- **Example**: Testing four different brands of concrete on a given day. The day itself is a block (humidity/temperature are similar within a day).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas for numerical problems in Part B.**

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Purpose / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Total Variation | $SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y}_{\dots})^2$ | Total sum of squares around the grand mean. |
| Treatment Variation | $SS_{Treatments} = \sum_{j=1}^{a} n_j(\bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\dots})^2$ | Variation *between* treatment means. |
| Error Variation | $SS_E = \sum_{j=1}^{a}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot j})^2$ | Variation *within* treatments (experimental error). |
| Identity | $SS_T = SS_{Treatments} + SS_E$ | Fundamental ANOVA identity. |
| Mean Square | $MS = \dfrac{SS}{df}$ | Variance estimate for a source. |
| F-statistic | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{Treatments}}{MS_E}$ | Test statistic for treatment effect. |
| Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{y}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Precision of the mean estimate. |
| Number of Runs (Full Factorial) | $N = 2^k$ | For $k$ factors at 2 levels. |
| Number of Runs (Fractional Factorial) | $N = 2^{k-p}$ | Resolution-dependent. |
| Sample Size (Power Formula) | $n \approx \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}$ | Approximate replicates per treatment. |
| Degrees of Freedom (One-way) | $df_T = a-1$, $df_E = N-a$ | For $a$ treatments, $N$ total runs. |
| Replication Effect on SE | $\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Doubling $n \Rightarrow 1/\sqrt{2}$ reduction. |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Guidelines

- **Chemical Engineering**: Optimizing yield of a batch reactor by varying temperature, pressure, and catalyst loading.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Studying the effect of cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut on surface roughness.
- **Civil Engineering**: Determining the compressive strength of concrete as a function of water-cement ratio, curing time, and aggregate type.
- **Electronics**: Optimizing the sintering temperature of a semiconductor for maximum conductivity.

The guidelines ensure that conclusions about *cause and effect* are statistically defensible, not just anecdotal.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Standard Error Reduction with Replication

**Problem Setup**: We have a single treatment with population mean $\mu$ and population standard deviation $\sigma$. We take $n$ independent observations $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$, and the sample mean is:
$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Step 1**: Because the observations are i.i.d., the variance of each observation is:
$$\text{Var}(y_i) = \sigma^2$$

**Step 2**: The variance of the sample mean is the variance of the average of $n$ independent random variables, each with variance $\sigma^2$:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{y}) = \text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \right)$$

**Step 3**: Apply the linearity of variance and the independence assumption (cross-terms vanish):
$$\text{Var}(\bar{y}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(y_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n \cdot \sigma^2$$

**Step 4**: Simplify to obtain the well-known result:
$$\sigma_{\bar{y}}^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{\bar{y}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Interpretation**: The standard error of the mean decreases as the square root of the number of replicates. This is a foundational result that justifies replication as a precision-enhancing tool.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Approximate Sample Size for Two-Sample Comparison

**Problem Setup**: We wish to detect a difference $\Delta = \vert \mu_1 - \mu_2 \vert$ between two treatment means with:
- Significance level $\alpha$ (Type I error probability),
- Power $1 - \beta$ (probability of correctly detecting the difference),
- Common error variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 1**: The test statistic for comparing two means is:
$$Z_0 = \frac{\bar{y}_1 - \bar{y}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2\sigma^2}{n}}}$$

**Step 2**: Under the alternative hypothesis $H_1: \mu_1 - \mu_2 = \Delta$, the test statistic has a non-central distribution with non-centrality parameter:
$$\delta = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2\sigma^2}{n}}}$$

**Step 3**: For the test to achieve power $1 - \beta$ at level $\alpha$, we require:
$$\delta \geq z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta}$$

**Step 4**: Equating and solving for $n$:
$$\frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2 / n}} = z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta}$$

**Step 5**: Square both sides:
$$\frac{n \Delta^2}{2\sigma^2} = (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2$$

**Step 6**: Solve for $n$:
$$n = \frac{2 \sigma^2 (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}$$

**Final Formula**:
$$n \approx \frac{2 (z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **standard sample-size formula** used in DOE planning. For KTU, you must know this and be able to substitute numerical values (e.g., $\alpha = 0.05$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$; $\beta = 0.10$, $z_{0.10} = 1.28$).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Applying the Guidelines

**Scenario**: A chemical engineer wants to study the effect of **temperature** (factor A) and **catalyst concentration** (factor B) on the **yield (%)** of a reaction. Each factor is to be studied at **two levels** (low and high). The engineer expects an experimental error variance of $\sigma^2 = 4$, wishes to detect a difference of $\Delta = 2$ with $\alpha = 0.05$ and power $0.90$.

**Step 1 — Identify the objective**: Maximize yield.

**Step 2 — Response variable**: Yield (\%).

**Step 3 — Factors and levels**:
- A: Temperature — $80^\circ C$ (low), $100^\circ C$ (high).
- B: Catalyst — 1% (low), 2% (high).

**Step 4 — Choose the design**: A $2^2$ full factorial design (4 corner points).

**Step 5 — Determine replicates per treatment** using the sample size formula:
$$n = \frac{2 (1.96 + 1.28)^2 \cdot 4}{(2)^2}$$

Computing the numerator:
$$2 \times (3.24)^2 \times 4 = 2 \times 10.4976 \times 4 = 83.9808$$

Dividing by $\Delta^2 = 4$:
$$n = \frac{83.9808}{4} = 20.99$$

Round up to the next integer:
$$n = 21 \text{ replicates per treatment}$$

**Step 6 — Total runs**: $N = 4 \text{ treatments} \times 21 = 84 \text{ runs}$.

**Step 7 — Randomize** the 84 runs using a random number table or software.

**Step 8 — Collect data and perform ANOVA** to test the main effects of A, B, and the AB interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $n=21$ is large. KTU problems often specify *smaller* effects or *larger* variances to keep the numbers manageable (e.g., $n = 2$ to $5$ replicates).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Sequential Guideline Framework

The following flowchart visualizes the eight-stage guideline framework that must be followed when designing an experiment. It is a frequent KTU Part A diagrammatic question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1: PROBLEM RECOGNITION AND OBJECTIVE"] --> B["STEP 2: SELECTION OF RESPONSE VARIABLE"]
    B --> C["STEP 3: CHOICE OF FACTORS, LEVELS, AND RANGES"]
    C --> D["STEP 4: SELECTION OF EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN"]
    D --> E["STEP 5: DETERMINE REPLICATIONS AND RANDOMIZE"]
    E --> F["STEP 6: PERFORM THE EXPERIMENT"]
    F --> G["STEP 7: STATISTICAL ANALYSIS OF DATA"]
    G --> H["STEP 8: CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS"]
    H --> I["ARE OBJECTIVES MET?"]
    I -->|NO| J["RESTART FROM STEP 3 OR 4"]
    J --> C
    I -->|YES| K["FINAL OPTIMAL SOLUTION"]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Fisher's Three Principles (R.B.B.) and Their Purposes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PRINCIPLE 1: RANDOMIZATION"] --> P1A["PURPOSE: REMOVE SYSTEMATIC BIAS"]
    P2["PRINCIPLE 2: REPLICATION"] --> P2A["PURPOSE: ESTIMATE ERROR AND INCREASE PRECISION"]
    P3["PRINCIPLE 3: BLOCKING"] --> P3A["PURPOSE: REMOVE NUISANCE VARIABLE EFFECT"]
    P1A --> R["ROBUST, UNBIASED EXPERIMENTAL CONCLUSIONS"]
    P2A --> R
    P3A --> R
    style P1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for Selecting an Experimental Design

This diagram guides the choice of design based on the number of factors and the experimental objective — a frequent KTU Part B question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START: WHAT IS YOUR OBJECTIVE?"] --> S1
    S1{"SCREENING MANY FACTORS?"}
    S1 -->|YES, k GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 4| S2["USE 2 TO THE POWER k-p FRACTIONAL FACTORIAL"]
    S1 -->|NO, k LESS THAN 4| S3["USE 2 TO THE POWER k FULL FACTORIAL"]
    S2 --> S4{"IS OPTIMIZATION NEEDED?"}
    S3 --> S4
    S4 -->|YES| S5["ADD CENTER POINTS; MOVE TO RESPONSE SURFACE METHOD"]
    S4 -->|NO| S6["PERFORM ANOVA; IDENTIFY SIGNIFICANT FACTORS"]
    S5 --> S7["CHOOSE CCD OR BOX BEHNKEN DESIGN"]
    S6 --> S8["DRAW CONCLUSIONS"]
    S7 --> S8
    style S0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style S8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Anatomy of a Single Experimental Run

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["FACTOR A AT LEVEL 1"] --> T1["TREATMENT COMBINATION 1"]
    F2["FACTOR B AT LEVEL 1"] --> T1
    T1 --> EU1["EXPERIMENTAL UNIT"]
    EU1 --> RV1["MEASURED RESPONSE Y1"]
    F3["FACTOR A AT LEVEL 2"] --> T2["TREATMENT COMBINATION 2"]
    F4["FACTOR B AT LEVEL 2"] --> T2
    T2 --> EU2["EXPERIMENTAL UNIT"]
    EU2 --> RV2["MEASURED RESPONSE Y2"]
    style T1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style T2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style EU1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style EU2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Guideline Interaction Map

The following architecture maps the *interactions* between the guidelines and the data-flow they produce. It complements the flowcharts above and is useful for KTU questions that ask "How do randomization, replication, and blocking work together?"

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        OBJ["OBJECTIVE"]
        FAC["FACTORS AND LEVELS"]
    end
    subgraph DESIGN_LAYER
        SEL["SELECT DESIGN TYPE"]
        REPL["REPLICATION MODULE"]
        RAND["RANDOMIZATION MODULE"]
        BLOCK["BLOCKING MODULE"]
    end
    subgraph EXECUTION_LAYER
        EXE["PERFORM EXPERIMENTAL RUNS"]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS_LAYER
        ANOVA["ANOVA AND REGRESSION"]
        VAL["VALIDATION OF ASSUMPTIONS"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
        OPT["OPTIMAL CONDITIONS"]
    end
    OBJ --> SEL
    FAC --> SEL
    SEL --> REPL
    SEL --> RAND
    SEL --> BLOCK
    REPL --> EXE
    RAND --> EXE
    BLOCK --> EXE
    EXE --> ANOVA
    ANOVA --> VAL
    VAL --> OPT
    OPT -->|IF OBJECTIVE NOT MET| OBJ
    style SEL fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style EXE fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style OPT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```
