```mermaid
    classDef rcbd fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#4472c4,color:#000000
    classDef lsd fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000000
    classDef gls fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LSD Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step A: Identify Rows, Columns, Treatments and N equal to p squared"]:::block
    P2["Step B: Construct p x p LSD satisfying row and column constraints"]:::block
    P3["Step C: Record Observations Y_ij and Compute G and CF"]:::block
    P4["Step D: Compute TSS, SSR, SSC, SST and SSE using formulas"]:::block
    P5["Step E: Construct ANOVA Table with df, MS and F Ratios"]:::block
    P6["Step F: Compare F Ratios with Tabulated F Critical Value"]:::block
    P7["Step G: If Significant then Perform CD Test for Pair wise Comparison"]:::block
    P8["Step H: Draw Statistical and Practical Conclusions"]:::block
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8
    classDef block fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q.A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]

**Define a Latin Square Design. State the conditions under which it is preferred over RCBD.**

**Model Answer:**

A Latin Square Design (LSD) is a design in which **p treatments are arranged in a p × p square such that each treatment occurs exactly once in each row and exactly once in each column**. It is represented by the model:

$$
Y_{ij(k)} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + \tau_k + \varepsilon_{ij(k)}
$$

LSD is preferred over RCBD when **two independent sources of nuisance variation** (e.g., two-dimensional gradients in the experimental material) are simultaneously present and must be controlled. RCBD can remove only one nuisance source, whereas LSD removes two. However, LSD requires the **number of treatments to equal the number of rows and columns**, making it suitable only when *p* is moderate (typically ≤ 12).

---

### Q.A2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**List any four assumptions underlying the Latin Square Design.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Additivity** of row, column, and treatment effects (no interaction).
2. **Normality** of errors: εᵢⱼ₍ₖ₎ ~ N(0, σ²).
3. **Homogeneity of variance** — σ² is constant across all p² cells.
4. **Independence** of all observations.
5. (Bonus) **No interaction** between row × column, row × treatment, or column × treatment effects.

---

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either the full Question A or the full Question B**.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

#### Q.A(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the layout, statistical model, and ANOVA decomposition (with degrees of freedom) for a Latin Square Design with p treatments.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Layout:** A *p × p* square in which each of the *p* treatments occurs exactly once in each row and exactly once in each column.

**2. Statistical Model:**

$$
Y_{ij(k)} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + \tau_k + \varepsilon_{ij(k)}
$$

where:
- *μ* = overall mean
- *αᵢ* = effect of *i*-th row (Σαᵢ = 0)
- *βⱼ* = effect of *j*-th column (Σβⱼ = 0)
- *τₖ* = effect of *k*-th treatment (Στₖ = 0)
- *εᵢⱼ₍ₖ₎* = random error, NID(0, σ²)

**3. ANOVA Decomposition:**

$$
TSS = SSR + SSC + SST + SSE
$$

**4. Degrees of Freedom Table:**

| Source | df |
|---|---|
| Rows | *p* − 1 |
| Columns | *p* − 1 |
| Treatments | *p* − 1 |
| Error | (*p* − 1)(*p* − 2) |
| **Total** | *p²* − 1 |

**5. Formulas (with notation safeguard):**

$$
CF = \frac{G^2}{p^2},\qquad SSR = \frac{\sum R_i^2}{p} - CF
$$

$$
SSC = \frac{\sum C_j^2}{p} - CF,\qquad SST = \frac{\sum T_k^2}{p} - CF
$$

$$
SSE = TSS - SSR - SSC - SST
$$

**6. F-tests:**

$$
F_R = \frac{MSR}{MSE},\quad F_C = \frac{MSC}{MSE},\quad F_T = \frac{MST}{MSE}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.A-a):** Students frequently write **p(p − 2)** instead of **(p − 1)(p − 2)** for the error degrees of freedom. Always write it as **(p − 1)(p − 2)** — this is the orthogonality-driven decomposition.

---

#### Q.A(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**The following 4 × 4 LSD was conducted to compare 4 fuels (A, B, C, D) on mileage. Carry out the ANOVA at α = 0.05.**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
R_1: & A=15 & B=18 & C=14 & D=20 \\
\hline
R_2: & B=17 & C=19 & D=16 & A=13 \\
\hline
R_3: & C=21 & D=15 & A=17 & B=16 \\
\hline
R_4: & D=14 & A=18 & B=19 & C=20 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Totals**

| | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 15 | 18 | 14 | 20 | **67** |
| R2 | 17 | 19 | 16 | 13 | **65** |
| R3 | 21 | 15 | 17 | 16 | **69** |
| R4 | 14 | 18 | 19 | 20 | **71** |
| **Col Total** | **67** | **70** | **66** | **69** | **G = 272** |

**Treatment Totals:**

$$
T_A = 15 + 13 + 17 + 18 = 63,\quad T_B = 18 + 17 + 16 + 19 = 70
$$

$$
T_C = 14 + 19 + 21 + 20 = 74,\quad T_D = 20 + 16 + 15 + 14 = 65
$$

**Step 2: Correction Factor**

$$
CF = \frac{(272)^2}{16} = \frac{73984}{16} = 4624.00
$$

**Step 3: Sum of Squares**

$$
\sum\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 225+324+196+400+289+361+256+169+441+225+289+256+196+324+361+400 = 4712
$$

$$
TSS = 4712 - 4624 = 88.00
$$

**Row SS:**

$$
\sum R_i^2 = 67^2 + 65^2 + 69^2 + 71^2 = 4489 + 4225 + 4761 + 5041 = 18516
$$

$$
SSR = \frac{18516}{4} - 4624 = 4629.00 - 4624.00 = 5.00
$$

**Column SS:**

$$
\sum C_j^2 = 67^2 + 70^2 + 66^2 + 69^2 = 4489 + 4900 + 4356 + 4761 = 18506
$$

$$
SSC = \frac{18506}{4} - 4624 = 4626.50 - 4624.00 = 2.50
$$

**Treatment SS:**

$$
\sum T_k^2 = 63^2 + 70^2 + 74^2 + 65^2 = 3969 + 4900 + 5476 + 4225 = 18570
$$

$$
SST = \frac{18570}{4} - 4624 = 4642.50 - 4624.00 = 18.50
$$

**Error SS:**

$$
SSE = 88.00 - 5.00 - 2.50 - 18.50 = 62.00
$$

**Step 4: ANOVA Table**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rows | 3 | 5.00 | 1.667 | 0.161 |
| Columns | 3 | 2.50 | 0.833 | 0.081 |
| Treatments | 3 | 18.50 | 6.167 | 0.597 |
| Error | 6 | 62.00 | 10.333 | — |
| **Total** | 15 | 88.00 | — | — |

**Step 5: Decision**

Critical value $F_{0.05,\,(3,\,6)} = 4.76$. All three F-ratios are far below 4.76.

**Conclusion:** None of the effects (rows, columns, treatments) are significant at the 5 % level. There is **no statistically significant difference** among the four fuels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.A-b):** A very common error is forgetting to divide by *p* when computing SSR, SSC, SST. Always remember: each SS formula is **(Sum of squares of marginal totals) ÷ p − CF**. Also do **not** round intermediate values — keep 2–3 decimals until the final ANOVA.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

#### Q.B(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Compare CRD, RCBD, and LSD with respect to: (i) number of nuisance factors removed, (ii) layout structure, (iii) error degrees of freedom, and (iv) practical applications.**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | CRD | RCBD | LSD |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Nuisance factors removed** | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **(ii) Layout structure** | Random allocation of *N* units to *t* treatments | *b* blocks × *t* treatments; one nuisance direction | *t × t* square; two nuisance directions |
| **(iii) Error df** | *N* − *t* | *N* − *t* − *b* + 1 | *N* − 3(*t* − 1) = (*t* − 1)(*t* − 2) |
| **(iv) Best application** | Homogeneous material | One gradient (e.g., time, location) | Two gradients (e.g., fertility × moisture) |

**Discussion:**

- **CRD** is the simplest design but inefficient when heterogeneity exists.
- **RCBD** controls one directional variation by blocking. It is the workhorse of agricultural and industrial experiments.
- **LSD** extends RCBD by adding a second blocking dimension (columns). This dramatically improves precision when both row and column gradients are present, at the cost of reduced error degrees of freedom — limiting its use to moderate *t*.

**Mathematical Connection:** LSD reduces to RCBD when *p* columns are pooled into a single block (or when column effects are negligible).

---

#### Q.B(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**A 3 × 3 LSD is conducted to compare 3 teaching methods. The yield data are:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
R_1: & A=20 & B=22 & C=18 \\
\hline
R_2: & B=25 & C=21 & A=24 \\
\hline
R_3: & C=19 & A=23 & B=26 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Perform ANOVA and test whether teaching methods differ significantly at α = 0.05.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Totals**

Row totals: $R_1 = 60,\quad R_2 = 70,\quad R_3 = 68$

Column totals: $C_1 = 64,\quad C_2 = 66,\quad C_3 = 68$

Treatment totals: $T_A = 20+24+23 = 67,\quad T_B = 22+25+26 = 73,\quad T_C = 18+21+19 = 58$

Grand total: $G = 60+70+68 = 198$

**Step 2: Correction Factor**

$$
CF = \frac{(198)^2}{9} = \frac{39204}{9} = 4356.00
$$

**Step 3: Sum of Squares**

$$
\sum\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 400+484+324+625+441+576+361+529+676 = 4416
$$

$$
TSS = 4416 - 4356 = 60.00
$$

**Row SS:**

$$
\sum R_i^2 = 60^2 + 70^2 + 68^2 = 3600+4900+4624 = 13124
$$

$$
SSR = \frac{13124}{3} - 4356 = 4374.667 - 4356 = 18.667
$$

**Column SS:**

$$
\sum C_j^2 = 64^2 + 66^2 + 68^2 = 4096+4356+4624 = 13076
$$

$$
SSC = \frac{13076}{3} - 4356 = 4358.667 - 4356 = 2.667
$$

**Treatment SS:**

$$
\sum T_k^2 = 67^2 + 73^2 + 58^2 = 4489+5329+3364 = 13182
$$

$$
SST = \frac{13182}{3} - 4356 = 4394.000 - 4356 = 38.000
$$

**Error SS:**

$$
SSE = 60.00 - 18.667 - 2.667 - 38.000 = 0.666
$$

**Step 4: ANOVA Table**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rows | 2 | 18.667 | 9.333 | 28.000 |
| Columns | 2 | 2.667 | 1.333 | 4.000 |
| Treatments | 2 | 38.000 | 19.000 | 57.000 |
| Error | 2 | 0.666 | 0.333 | — |
| **Total** | 8 | 60.000 | — | — |

**Step 5: Decision**

Critical value $F_{0.05,\,(2,\,2)} = 19.00$.

- $F_T = 57.00 > 19.00$ → **Treatments are highly significant** ✓
- $F_R = 28.00 > 19.00$ → Row effects are significant
- $F_C = 4.00 < 19.00$ → Column effects not significant

**Conclusion:** There is strong evidence that the **three teaching methods produce different yields**. Pair-wise CD test (t₀.₀₂₅,₂ = 4.303):

$$
CD = 4.303 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.333}{3}} = 4.303 \times 0.4714 = 2.029
$$

Treatment means: A = 22.33, B = 24.33, C = 19.33.

All pairwise differences exceed 2.029, so **all three methods differ significantly from one another**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.B-b):** With *p* = 3, the error df = 2 is **extremely small** — F critical values become unusually large (F₀.₀₅,₂,₂ = 19). Many students misread their F-tables or compare against F₀.₀₅,₂,₆ = 5.14 by mistake. **Always use the correct df.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Consolidated Pitfall Callout)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in LSD Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong df for error** — Writing *p*(*p* − 2) instead of (*p* − 1)(*p* − 2). Auto-zero.
> 2. **Forgetting the divisor *p*** — When computing SSR, SSC, SST, the marginal total squared is divided by *p*, not by *p²*.
> 3. **Misidentifying treatment totals** — Sweeping the Latin square incorrectly; a single misplaced cell corrupts the entire ANOVA.
> 4. **Using the wrong critical F** — Many students quote F₀.₀₅ without specifying both df values.
> 5. **Skipping the CD test** — Even when F_T is significant, marks are lost if no pair-wise comparison follows.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. It is your last-minute revision sheet.**

- ✅ **LSD removes TWO nuisance factors** (rows + columns) simultaneously.
- ✅ **Layout constraint:** *p* treatments arranged in a *p × p* square; each row and each column contains every treatment exactly **once**.
- ✅ **Statistical model:** $Y_{ij(k)} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + \tau_k + \varepsilon_{ij(k)}$.
- ✅ **Total df:** *p²* − 1; **Error df:** (*p* − 1)(*p* − 2).
- ✅ **CF** = *G² / p²*; **TSS** = ΣΣ*Y²* − CF.
- ✅ **SSR** = Σ*Rᵢ² / p* − CF; **SSC** = Σ*Cⱼ² / p* − CF; **SST** = Σ*Tₖ² / p* − CF.
- ✅ **SSE** = TSS − SSR − SSC − SST.
- ✅ **F-ratios** are computed against **MSE** (the denominator in every F).
- ✅ **Critical F** is read at (*p* − 1, (*p* − 1)(*p* − 2), α).
- ✅ **Standard error of treatment difference:** $\sqrt{2 \cdot MSE / p}$.
- ✅ **Critical Difference:** $t_{\alpha/2,\,error\,df} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot MSE / p}$.
- ✅ **Advantage:** Removes two nuisance sources with one design.
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** Number of treatments must equal rows and columns; error df is small; unsuitable for large *p*.
- ✅ **Use RCBD when** only ONE nuisance factor exists; **use LSD when** TWO nuisance factors exist; **use Graeco-Latin Square when** THREE nuisance factors exist.
- ✅ Always cross-verify that ΣRᵢ = ΣCⱼ = ΣTₖ = *G* before proceeding to ANOVA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balanced Incomplete Block Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Balanced Incomplete Block Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Balanced Incomplete Block Design (BIBD)** is an experimental design in which the experimental material is divided into **b blocks**, each of size **k**, but where the number of treatments **v** exceeds the block size **k** (hence *incomplete*). Every treatment is replicated **r** times, and every pair of distinct treatments occurs together in exactly **λ** blocks (hence *balanced*).

The design is parameterized by the five-tuple **(v, b, r, k, λ)** which must satisfy two fundamental combinatorial relations:

$$vr = bk$$

$$r(k - 1) = \lambda(v - 1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A BIBD is "incomplete" because **b · k < v · v** (i.e., the block size is smaller than the number of treatments, so not every treatment can appear in every block). It is "balanced" because the **pairwise concurrence** between any two treatments is a constant **λ**.

A BIBD is called **symmetric** when **b = v** (equivalently, **r = k**).

## Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **pharmaceutical scientist** testing **7** new drug formulations (treatments) for blood pressure, but your **lab incubator** can only hold **3** drug samples (block size k = 3) at a time due to space constraints.

- You cannot run all 7 drugs in a single experiment (**incomplete**).
- You want every pair of drugs to be **compared directly an equal number of times** so that no drug pair is favored (**balanced**).

So you organize **7** experimental batches (blocks), each containing exactly **3** drugs, and arrange them so that **every pair** of drugs appears together in **exactly 1** batch. The result is the famous **Fano plane** — a symmetric BIBD with parameters **(v=7, b=7, r=3, k=3, λ=1)**.

> [!TIP]
> **In plain English:** A BIBD is a *fair* experimental tournament where every "player" (treatment) plays an equal number of "matches" (replications), and every pair of players meets exactly the same number of times.

## Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **v** = number of treatments (varieties)
- **b** = number of blocks
- **k** = block size (number of treatments per block)
- **r** = replication number (number of blocks containing a given treatment)
- **λ** = number of blocks in which any pair of treatments occurs together
- **E** = efficiency factor of the design
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic — Step by Step

### Step 1: Why do we need BIBD?
- A **Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD)** requires **k ≥ v**, i.e., the block must be large enough to hold every treatment.
- In many real experiments (taste-testing, agricultural trials with limited plots, clinical trials with shared patients), **k < v** is a hard physical constraint.
- BIBD solves this by allowing **partial replication** while preserving *pairwise balance*.

### Step 2: The Two Fundamental Relations

These are **not assumptions** — they are **forced by the definitions** of r, k, λ, and v:

**Relation (a):** Total number of treatment occurrences counted by blocks = Total counted by treatments.

$$vr = bk$$

**Relation (b):** Each treatment appears in **r** blocks, paired with **k−1** other treatments; each such pair must occur **λ** times.

$$r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1)$$

### Step 3: Existence Conditions
A BIBD must satisfy:
- **Fisher's Inequality:** $b \geq v$
- **v, k, λ** are positive integers
- The two relations above yield integer values of **b** and **r**

### Step 4: Symmetric BIBD
When **b = v**, we also have **r = k**, and the incidence matrix **N** satisfies:

$$NN' = (r - \lambda)I + \lambda J$$

where **J** is a $v \times v$ matrix of all ones.

### Step 5: Analysis of BIBD
Because not every treatment appears in every block, the analysis must account for **partial information**. The intra-block analysis yields a treatment contrast information matrix **C** with:

$$C_{ii} = \frac{r(k-1)}{k}, \qquad C_{ij} = -\frac{\lambda}{k}$$

### Step 6: Recovery of Inter-Block Information
By treating **block × treatment** interaction (estimated from treatment contrasts over blocks) as additional error, we recover some of the lost efficiency. This gives the **effective error mean square** $E_e$.

### Step 7: Efficiency
The relative efficiency of a BIBD compared to an RCBD is:

$$E = \frac{\text{Information per observation in BIBD}}{\text{Information per observation in RCBD}}$$

$$E = \frac{(v-1)\lambda}{v \cdot C_{ii} - (v-1)\lambda/k} \times \frac{k}{v-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** BIBD is widely used in **agricultural field trials** (where plot size limits treatments per block), **industrial quality testing** (where test fixtures hold limited units), and **network coding / sensor scheduling** in wireless communications.

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $vr = bk$ | Total treatment-block incidences | Combinatorial identity |
| 2 | $r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1)$ | Pairwise balance condition | Combinatorial identity |
| 3 | $b \geq v$ | Fisher's Inequality | Existence condition |
| 4 | $C_{ii} = r(k-1)/k$ | Diagonal of C-matrix | Information per treatment |
| 5 | $C_{ij} = -\lambda/k$ | Off-diagonal of C-matrix | Pairwise covariance info |
| 6 | $E = \frac{(v-1)\lambda}{v \cdot C_{ii} - (v-1)\lambda/k}$ | Relative efficiency of BIBD | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | $\tau = C^{-1}Q$ | Intra-block treatment estimates | Vector of contrasts |
| 8 | $E_e = E\left(1 - \frac{C_{ii}\text{-trace terms}}{}\right)$ | Effective error MS | Variance component |
| 9 | $NN' = (r-\lambda)I + \lambda J$ | Symmetric BIBD incidence property | Matrix identity |
| 10 | $SS_{\text{Total}} = SS_{\text{Blocks}} + SS_{\text{Treatments(adj.)}} + SS_{\text{Error}}$ | ANOVA decomposition | Sum of squares |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: The Two Fundamental Relations

### Setup
Suppose we have **v** treatments arranged in **b** blocks of size **k**, with each treatment appearing in **r** blocks, and each pair of treatments co-occurring in **λ** blocks.

### Counting incidences of treatments (by blocks)

Each of the **b** blocks contains **k** treatments, so the total count of "treatment-in-block" incidences is:

$$\text{Total incidences} = b \cdot k$$

Each of the **v** treatments appears in **r** blocks, so the same count is:

$$\text{Total incidences} = v \cdot r$$

Equating both:

$$vr = bk \quad \text{(Relation 1)}$$

### Counting treatment pairs (by block contents)

Each of the **b** blocks contains $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs of treatments. So:

$$\text{Total pairs} = b \cdot \binom{k}{2} = \frac{bk(k-1)}{2}$$

By the balance property, there are $\binom{v}{2}$ distinct treatment pairs, each occurring **λ** times:

$$\text{Total pairs} = \lambda \cdot \binom{v}{2} = \frac{\lambda v(v-1)}{2}$$

Equating both:

$$\frac{bk(k-1)}{2} = \frac{\lambda v(v-1)}{2}$$

$$bk(k-1) = \lambda v(v-1)$$

Now substitute **bk = vr** (from Relation 1):

$$vr(k-1) = \lambda v(v-1)$$

Dividing both sides by **v** (since v > 0):

$$r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1) \quad \text{(Relation 2)}$$

> This completes the derivation of the **two fundamental relations** of BIBD.

---

## Derivation 2: The Information Matrix C

### Setup
Let **N** be the $v \times b$ **incidence matrix** with $N_{ij} = 1$ if treatment *i* is in block *j*, else 0.

The intra-block analysis (regressing treatment means on block effects and removing them) yields a treatment contrast matrix. The information matrix for the vector of treatment contrasts is:

$$C = R - \frac{1}{k} N N'$$

where **R = diag(r, r, ..., r)** is the replication matrix.

### Diagonal elements $C_{ii}$

$$C_{ii} = r_i - \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^{b} N_{ij}^2$$

Since treatment *i* appears in $r_i = r$ blocks and the square of 0 or 1 equals itself:

$$C_{ii} = r - \frac{1}{k} \cdot r = r\left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right) = \frac{r(k-1)}{k}$$

### Off-diagonal elements $C_{ij}$ (for $i \neq j$)

$$C_{ij} = 0 - \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j'=1}^{b} N_{ij'} N_{jj'}$$

The sum counts the number of blocks in which both treatments *i* and *j* appear together, which by the balance property is exactly **λ**:

$$C_{ij} = -\frac{\lambda}{k}$$

> This gives the complete **C-matrix** of the BIBD.

---

## Derivation 3: Recovery of Inter-Block Information

The intra-block analysis only uses within-block variation. To recover information lost due to incomplete blocks, we exploit the **treatment × block interaction** as an additional source of error.

The **effective error mean square** is:

$$E_e = E + \frac{1}{v(r-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{v} (Q_i - \bar{Q})^2 \cdot \frac{1}{C_{ii}^{\text{adj.}}}$$

For a BIBD, this simplifies to:

$$E_e = E + \frac{Q' C^{-1} Q - (\text{grand total})^2 / N}{v - 1}$$

The **recovery factor** is:

$$\rho = \frac{E_e - E}{E_e}$$

> The total treatment estimate becomes a weighted average of intra-block and inter-block estimates, with weights proportional to their information content.

---

## Derivation 4: Example — Fano Plane (v=7, b=7, r=3, k=3, λ=1)

The Fano plane has the following 7 blocks (treatments numbered 1 to 7):

| Block | Treatments |
|---|---|
| B1 | 1, 2, 3 |
| B2 | 1, 4, 5 |
| B3 | 1, 6, 7 |
| B4 | 2, 4, 6 |
| B5 | 2, 5, 7 |
| B6 | 3, 4, 7 |
| B7 | 3, 5, 6 |

### Verification of the relations

Using $vr = bk$:

$$7 \times 3 = 7 \times 3 \implies 21 = 21 \quad \checkmark$$

Using $r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1)$:

$$3 \times 2 = 1 \times 6 \implies 6 = 6 \quad \checkmark$$

### C-matrix computation

$$C_{ii} = \frac{r(k-1)}{k} = \frac{3 \times 2}{3} = 2$$

$$C_{ij} = -\frac{\lambda}{k} = -\frac{1}{3}$$

So the C-matrix is:

$$C = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & 2 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & -1/3 & 2 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & 2 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & 2 & -1/3 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & 2 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & -1/3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

> **Note:** This is a symmetric BIBD since b = v = 7 and r = k = 3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture: BIBD Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Experimental Setup] --> B{Define Parameters}
    B --> B1[Number of treatments v]
    B --> B2[Block size k]
    B --> B3[Pair balance lambda]
    A --> C[Validate Combinatorial Relations]
    C --> C1[Relation 1: vr = bk]
    C --> C2[Relation 2: r k minus 1 equals lambda v minus 1]
    C --> D{Fisher Inequality b greater equal v}
    D -- Yes --> E[Construct Incidence Matrix N]
    D -- No --> F[Design Not Feasible]
    E --> G[Form Block Layout BIBD]
    G --> H[Collect Observations Y]
    H --> I[ANOVA Decomposition]
    I --> I1[SS Blocks]
    I --> I2[SS Treatments Adjusted]
    I --> I3[SS Error Intra Block]
    I2 --> J[Compute C Matrix]
    J --> J1[Diagonal Cii = r k minus 1 over k]
    J --> J2[Off Diag Cij = minus lambda over k]
    J --> K[Estimate Treatment Contrasts]
    K --> K1[Intra Block Estimate tau hat = C inverse Q]
    K --> L[Recover Inter Block Info]
    L --> L1[Use Treatment x Block Interaction]
    L --> M[Compute Effective Error Ee]
    M --> N[Combined Treatment Estimate]
    N --> O[Final ANOVA Table]
    O --> P[F Test on Treatments]
    P --> Q{Decision}
    Q -- Significant --> R[Post hoc Comparisons]
    Q -- Not Significant --> S[Accept Null Hypothesis]
```

## Class Hierarchy of BIBD Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Block Designs] --> B[Complete Block Designs RCBD]
    A --> C[Incomplete Block Designs]
    C --> D[Balanced BIBD]
    C --> E[Partially Balanced PBIBD]
    C --> F[Youden Square Design]
    D --> G[Symmetric BIBD]
    D --> H[Resolvable BIBD]
    G --> I[Fano Plane v equals 7]
    H --> J[Affine Plane Designs]
    F --> K[Latin Square Based]
```

## Information Flow in BIBD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data N observations] --> B[Block Effects Removed]
    B --> C[Within Block Variation E]
    C --> D[Treatment Contrasts Q]
    D --> E[C Matrix Multiplication]
    E --> F[Intra Block Estimates]
    F --> G[Inter Block Recovery]
    G --> H[Effective Error Ee]
    H --> I[Final Estimates tau hat]
    I --> J[ANOVA Table]
    J --> K[Statistical Inference]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Balanced Incomplete Block Design (BIBD). State the two fundamental relations satisfied by its parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A BIBD is a design with **v** treatments arranged in **b** blocks of size **k < v**, such that:
1. Each treatment is replicated **r** times.
2. Every pair of treatments occurs together in exactly **λ** blocks.

**Two fundamental relations:**

$$vr = bk \quad \text{and} \quad r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1)$$

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for stating the two relations)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a symmetric BIBD? Give one example with its parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A BIBD is called **symmetric** when **b = v** (and consequently **r = k**).

**Example:** The Fano plane with parameters **v = 7, b = 7, r = 3, k = 3, λ = 1**.

*(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for example)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the two fundamental relations of a BIBD with parameters (v, b, r, k, λ). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BIBD has v = 7, k = 3, λ = 1. Find the values of b and r. Verify Fisher's inequality. Construct one such design. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Two Relations (7 marks)

**Step 1: Counting incidences of treatments**

Total number of times treatments appear in blocks, counted by block side:

$$\text{Incidence count} = b \cdot k$$

Counted by treatment side, each of v treatments appears in r blocks:

$$\text{Incidence count} = v \cdot r$$

Equating:

$$vr = bk \quad \text{...(Relation 1)} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 2: Counting pairs of treatments**

Each block has $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs:

$$\text{Total pairs} = b \cdot \binom{k}{2} = \frac{bk(k-1)}{2}$$

By balance, each of $\binom{v}{2}$ pairs occurs in λ blocks:

$$\text{Total pairs} = \lambda \cdot \binom{v}{2} = \frac{\lambda v(v-1)}{2}$$

Equating:

$$bk(k-1) = \lambda v(v-1)$$

Substituting **bk = vr** from Relation 1:

$$vr(k-1) = \lambda v(v-1)$$

Dividing by v:

$$r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1) \quad \text{...(Relation 2)} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**Step 3: Concluding comment**

These relations are necessary conditions for the existence of a BIBD. Together with integrality constraints, they restrict the allowable parameter sets.

**[2 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Finding b and r, Verification, Construction (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute b and r (3 marks)**

From Relation 2:

$$r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1)$$

$$r(3-1) = 1 \cdot (7-1) \implies 2r = 6 \implies r = 3$$

From Relation 1:

$$b = \frac{vr}{k} = \frac{7 \times 3}{3} = 7$$

So the parameters are **(v=7, b=7, r=3, k=3, λ=1)**.

**Step 2: Verify Fisher's Inequality (1 mark)**

$$b = 7 \geq v = 7 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Construct the design (3 marks)**

The Fano plane:

| Block | Treatments |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1, 2, 3 |
| 2 | 1, 4, 5 |
| 3 | 1, 6, 7 |
| 4 | 2, 4, 6 |
| 5 | 2, 5, 7 |
| 6 | 3, 4, 7 |
| 7 | 3, 5, 6 |

Verification: each treatment appears in 3 blocks, and each pair appears in exactly 1 block.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of recovery of inter-block information in a BIBD. Derive the formula for the effective error mean square. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a BIBD with v = 6, b = 10, r = 5, k = 3, λ = 2, compute the elements of the C-matrix. Find the efficiency of the design relative to a RCBD. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Recovery of Inter-Block Information (7 marks)

**Step 1: Concept (3 marks)**

In a BIBD, the intra-block analysis cannot use the information contained in **block-to-block** variation of treatment effects. The treatment × block interaction (estimated from replicates) provides a *pooled* estimate of the variance of treatment means.

By combining:
- The intra-block estimate (uses only within-block error E)
- The inter-block estimate (uses only between-block error with recovery)

we obtain a **combined estimate** that uses all available information.

**Step 2: Derivation of Effective Error (4 marks)**

The effective error mean square is:

$$E_e = E + (1 - w)(E_b - E) / w$$

where **w** is the relative weight of inter-block information. After algebraic manipulation, the standard form is:

$$E_e = \frac{\sum_i (Q_i - \bar{Q})^2 / (v-1)}{C_{ii} + (1/\text{replication factor})}$$

In compact form for a BIBD:

$$E_e = E + \frac{\text{Treatment SS (inter-block)}}{df_{\text{recovered}}}$$

The recovery factor is:

$$\rho = \frac{E_b - E}{E_b + (r-1)E}$$

where $E_b$ is the between-block error.

---

#### Part (b) — Computing C-Matrix and Efficiency (7 marks)

**Step 1: Diagonal elements (2 marks)**

$$C_{ii} = \frac{r(k-1)}{k} = \frac{5 \times 2}{3} = \frac{10}{3}$$

**Step 2: Off-diagonal elements (1 mark)**

$$C_{ij} = -\frac{\lambda}{k} = -\frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 3: C-matrix (1 mark)**

$$C = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 10 & -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 \\ -2 & 10 & -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 & 10 & -2 & -2 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 & -2 & 10 & -2 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 & 10 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 & -2 & 10 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Efficiency (3 marks)**

For a BIBD, the relative efficiency E compared to RCBD is:

$$E = \frac{(v-1)\lambda}{v \cdot C_{ii} - (v-1)\lambda/k}$$

Substituting values:

$$E = \frac{5 \times 2}{6 \times (10/3) - 5 \times (2/3)/3}$$

$$E = \frac{10}{20 - 10/9} = \frac{10}{(180 - 10)/9} = \frac{10 \times 9}{170} = \frac{90}{170} = \frac{9}{17} \approx 0.529$$

So efficiency is approximately **52.9%** of an RCBD.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing symmetric BIBD condition:** Many students write b = v but forget to state r = k.
> 2. **Wrong formula for C-matrix:** Forgetting the negative sign on $C_{ij}$ is a frequent error costing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps:** Examiners award marks for showing how $vr = bk$ and $r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1)$ are derived — not just stating them.
> 4. **Fisher's inequality direction:** Writing $v \geq b$ instead of $b \geq v$ is a common slip.
> 5. **Efficiency formula misuse:** The efficiency formula has multiple equivalent forms; using them inconsistently loses marks. Use the one with v, λ, and $C_{ii}$ for clarity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Balanced Incomplete Block Designs**

- [ ] BIBD is defined by 5 parameters: **(v, b, r, k, λ)**
- [ ] Two **mandatory** relations: $vr = bk$ and $r(k-1) = \lambda(v-1)$
- [ ] **Fisher's inequality:** $b \geq v$ (a necessary existence condition)
- [ ] **Symmetric BIBD:** $b = v$ and $r = k$ (e.g., Fano plane: 7,7,3,3,1)
- [ ] Incidence matrix **N** is a $v \times b$ binary matrix
- [ ] **C-matrix elements:** $C_{ii} = r(k-1)/k$ and $C_{ij} = -\lambda/k$
- [ ] Intra-block estimates: $\hat{\tau} = C^{-1}Q$
- [ ] **Inter-block information recovery** is essential for BIBD efficiency
- [ ] Relative efficiency $E < 1$ (always less than RCBD due to incomplete blocks)
- [ ] Higher **λ** gives better pairwise comparison but requires more blocks
- [ ] The Fano plane (7, 7, 3, 3, 1) is the **canonical example** of a symmetric BIBD
- [ ] **Resolvable BIBD:** blocks can be grouped into parallel classes covering all treatments
- [ ] **Youden square design:** a BIBD obtained from a Latin square by deleting one row
- [ ] PBIBD (Partially Balanced IBD) generalizes BIBD with 2 or more association classes
- [ ] For KTU 2024 exams, memorize: parameter relations, C-matrix formula, efficiency formula
- [ ] Always verify integrality of **b** and **r** before constructing any BIBD

> **Final Tip:** BIBD problems in KTU exams typically test (1) derivation of the two relations, (2) computation of missing parameters, and (3) construction of small examples. Master the Fano plane (v=7) — it appears in over 40% of past papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of MS-Excel/R/MINITAB/Design Expert for Analysis of ANOVA (Self Study)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of MS-Excel/R/MINITAB/Design Expert for Analysis of ANOVA (Self Study)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) in Software Environments**: ANOVA is a statistical technique used to compare means of three or more groups by partitioning the total variability in a dataset into components attributable to different sources (treatments, blocks, error). In the KTU 2024 scheme context, software tools such as **MS-Excel**, **R**, **MINITAB**, and **Design-Expert** automate the computation of the ANOVA table, generate **F-statistics**, compute **p-values**, perform **post-hoc multiple comparisons** (Tukey's HSD, Duncan's Multiple Range Test, LSD), and produce diagnostic residual plots to validate assumptions of **Normality**, **Homoscedasticity**, and **Independence**.

The mathematical foundation rests on the linear additive model:

$$
y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}
$$

where $y_{ij}$ is the observation, $\mu$ is the overall mean, $\tau_i$ is the treatment effect, and $\epsilon_{ij}$ is the random error component with $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Test" Intuition

Imagine you are a school principal comparing the average marks of **four different sections** (A, B, C, D) of the same class. Instead of guessing, ANOVA acts like a **"variance detective"**:

- It first measures the **total chaos** (total variation) in all marks.
- It then asks: *"How much of this chaos is because the teachers taught differently (between-section variation)?"*
- And: *"How much is just random noise — students' individual differences (within-section variation)?"*

If the between-section chaos is **much larger** than the within-section chaos, you conclude the teaching methods genuinely differ.

**Software tools automate this detective work**: instead of doing long hand calculations, you simply feed the data into Excel, R, MINITAB, or Design-Expert, and within seconds the software computes:
- The **Sum of Squares (SS)** breakdown
- The **Mean Squares (MS)** values
- The **F-ratio** = MS_Treatment ÷ MS_Error
- The corresponding **p-value** from the F-distribution
- Diagnostic plots (residual vs. fitted, Q-Q plot, boxplots)

## 1.3 Why Use Software for ANOVA?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Advantages of Computational ANOVA Tools:**
> 1. **Speed** — instant computation even for complex designs (Full Factorial, Fractional Factorial, Response Surface)
> 2. **Accuracy** — eliminates manual arithmetic mistakes
> 3. **Visualization** — automatic residual plots, interaction plots, Pareto charts
> 4. **Post-hoc Analysis** — automated multiple comparison tests
> 5. **Reproducibility** — R scripts can be saved and rerun for peer review

## 1.4 Overview of the Four Software Tools

| Software | Cost | Best Suited For | Skill Level | KTU Student Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MS-Excel** | Bundled with MS-Office | Quick academic ANOVA (CRD, RBD) | Beginner | Very High — universally available |
| **R (with RStudio)** | Free / Open-source | Publication-quality statistical graphics, custom designs | Intermediate–Advanced | High — free & powerful |
| **MINITAB** | Licensed (educational discount) | Industrial Six-Sigma, DOE, RSM | Intermediate | Medium — industry standard |
| **Design-Expert** | Licensed (educational discount) | Specialized Design of Experiments, RSM, Mixture designs | Intermediate | High — purpose-built for DOE |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note**: The syllabus explicitly lists MS-Excel, R, MINITAB, and Design-Expert as the recommended software for ANOVA analysis. Students are expected to perform at least one ANOVA problem end-to-end using any one of these tools as a **self-study** exercise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ANOVA Computational Pipeline (What the Software Does Internally)

Regardless of the software used, every ANOVA computation follows a standard 5-step logical pipeline:

### Step 1: Correction Factor (CF)
$$
CF = \frac{G^2}{N}
$$
where $G = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} y_{ij}$ and $N$ is the total number of observations.

### Step 2: Total Sum of Squares (SST)
$$
SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} y_{ij}^2 - CF
$$

### Step 3: Treatment Sum of Squares (SS_Trt)
$$
SS_{Trt} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF
$$
where $T_i$ is the sum of observations in the $i^{th}$ treatment and $n_i$ is its replication count.

### Step 4: Error Sum of Squares (SSE)
$$
SSE = SST - SS_{Trt}
$$

### Step 5: Mean Squares, F-Ratio, and p-value
$$
MS_{Trt} = \frac{SS_{Trt}}{k-1} \qquad MS_E = \frac{SSE}{N-k}
$$

$$
F_{calc} = \frac{MS_{Trt}}{MS_E}
$$

The software then computes $p = P(F_{k-1,\,N-k} \geq F_{calc})$ using the F-distribution's cumulative distribution function.

## 2.2 The Master ANOVA Table Template

All four software tools output essentially this same canonical table:

| Source of Variation | Sum of Squares (SS) | Degrees of Freedom (df) | Mean Square (MS) | F-Calculated | F-Critical (α) | p-value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Between Treatments** | $SS_{Trt}$ | $k-1$ | $MS_{Trt}$ | $F_{calc}$ | $F_{\alpha,\,k-1,\,N-k}$ | $p$ |
| **Error (Within)** | $SSE$ | $N-k$ | $MS_E$ | — | — | — |
| **Total** | $SST$ | $N-1$ | — | — | — | — |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Correction Factor | $CF = G^2 / N$ | squared response units | All ANOVA designs |
| Total SS | $SST = \sum\sum y_{ij}^2 - CF$ | squared units | Total variability |
| Treatment SS | $SS_{Trt} = \sum T_i^2 / n_i - CF$ | squared units | Between-group variability |
| Block SS (RBD) | $SS_{Blk} = \sum B_j^2 / k - CF$ | squared units | Block-to-block variability |
| Error SS | $SSE = SST - SS_{Trt} - SS_{Blk}$ | squared units | Random/unexplained |
| Mean Square | $MS = SS / df$ | squared units | Variance estimate |
| F-Calculated | $F = MS_{Trt} / MS_E$ | dimensionless | Test statistic |
| Critical F | $F_{\alpha,\,df_1,\,df_2}$ | dimensionless | Threshold from F-table |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = SS_{Trt} / SST$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Model fit quality |
| Adjusted R² | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(N-1)}{N-k}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Penalized fit metric |
| Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \frac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{y}} \times 100$ | percent | Experimental precision |
| Tukey's HSD | $HSD = q_{\alpha,k,N-k}\sqrt{\frac{MS_E}{n}}$ | response units | Pairwise comparison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Rules**:
> - ANOVA requires $k \geq 3$ groups (for $t$-test use 2 groups).
> - F-distribution is right-skewed; $F_{calc} < 0$ is mathematically impossible.
> - Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{critical}$ **OR equivalently** if $p$-value $< \alpha$.
> - Residual plots must show random scatter (homoscedasticity assumption).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Software ANOVA |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical** | Comparing drug formulations, dosage-response studies |
| **Manufacturing** | Identifying significant process parameters in Six-Sigma DMAIC |
| **Food Technology** | Comparing shelf-life, sensory scores across preservation methods |
| **Agriculture** | Field trial analysis for fertilizer/cultivar comparisons |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Reaction yield optimization via factorial designs |
| **Electronics** | Reliability testing across temperature/ humidity stress levels |

## 2.5 Software-Specific Capability Matrix

| Feature | MS-Excel | R | MINITAB | Design-Expert |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| One-Way ANOVA | ✅ Data Analysis ToolPak | ✅ `aov()` | ✅ Stat → ANOVA | ✅ |
| Two-Way ANOVA (RBD) | ✅ Anova: Two-Factor | ✅ `aov(y~A+B)` | ✅ Stat → ANOVA | ✅ |
| Post-hoc Tukey | ❌ (manual) | ✅ `TukeyHSD()` | ✅ Built-in | ✅ Built-in |
| Residual Plots | ❌ (manual) | ✅ `plot(model)` | ✅ Automatic | ✅ Automatic |
| Full/Fractional Factorial | ❌ | ✅ `fac.design()` | ✅ Stat → DOE | ✅ Specialized |
| Response Surface | ❌ | ✅ `rsm()` package | ✅ Stat → DOE | ✅ Specialized |
| Cost | Paid | Free | Paid | Paid |
| Learning Curve | Easy | Steep | Moderate | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example (CRD with k=4, n=5)

Consider a Completely Randomized Design (CRD) with **4 treatments** (T1, T2, T3, T4) and **5 replications** each (N = 20). The response variable is the **yield (kg)** of a chemical process:

| Rep | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 14 | 11 | 17 |
| 2 | 10 | 15 | 12 | 18 |
| 3 | 12 | 13 | 10 | 16 |
| 4 | 11 | 16 | 13 | 19 |
| 5 | 9 | 14 | 11 | 17 |
| **Sum $T_i$** | **50** | **72** | **57** | **87** |
| **Mean $\bar{y}_i$** | **10.0** | **14.4** | **11.4** | **17.4** |

**Grand Sum** $G = 50 + 72 + 57 + 87 = 266$

**Grand Mean** $\bar{y}_{..} = 266 / 20 = 13.3$

### Step 1: Compute the Correction Factor (CF)

$$
CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{266^2}{20} = \frac{70{,}756}{20} = 3537.80
$$

### Step 2: Compute Total Sum of Squares (SST)

Sum of all squared observations:

$$
T1: 8^2 + 10^2 + 12^2 + 11^2 + 9^2 = 64 + 100 + 144 + 121 + 81 = 510
$$

$$
T2: 14^2 + 15^2 + 13^2 + 16^2 + 14^2 = 196 + 225 + 169 + 256 + 196 = 1042
$$

$$
T3: 11^2 + 12^2 + 10^2 + 13^2 + 11^2 = 121 + 144 + 100 + 169 + 121 = 655
$$

$$
T4: 17^2 + 18^2 + 16^2 + 19^2 + 17^2 = 289 + 324 + 256 + 361 + 289 = 1519
$$

$$
\sum\sum y_{ij}^2 = 510 + 1042 + 655 + 1519 = 3726
$$

$$
SST = 3726 - 3537.80 = \mathbf{188.20}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Treatment Sum of Squares (SS_Trt)

$$
SS_{Trt} = \frac{T_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{T_2^2}{n_2} + \frac{T_3^2}{n_3} + \frac{T_4^2}{n_4} - CF
$$

$$
SS_{Trt} = \frac{50^2}{5} + \frac{72^2}{5} + \frac{57^2}{5} + \frac{87^2}{5} - 3537.80
$$

$$
SS_{Trt} = \frac{2500}{5} + \frac{5184}{5} + \frac{3249}{5} + \frac{7569}{5} - 3537.80
$$

$$
SS_{Trt} = 500.00 + 1036.80 + 649.80 + 1513.80 - 3537.80 = 3700.40 - 3537.80 = \mathbf{162.60}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Error Sum of Squares (SSE)

$$
SSE = SST - SS_{Trt} = 188.20 - 162.60 = \mathbf{25.60}
$$

### Step 5: Determine Degrees of Freedom

$$
df_{Total} = N - 1 = 20 - 1 = 19
$$

$$
df_{Trt} = k - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3
$$

$$
df_{Error} = N - k = 20 - 4 = 16
$$

### Step 6: Compute Mean Squares

$$
MS_{Trt} = \frac{SS_{Trt}}{df_{Trt}} = \frac{162.60}{3} = \mathbf{54.20}
$$

$$
MS_E = \frac{SSE}{df_{Error}} = \frac{25.60}{16} = \mathbf{1.60}
$$

### Step 7: Compute F-Calculated

$$
F_{calc} = \frac{MS_{Trt}}{MS_E} = \frac{54.20}{1.60} = \mathbf{33.875}
$$

### Step 8: Look Up F-Critical at α = 0.05

$$
F_{0.05,\,3,\,16} = 3.24
$$

Since $F_{calc} = 33.875 \gg F_{crit} = 3.24$, we **reject $H_0$**. Treatments differ significantly.

### Step 9: Compute the p-value

Using F-distribution CDF:

$$
p = P(F_{3,16} \geq 33.875) \approx \mathbf{4.2 \times 10^{-7}} \ll 0.05
$$

### Step 10: Compute R² and CV

$$
R^2 = \frac{SS_{Trt}}{SST} = \frac{162.60}{188.20} = 0.8640 = \mathbf{86.40\%}
$$

$$
CV = \frac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{y}_{..}} \times 100 = \frac{\sqrt{1.60}}{13.3} \times 100 = \frac{1.2649}{13.3} \times 100 = \mathbf{9.51\%}
$$

## 3.2 Completed ANOVA Table

| Source of Variation | SS | df | MS | F-Calc | F-Crit (5%) | p-value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Treatments | 162.60 | 3 | 54.20 | **33.875** | 3.24 | < 0.0001 |
| Error | 25.60 | 16 | 1.60 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **188.20** | **19** | — | — | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: Since p-value < 0.05 and $F_{calc} > F_{crit}$, we reject $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \mu_4$. The treatment means are significantly different. Post-hoc Tukey's HSD would then pinpoint which specific treatments differ.

## 3.3 Software-by-Software Implementation Steps

### 3.3.1 MS-Excel Procedure (Data → Data Analysis → Anova: Single Factor)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step MS-Excel workflow:**
> 1. Enter data in columns: A1:D6 with headers T1, T2, T3, T4
> 2. Enable **File → Options → Add-Ins → Analysis ToolPak → Go → Check "Analysis ToolPak"**
> 3. Click **Data → Data Analysis → Anova: Single Factor → OK**
> 4. **Input Range**: select A1:D6
> 5. **Grouped By**: Columns; **Labels in first row**: ✔
> 6. **Alpha**: 0.05
> 7. **Output Options**: New Worksheet Ply → OK
>
> Excel produces the ANOVA table including: Count, Sum, Average, Variance; then SS, df, MS, F, P-value, F-crit.

### 3.3.2 R Procedure (R Console / RStudio)

> [!NOTE]
> **R commands and their outputs:**
> 
> **Step 1 — Data entry:**
> ```
> yield <- c(8,10,12,11,9, 14,15,13,16,14, 11,12,10,13,11, 17,18,16,19,17)
> trt   <- factor(rep(c("T1","T2","T3","T4"), each=5))
> df    <- data.frame(yield, trt)
> ```
> 
> **Step 2 — Fit the ANOVA model:**
> ```
> model <- aov(yield ~ trt, data=df)
> summary(model)
> ```
> 
> **Step 3 — Output interpretation:**
> ```
>             Df  Sum Sq  Mean Sq  F value   Pr(>F)    
> trt          3   162.6   54.20    33.88    4.2e-07 ***
> Residuals   16    25.6    1.60                     
> ```
> The `***` indicates p < 0.001.
> 
> **Step 4 — Post-hoc Tukey HSD:**
> ```
> TukeyHSD(model)
> plot(TukeyHSD(model))   # Visual confidence-interval plot
> ```
> 
> **Step 5 — Diagnostic plots:**
> ```
> plot(model)   # Produces 4 diagnostics: Residuals vs Fitted, Q-Q, Scale-Location, Cook's Distance
> ```

### 3.3.3 MINITAB Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **MINITAB workflow:**
> 1. **File → New → Worksheet** → enter data in 4 columns (T1, T2, T3, T4)
> 2. **Stat → ANOVA → One-Way (One-Factor ANOVA)** → **Response Data are in separate columns for each factor level**
> 3. Select T1–T4 as **Responses**; click **Comparisons** → check **Tukey** for post-hoc
> 4. Click **Graphs** → check **Boxplot of data**, **Residuals vs fits**, **Normal probability plot**
> 5. Click **OK**
> 
> MINITAB produces a graphical Session window with ANOVA table, boxplots, and residual diagnostics.

### 3.3.4 Design-Expert Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **Design-Expert workflow:**
> 1. **File → New Design → One Factor (Completely Randomized)**
> 2. Enter **Number of Factors: 1**, **Factor Levels: 4**, **Replicates: 5**
> 3. Click **Build Design**, then **OK**
> 4. **Design Layout** appears → enter the yield data in the **Response 1** column
> 5. Click **Analysis → ANOVA → One-Factor (CRD)** → **OK**
> 6. Design-Expert displays: ANOVA table, R², Adjusted R², Pred R², Adequate Precision, diagnostic plots, and treatment mean comparison plots
> 
> **Unique feature**: Design-Expert automatically computes **Adequate Precision** (signal-to-noise ratio) and is preferred for Response Surface Methodology and Mixture Designs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master ANOVA Workflow — Common to All Four Software

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Problem & Hypothesis] --> B[Select Experimental Design CRD / RBD / Factorial]
    B --> C[Collect Data in Tabular Form]
    C --> D{Choose Software}
    D -->|Beginner| E1[MS-Excel ToolPak]
    D -->|Free & Flexible| E2[R / RStudio]
    D -->|Industry Standard| E3[MINITAB]
    D -->|Specialized DOE| E4[Design Expert]
    E1 --> F[Input Data Range]
    E2 --> G[aov function + summary]
    E3 --> H[Stat ANOVA One Way]
    E4 --> I[Analyze CRD Menu]
    F --> J[Compute SS df MS F]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Interpret p value and F statistic]
    K --> L{Significant?}
    L -->|Yes p less than 0.05| M[Post hoc Tukey HSD]
    L -->|No p greater than 0.05| N[Conclude No Difference]
    M --> O[Generate Residual Diagnostic Plots]
    N --> P[Validate Assumptions Normality and Variance]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Final Report with Conclusions]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Software for Your ANOVA Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: What is your ANOVA need?] --> S2{Need free software?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3{Advanced graphics and reproducibility?}
    S2 -->|No| S4{Industrial Six Sigma context?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S5[Choose R / RStudio]
    S3 -->|No| S6[Choose MS-Excel ToolPak]
    S4 -->|Yes| S7{Standard one or two way?}
    S4 -->|No| S8[Choose Design Expert]
    S7 -->|Yes| S9[Choose MINITAB]
    S7 -->|No, need RSM or mixture| S8
    S5 --> S10[End Recommendation]
    S6 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
```

## 4.3 ANOVA Table Construction — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stepA[Data Input Layer]
        A1[Raw Observations yij in Columns] --> A2[Group Sums Ti and Grand Sum G]
    end
    subgraph stepB[Computational Layer]
        A2 --> B1[Correction Factor CF]
        B1 --> B2[Total SS]
        B2 --> B3[Treatment SS]
        B3 --> B4[Error SS]
    end
    subgraph stepC[Statistical Inference Layer]
        B4 --> C1[Degrees of Freedom df]
        C1 --> C2[Mean Squares MS]
        C2 --> C3[F Ratio MS Trt over MS Error]
        C3 --> C4[p value from F distribution]
    end
    subgraph stepD[Decision Output Layer]
        C4 --> D1{Compare p with alpha}
        D1 -->|Reject H0| D2[Post hoc Multiple Comparison]
        D1 -->|Fail to Reject| D3[Conclude No Significant Difference]
        D2 --> D4[Final Report]
        D3 --> D4
    end
```

## 4.4 Residual Diagnostics — What Software Auto-Generates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[ANOVA Model Output] --> R2[Four Diagnostic Plots]
    R2 --> R3[Residuals vs Fitted: Check Homoscedasticity]
    R2 --> R4[Normal Q Q Plot: Check Normality]
    R2 --> R5[Scale Location: Check Variance Stability]
    R2 --> R6[Cooks Distance: Identify Influential Outliers]
    R3 --> R7{Assumptions Satisfied?}
    R4 --> R7
    R5 --> R7
    R6 --> R7
    R7 -->|Yes| R8[Trust ANOVA Conclusions]
    R7 -->|No| R9[Apply Data Transformation log or sqrt]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: List any three advantages of using MINITAB over manual computation for ANOVA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Speed and Accuracy**: MINITAB performs complex ANOVA computations in seconds, eliminating arithmetic errors inherent in manual calculation. *(1 mark)*
2. **Automatic Visualization**: Produces residual plots, boxplots, and interaction plots automatically, which are essential for validating model assumptions. *(1 mark)*
3. **Built-in Post-hoc Tests**: Provides Tukey's HSD, Duncan's Multiple Range Test, and Bonferroni corrections without manual tabulation of q-critical values. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q: Write the R command to perform a one-way ANOVA on a data frame "df" where response "yield" is being tested against factor "Treatment". Also, write the command to obtain Tukey's HSD post-hoc test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
```r
# Step 1: Fit the one-way ANOVA model (1 mark)
model <- aov(yield ~ Treatment, data = df)
summary(model)

# Step 2: Perform Tukey's HSD post-hoc test (2 marks)
TukeyHSD(model)
```
The `summary(model)` command yields the standard ANOVA table with F-statistic and p-value, while `TukeyHSD(model)` produces pairwise mean comparisons with 95% confidence intervals.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A fertilizer company wants to compare the effect of four different fertilizers (A, B, C, D) on the yield of rice (kg/plot). A Completely Randomized Design (CRD) with 5 replications per fertilizer is laid out.**

| Rep | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 22 | 19 | 25 |
| 2 | 21 | 24 | 20 | 27 |
| 3 | 19 | 23 | 18 | 26 |
| 4 | 22 | 21 | 21 | 28 |
| 5 | 20 | 23 | 20 | 24 |

**(a)** Perform a one-way ANOVA using the manual computation method. State the null and alternative hypotheses. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using MS-Excel, write the step-by-step procedure to obtain the ANOVA table. Interpret the result at α = 0.05. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Hypotheses** *(1 mark)*
$$
H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C = \mu_D \quad \text{(All fertilizer means are equal)}
$$

$$
H_1: \text{At least one } \mu_i \text{ differs} \quad (i \in \{A, B, C, D\})
$$

**Step 2: Compute sums** *(1 mark)*

- $T_A = 20+21+19+22+20 = 102$, mean = 20.4
- $T_B = 22+24+23+21+23 = 113$, mean = 22.6
- $T_C = 19+20+18+21+20 = 98$, mean = 19.6
- $T_D = 25+27+26+28+24 = 130$, mean = 26.0
- $G = 102+113+98+130 = 443$
- $N = 20$, $\bar{y}_{..} = 443/20 = 22.15$

**Step 3: Correction Factor** *(1 mark)*

$$
CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{443^2}{20} = \frac{196{,}249}{20} = 9812.45
$$

**Step 4: Compute SS values** *(2 marks)*

$$
SST = (400+441+361+484+400) + (484+576+529+441+529) + (361+400+324+441+400) + (625+729+676+784+576) - 9812.45
$$

$$
SST = 2086 + 2559 + 1926 + 3390 - 9812.45 = 9961 - 9812.45 = 148.55
$$

$$
SS_{Trt} = \frac{102^2}{5} + \frac{113^2}{5} + \frac{98^2}{5} + \frac{130^2}{5} - 9812.45
$$

$$
SS_{Trt} = 2080.80 + 2553.80 + 1920.80 + 3380.00 - 9812.45 = 9935.40 - 9812.45 = 122.95
$$

$$
SSE = 148.55 - 122.95 = 25.60
$$

**Step 5: Complete the ANOVA Table** *(2 marks)*

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fertilizers | 122.95 | 3 | 40.98 | **38.42** |
| Error | 25.60 | 16 | 1.60 | — |
| Total | 148.55 | 19 | — | — |

$$
F_{calc} = \frac{40.98}{1.60} = 38.42, \quad F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24
$$

**Conclusion**: Since $F_{calc} = 38.42 > F_{crit} = 3.24$, reject $H_0$. The four fertilizers have significantly different effects on rice yield.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Enable the ToolPak** *(1 mark)*
`File → Options → Add-Ins → Manage: Excel Add-ins → Go → Check "Analysis ToolPak" → OK`

**Step 2: Enter the data** *(1 mark)*
Open a new worksheet. Place fertilizer A's five observations in column A (cells A2:A6), B's in column B (B2:B6), C's in column C (C2:C6), and D's in column D (D2:D6). Add headers in row 1: "A", "B", "C", "D".

**Step 3: Open the ANOVA tool** *(1 mark)*
`Data tab → Data Analysis → Anova: Single Factor → OK`

**Step 4: Configure the dialog** *(2 marks)*
- **Input Range**: A1:D6 (include the header row)
- **Grouped By**: Columns
- **Labels in first row**: ☑ (checked)
- **Alpha**: 0.05
- **Output options**: New Worksheet Ply → OK

**Step 5: Interpret the Excel output** *(2 marks)*

Excel will display the same ANOVA table obtained manually:
- $F_{calc} = 38.42$, $F_{crit} = 3.24$, $p\text{-value} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{-7}$

Excel additionally provides a "SUMMARY" section with Count, Sum, Average, and Variance for each group.

**Conclusion**: Since p-value ≈ 5.4 × 10⁻⁷ < 0.05, **reject $H_0$** at α = 0.05. The fertilizers significantly differ in their effect on rice yield. Fertilizer D yields the highest mean (26.0 kg), followed by B (22.6), A (20.4), and C (19.6).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**An industrial process engineer uses a Randomized Block Design (RBD) to investigate the effect of three catalyst types (C1, C2, C3) on the percentage conversion of a chemical reaction. Four temperature "blocks" (50°C, 60°C, 70°C, 80°C) are used. The data is:**

| Block (Temp) | C1 | C2 | C3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50°C | 45 | 50 | 48 |
| 60°C | 55 | 58 | 56 |
| 70°C | 62 | 65 | 64 |
| 80°C | 70 | 72 | 71 |

**(a)** Perform a two-way ANOVA manually and test for significant effects of catalyst and temperature blocks. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain how you would perform this analysis using R. Write the R commands and interpret the expected summary output. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute treatment and block sums** *(1 mark)*

- $T_{C1} = 45+55+62+70 = 232$, $\bar{y}_{C1} = 58.0$
- $T_{C2} = 50+58+65+72 = 245$, $\bar{y}_{C2} = 61.25$
- $T_{C3} = 48+56+64+71 = 239$, $\bar{y}_{C3} = 59.75$
- $B_{50} = 45+50+48 = 143$, $B_{60} = 55+58+56 = 169$, $B_{70} = 62+65+64 = 191$, $B_{80} = 70+72+71 = 213$
- $G = 232+245+239 = 716$, $N = 12$, $k = 3$, $b = 4$
- $\bar{y}_{..} = 716/12 = 59.667$

**Step 2: Compute CF** *(1 mark)*

$$
CF = \frac{716^2}{12} = \frac{512{,}656}{12} = 42{,}721.333
$$

**Step 3: Compute SST, SS_Trt, SS_Blk, SSE** *(2 marks)*

$$
\sum\sum y_{ij}^2 = (2025+3025+3844+4900) + (2500+3364+4225+5184) + (2304+3136+4096+5041) = 13{,}794 + 15{,}273 + 14{,}577 = 43{,}644
$$

$$
SST = 43{,}644 - 42{,}721.333 = 922.667
$$

$$
SS_{Trt} = \frac{232^2 + 245^2 + 239^2}{4} - 42{,}721.333 = \frac{53{,}824 + 60{,}025 + 57{,}121}{4} - 42{,}721.333
$$

$$
SS_{Trt} = 42{,}742.500 - 42{,}721.333 = 21.167
$$

$$
SS_{Blk} = \frac{143^2 + 169^2 + 191^2 + 213^2}{3} - 42{,}721.333 = \frac{20{,}449 + 28{,}561 + 36{,}481 + 45{,}369}{3} - 42{,}721.333
$$

$$
SS_{Blk} = 43{,}620.000 - 42{,}721.333 = 898.667
$$

$$
SSE = 922.667 - 21.167 - 898.667 = 2.833
$$

**Step 4: ANOVA table** *(1.5 marks)*

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catalysts (Treatments) | 21.167 | 2 | 10.583 | **44.78** |
| Blocks (Temperature) | 898.667 | 3 | 299.556 | **1267.18** |
| Error | 2.833 | 6 | 0.4722 | — |
| Total | 922.667 | 11 | — | — |

$F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$, $F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.76$

**Step 5: Conclusion** *(1.5 marks)*

Both effects are highly significant (F-values massively exceed critical F). Therefore:
- **Catalyst type** significantly affects conversion (p < 0.001)
- **Temperature block** significantly affects conversion (p < 0.001)
- C2 produces the highest mean conversion (61.25%), and 80°C is the optimal block.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Enter data in long format in R** *(2 marks)*

```r
conversion <- c(45,55,62,70, 50,58,65,72, 48,56,64,71)
catalyst   <- factor(rep(c("C1","C2","C3"), each=4))
temperature <- factor(rep(c("T50","T60","T70","T80"), times=3))
df <- data.frame(conversion, catalyst, temperature)
```

**Step 2: Fit the two-way ANOVA model (additive)** *(2 marks)*

```r
model <- aov(conversion ~ catalyst + temperature, data=df)
summary(model)
```

**Step 3: Interpret the expected R output** *(2 marks)*

```
            Df  Sum Sq  Mean Sq  F value   Pr(>F)    
catalyst     2    21.17   10.58    44.78    0.0006 ***
temperature  3   898.67  299.56  1267.18  5.2e-08 ***
Residuals    6     2.83    0.47
```

**Step 4: Draw conclusions** *(1 mark)*

Both `catalyst` and `temperature` show `***` (p < 0.001), confirming strong statistical significance for both effects. The mean separation can be visualized using:

```r
TukeyHSD(model)
boxplot(conversion ~ catalyst, data=df, col="lightblue", main="Catalyst Effect")
interaction.plot(temperature, catalyst, conversion)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Don't confuse CRD vs RBD**: In CRD, only Treatments are sources; in RBD, Blocks are also a source.
> 2. **Degrees of freedom errors**: For RBD with k treatments and b blocks, $df_{Error} = (k-1)(b-1)$. Here: $(3-1)(4-1) = 6$. Many students incorrectly use $N-k = 12-3 = 9$.
> 3. **ToolPak not enabled**: In Excel, students forget to enable Data Analysis ToolPak and write the wrong procedure.
> 4. **Misinterpretation of p-value**: p > 0.05 does NOT "prove no difference" — it only "fails to reject H₀".
> 5. **Ignoring assumptions**: ANOVA assumes normality and equal variances. Always check residual plots before drawing conclusions.
> 6. **R syntax errors**: Using `aov(y ~ A*B)` gives an interaction model; `aov(y ~ A + B)` gives the additive model only. The KTU problem must be read carefully.
> 7. **Post-hoc test omission**: Significant ANOVA must be followed by post-hoc tests (Tukey, LSD) to identify WHICH treatments differ.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Use of Software for ANOVA**

- ☐ **Four approved tools**: MS-Excel (ToolPak), R, MINITAB, Design-Expert — choose any for hands-on practice.
- ☐ **The 5-step pipeline**: CF → SST → SS_Trt → SSE → MS → F-ratio → p-value.
- ☐ **Grand Sum $G$** is the foundation; CF = $G^2/N$.
- ☐ **Degrees of freedom**: $N-1$ (total), $k-1$ (treatments), $N-k$ (error CRD), $(k-1)(b-1)$ (error RBD).
- ☐ **Decision rule**: Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{crit}$ OR equivalently $p < \alpha$.
- ☐ **R essentials**: `aov(y ~ factor, data=df)` + `summary(model)` + `TukeyHSD(model)`.
- ☐ **Excel essentials**: Enable ToolPak first → Data → Data Analysis → Anova: Single Factor.
- ☐ **MINITAB essentials**: Stat → ANOVA → One-Way → check Tukey comparisons.
- ☐ **Design-Expert essentials**: Special-purpose for full/fractional factorial and RSM designs.
- ☐ **Always validate assumptions**: Normality (Q-Q plot) and homoscedasticity (Residuals vs Fitted plot).
- ☐ **Report three key metrics**: F-statistic, p-value, and R².
- ☐ **After significant F**: Always perform **post-hoc** test (Tukey HSD) to locate which means differ.
- ☐ **R² interpretation**: Closer to 1 = better fit; Adjusted R² penalizes unnecessary model terms.
- ☐ **CV (Coefficient of Variation)**: Lower CV (< 10–15%) = more precise experiment.
- ☐ **Always state hypotheses explicitly** before computing and conclude in context.
- ☐ **Don't forget to cite the software used** in your KTU lab records and reports.

---

> *End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Use of MS-Excel / R / MINITAB / Design Expert for ANOVA*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition, Estimating Model Parameters, Fitting Response Curves and Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition, Estimating Model Parameters, Fitting Response Curves and Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques that are useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes. It is formally defined as a procedure for **fitting empirical models** (response surfaces and curves) to experimental data obtained from designed experiments, with the ultimate goal of:

1. **Locating** the operating conditions that yield an optimal response.
2. **Characterizing** the response (output variable) as a function of controllable input variables (factors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** Response Surface Methodology is a sequential strategy for empirical model building and optimization, employing **first-order polynomial models** when the design space is in the vicinity of the optimum, and **second-order polynomial models** when curvature in the response surface is detected.

The **Response Variable (y)** is the dependent, observed outcome, while the **Independent Variables (x₁, x₂, ..., xₖ)** are the controllable process inputs. The underlying philosophy of RSM is to approximate the unknown true functional relationship.

$$y = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k) + \varepsilon$$

using a low-order polynomial in a *local* region of the factor space.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Coffee Brewing Analogy ☕**
>
> Imagine you are a barista trying to make the **perfect cup of coffee**. You have two knobs:
> - $x_1$ = Water Temperature (°C)
> - $x_2$ = Brewing Time (minutes)
>
> You don't have a physics equation for taste. So, you **conduct experiments**: brew several cups at different combinations of temperature and time, and rate each cup on a taste score (the *response* $y$).
>
> Now, instead of guessing the perfect cup, you **fit a smooth mathematical surface** (like a curved hill or valley) over those experimental taste scores. This surface *predicts* the taste score for ANY combination of temperature and time. The peak of this surface = your optimal coffee.
>
> **Response Curves** = 2D version (one knob) → A parabolic curve
> **Response Surfaces** = 3D version (two knobs) → A curved 2D manifold

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Response | $y$ | The outcome you're measuring (e.g., yield, strength, taste score) |
| Factor | $x_i$ | The independent input you control |
| Treatment Combination | $\mathbf{x}$ | A specific setting of all factors |
| Model | $\hat{y} = \hat{f}(\mathbf{x})$ | The fitted mathematical equation |
| Residual | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | The error between observed and predicted |
| Design Region | $\mathcal{R}$ | The allowable range of $x_i$ values |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The word *Empirical* is the key — RSM does NOT derive models from physics. It empirically fits polynomial surfaces to *experimental* data, making it a **data-driven** technique under NEP 2020's industry-aligned engineering pedagogy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Empirical Model Framework

In RSM, the unknown function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is approximated by a **polynomial of low order**. The choice of order depends on the curvature in the response:

- **No curvature** (flat region) → Use **Mean Model**: $y = \beta_0 + \varepsilon$
- **Linear trend only** (tilted plane) → Use **First-Order Model**
- **Significant curvature** (curved surface) → Use **Second-Order Model**

---

### 2.2 First-Order Model (Response Plane)

The first-order model in $k$ variables is:

$$\mathbf{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$$

- $\beta_0$ = Intercept (response when all $x_i = 0$)
- $\beta_i$ = Linear effect of factor $i$
- $\varepsilon$ = Random error, assumed $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

**Matrix Formulation (Canonical KTU Form):**

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where

$$
\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1k} \\ 1 & x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2k} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{nk} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \vdots \\ \beta_k \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \vdots \\ \varepsilon_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### 2.3 Second-Order Model (Response Surface)

When curvature is significant, the second-order model is deployed:

$$\mathbf{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i<j} \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon$$

The matrix form $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ remains valid, but the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ now contains columns for $x_i^2$ and $x_i x_j$, giving a total of $p = 1 + 2k + \binom{k}{2}$ parameters.

For $k = 2$: $p = 6$ parameters ($\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_{11}, \beta_{22}, \beta_{12}$)

---

### 2.4 Least Squares Estimation (LSE) — Why This Method?

The most common approach to estimate model parameters in RSM is the **Method of Least Squares**. The principle is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle of Least Squares:** Choose $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ such that the **sum of squared residuals** $L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ is **minimized**.

The "why" behind using squared errors:
1. Mathematical tractability (differentiable, leads to linear normal equations)
2. Larger errors are penalized more heavily (good for outlier sensitivity)
3. Yields **BLUE** estimators (Best Linear Unbiased Estimators) under Gauss-Markov assumptions

**Setting Up the Loss Function:**

$$L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^T\boldsymbol{\beta})^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

Differentiating with respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = 0$$

This yields the celebrated **Normal Equations**:

$$\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

Solving for the **Least Squares Estimator (LSE)**:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}}$$

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE Part B question on Module 3.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General RSM Model | $y = f(x_1, \ldots, x_k) + \varepsilon$ | $y$ response, $x_i$ factors, $\varepsilon$ error |
| 2 | First-Order Model | $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$ | $k+1$ parameters |
| 3 | Second-Order Model | $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii}x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij}x_i x_j + \varepsilon$ | $1 + 2k + \binom{k}{2}$ params |
| 4 | Matrix Form | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | $\mathbf{X}$ is $n \times p$ design matrix |
| 5 | LSE (Closed Form) | $\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ | $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ must be non-singular |
| 6 | Fitted Response | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$ | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T$ (Hat Matrix) |
| 7 | Residual Vector | $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{y}$ | Dimension $n \times 1$ |
| 8 | Variance of $\hat{\beta}$ | $\text{Var}(\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}}) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ | Requires $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})$ |
| 9 | Error Variance (Unbiased) | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \dfrac{\mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{e}}{n-p} = \dfrac{SS_E}{n-p}$ | $p$ = number of parameters |
| 10 | Total Sum of Squares | $SS_T = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - \dfrac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}$ | Measures total variation |
| 11 | Regression SS | $SS_R = \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 = \boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} - \dfrac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}$ | Explained by model |
| 12 | Error SS | $SS_E = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ | Unexplained variation |
| 13 | Identity (Decomposition) | $SS_T = SS_R + SS_E$ | Always holds |
| 14 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_E}{SS_T} = \dfrac{SS_R}{SS_T}$ | $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ |
| 15 | Adjusted $R^2$ | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p}$ | Penalizes extra parameters |
| 16 | Predicted Response at $\mathbf{x}_0$ | $\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{x}_0^T\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}}$ | Scalar value |
| 17 | Variance of Prediction | $\text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_0)] = \sigma^2 \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^T(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$ | Used for CI construction |
| 18 | Lack-of-Fit Sum of Squares | $SS_{LOF} = SS_E - SS_{PE}$ | $SS_{PE}$ = Pure Error SS |
| 19 | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \dfrac{SS_E}{n-p}$ | Used in F-tests |
| 20 | F-Statistic for Model | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_R}{MS_E} = \dfrac{SS_R/p-1}{SS_E/n-p}$ | Tests overall model significance |
