# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Hat Matrix and list any three of its properties used in regression diagnostics.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Hat Matrix is $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T$. It projects $\mathbf{y}$ onto the column space of $\mathbf{X}$, so that $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{Hy}$ and $\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{y}$.

**Three Properties:**
1. **Symmetric:** $\mathbf{H}^T = \mathbf{H}$
2. **Idempotent:** $\mathbf{H}^2 = \mathbf{H}$
3. **Trace:** $tr(\mathbf{H}) = p$ (number of parameters)

These properties allow the diagonal element $h_{ii}$ to act as the **leverage** of observation $i$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between internally and externally studentized residuals. When is the latter preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Internally Studentized** $r_i = \dfrac{e_i}{s\sqrt{1-h_{ii}}}$ uses $s^2$ (MSE from the **full** dataset).
>
> **Externally (Deleted) Studentized** $t_i = \dfrac{e_i}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{1-h_{ii}}}$ uses $s_{(i)}^2$ (MSE after deleting observation $i$).
>
> The **externally studentized residual** is preferred because, under the null hypothesis that observation $i$ is not an outlier, it follows an **exact $t_{(n-p-1)}$ distribution**, enabling formal statistical tests. This is the version used by automated outlier-detection procedures.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define Cook's Distance and explain its role in identifying influential observations. State the cutoff rule used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following 6 observations of $(x_i, y_i)$:

| $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| $y_i$ | 2.5 | 4.2 | 5.8 | 8.1 | 9.6 | 11.5 |

Fit a simple linear regression, compute the residuals, leverages, and Cook's Distance. Identify any influential observations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Cook's Distance** for observation $i$ is defined as:

$$
D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p \cdot MSE} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1 - h_{ii})^2}
$$

Equivalently, it can be expressed as the squared distance between $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)}$ (the estimate obtained after deleting observation $i$), scaled by the size of the $(1-\alpha)$ confidence ellipsoid for $\boldsymbol{\beta}$:

$$
D_i = \frac{(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)})^T (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}) (\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)})}{p \cdot MSE}
$$

**Role:** $D_i$ combines **residual size** $e_i$ and **leverage** $h_{ii}$. A large $D_i$ means deleting this observation substantially changes the regression coefficients, indicating the observation is **influential**.

**Cutoff Rules:**
- $D_i > 1$ → highly influential (conservative).
- $D_i > \dfrac{4}{n}$ → potentially influential (commonly used).
- $D_i > F_{0.5, \, p, \, n-p}$ → borderline significant.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute means and sums:**

$$
\bar{x} = 3.5, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2.5+4.2+5.8+8.1+9.6+11.5}{6} = \frac{41.7}{6} = 6.95
$$

$$
S_{xx} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 6.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 = 17.5
$$

$$
S_{xy} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = (27.625) \text{ (computed as: } -2.5 \times 4.45 = -11.125, -1.5 \times 2.75 = -4.125, -0.5 \times 1.15 = -0.575, 0.5 \times 1.15 = 0.575, 1.5 \times 2.65 = 3.975, 2.5 \times 4.55 = 11.375) \text{ sum} = 0.10
$$

Wait, let me recompute carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{xy} &= (-2.5)(6.95-2.5) + (-1.5)(6.95-4.2) + (-0.5)(6.95-5.8) + (0.5)(6.95-8.1) \\
       &\quad + (1.5)(6.95-9.6) + (2.5)(6.95-11.5) \\
       &= (-2.5)(4.45) + (-1.5)(2.75) + (-0.5)(1.15) + (0.5)(-1.15) \\
       &\quad + (1.5)(-2.65) + (2.5)(-4.55) \\
       &= -11.125 - 4.125 - 0.575 - 0.575 - 3.975 - 11.375 \\
       &= -31.75
\end{aligned}
$$

This negative value indicates a computational error. Let me redo using direct deviation products:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{xy} &= (1-3.5)(2.5-6.95) + (2-3.5)(4.2-6.95) + (3-3.5)(5.8-6.95) \\
       &\quad + (4-3.5)(8.1-6.95) + (5-3.5)(9.6-6.95) + (6-3.5)(11.5-6.95) \\
       &= (-2.5)(-4.45) + (-1.5)(-2.75) + (-0.5)(-1.15) + (0.5)(1.15) + (1.5)(2.65) + (2.5)(4.55) \\
       &= 11.125 + 4.125 + 0.575 + 0.575 + 3.975 + 11.375 \\
       &= 31.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Estimates:**

$$
\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{31.75}{17.5} = 1.814, \quad \hat{\beta}_0 = 6.95 - 1.814 \times 3.5 = 6.95 - 6.349 = 0.601
$$

**Fitted Model:** $\hat{y} = 0.601 + 1.814 \, x$

**Step 3 — Residuals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= 2.5 - (0.601 + 1.814) = 2.5 - 2.415 = 0.085 \\
e_2 &= 4.2 - (0.601 + 3.628) = 4.2 - 4.229 = -0.029 \\
e_3 &= 5.8 - (0.601 + 5.442) = 5.8 - 6.043 = -0.243 \\
e_4 &= 8.1 - (0.601 + 7.256) = 8.1 - 7.857 = 0.243 \\
e_5 &= 9.6 - (0.601 + 9.070) = 9.6 - 9.671 = -0.071 \\
e_6 &= 11.5 - (0.601 + 10.884) = 11.5 - 11.485 = 0.015
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — SSE and MSE:**

$$
SSE = 0.0072 + 0.0008 + 0.0590 + 0.0590 + 0.0050 + 0.0002 = 0.1313
$$

$$
MSE = \frac{0.1313}{6-2} = 0.0328
$$

**Step 5 — Leverages** (using $h_{ii} = \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{11} &= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{6.25}{17.5} = 0.1667 + 0.3571 = 0.5238 \\
h_{22} &= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{2.25}{17.5} = 0.1667 + 0.1286 = 0.2953 \\
h_{33} &= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{0.25}{17.5} = 0.1667 + 0.0143 = 0.1810 \\
h_{44} &= 0.1810 \\
h_{55} &= 0.2953 \\
h_{66} &= 0.5238
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** $\sum h_{ii} = 0.5238 + 0.2953 + 0.1810 + 0.1810 + 0.2953 + 0.5238 = 2.0002 \approx 2$ ✓

**Step 6 — Cook's Distance** (with $p = 2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \frac{(0.085)^2}{2 \times 0.0328} \cdot \frac{0.5238}{(1-0.5238)^2} = \frac{0.0072}{0.0656} \cdot \frac{0.5238}{0.2264} = 0.1101 \times 2.3136 = 0.2547 \\
D_2 &= \frac{(0.029)^2}{0.0656} \cdot \frac{0.2953}{0.4972} = 0.01283 \times 0.5939 = 0.00762 \\
D_3 &= \frac{(0.243)^2}{0.0656} \cdot \frac{0.1810}{0.6710} = 0.9002 \times 0.2697 = 0.2428 \\
D_4 &= 0.2428 \\
D_5 &= 0.00762 \\
D_6 &= 0.2547
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Decision** using $4/n = 4/6 = 0.667$:

All $D_i < 0.667$. **No observation is influential** under the conventional threshold. The largest values are $D_1 = D_6 = 0.255$ (high leverage but small residuals).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Cook's Distance:**
> 1. **Forgetting** to multiply by $h_{ii}/(1-h_{ii})^2$ — the leverage part is mandatory. Lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong cutoff** — students often quote "$D_i > 1$" without justifying the sample size. Use $4/n$ for the adaptive threshold. Lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the trace check** $\sum h_{ii} = p$. Validators look for this **2-mark** sanity check.
> 4. **Mixing up** Cook's D with DFFITS — they are different quantities. Lose **3 marks** if confused.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Breusch–Pagan test for heteroscedasticity. State the null hypothesis, test statistic, and the decision rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A regression of yield $Y$ on temperature $X_1$ and pressure $X_2$ was run with $n = 25$ observations. The Breusch–Pagan auxiliary regression of $e_i^2$ on $X_1$ and $X_2$ yielded $R^2_{\text{aux}} = 0.31$. Test for heteroscedasticity at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

> The **Breusch–Pagan (1979) test** checks whether the error variance is systematically related to the regressors.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**

$$
H_0: \sigma_i^2 = \sigma^2 \;\; \text{(homoscedasticity)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \sigma_i^2 = \sigma^2 \cdot f(\alpha_0 + \alpha_1 X_{1i} + \cdots + \alpha_k X_{ki})
$$

Equivalently: $H_0: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \cdots = \alpha_k = 0$.

**Step 2 — Procedure:**

1. Estimate the original OLS model and obtain residuals $e_i$.
2. Compute $e_i^2$.
3. Run the **auxiliary regression** $e_i^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 X_{1i} + \cdots + \alpha_k X_{ki} + u_i$ and obtain $R^2_{\text{aux}}$.
4. Compute the **Lagrange Multiplier** statistic:

$$
LM_{BP} = n \cdot R^2_{\text{aux}}
$$

5. Under $H_0$, $LM_{BP} \sim \chi^2_{(k)}$.
6. **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $LM_{BP} > \chi^2_{(k), \alpha}$.

**Properties:**
- The test is **asymptotically distributed** (valid for large $n$).
- It is **sensitive to non-normality** — pair with a normality test.
- It does **not identify** the form of heteroscedasticity.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $n = 25$, $k = 2$ (regressors $X_1$ and $X_2$), $R^2_{\text{aux}} = 0.31$, $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Compute LM statistic:**

$$
LM_{BP} = n \cdot R^2_{\text{aux}} = 25 \times 0.31 = 7.75
$$

**Step 2 — Critical value:** $\chi^2_{(2), \, 0.05} = 5.991$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
LM_{BP} = 7.75 > 5.991 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ at the 5% level}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** There is **significant evidence of heteroscedasticity**. The error variance depends systematically on temperature and/or pressure.

**Step 5 — Remedial measures (to earn full marks):**
- Use **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** with weights $w_i = 1/\hat{\sigma}_i^2$.
- Apply **variance-stabilizing transformation** (e.g., $\ln Y$, $\sqrt{Y}$).
- Use **heteroscedasticity-consistent (White) standard errors**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Breusch–Pagan Test:**
> 1. **Confusing** the test statistic with the F-statistic — Breusch–Pagan is an **LM test** (chi-square), not an F-test. Lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting** that the test requires $e_i^2$ (not $e_i$) on the LHS. Lose **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating** the degrees of freedom. Lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the remedial step** — examiners expect at least one practical suggestion. Lose **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Regression Model Diagnostics (Module 4)**

### Core Conceptual Points
- ✅ The **Hat Matrix** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T$ is **idempotent and symmetric**, with $tr(\mathbf{H}) = p$.
- ✅ The **leverage** $h_{ii}$ measures how far $x_i$ is from $\bar{x}$; threshold for "high" leverage: $h_{ii} > 2p/n$.
- ✅ **Raw residuals** are NOT comparable because $Var(e_i) = \sigma^2(1-h_{ii})$ — always studentize.
- ✅ **Cook's Distance** combines leverage and residual; cutoff $4/n$.
- ✅ **DFFITS** scales the change in fitted value by the deleted-MSE; cutoff $2\sqrt{p/n}$.
- ✅ **DFBETAS** scales the change in coefficient $\beta_j$; cutoff $2/\sqrt{n}$.

### Assumption Tests
- ✅ **Linearity** → residual vs. fitted plot; cure with polynomial terms or Box–Cox.
- ✅ **Independence** → Durbin–Watson; cure with GLS, Cochrane–Orcutt.
- ✅ **Homoscedasticity** → Breusch–Pagan ($LM = nR^2 \sim \chi^2_{(k)}$) or White's test.
- ✅ **Normality** → Shapiro–Wilk, Anderson–Darling, Q–Q plot; cure with transformations.
- ✅ **Multicollinearity** → $VIF_j = 1/(1-R_j^2) > 10$ is severe; $\kappa > 30$ is also a red flag.
- ✅ **Lack of Fit** → $F = \dfrac{SS_{LF}/(c-p)}{SS_{PE}/(n-c)}$; needs replicates.

### Common Pitfalls in KTU Valuation
- ❌ Using **$D_i > 1$** rigidly without considering $n$ — prefer $4/n$.
- ❌ Reporting **unstandardized residuals** as if they were comparable.
- ❌ Confusing **Cook's D** with **DFFITS** or **DFBETAS** — they answer different questions.
- ❌ Forgetting the **trace check** $\sum h_{ii} = p$ in numerical work.
- ❌ Applying **Breusch–Pagan** on small samples (asymptotic test, requires $n \geq 30$ ideally).
- ❌ Reporting influential observations without **suggesting remedial action** (WLS, removal, robust regression, transformation).

### Engineering Real-World Utility
- 🔧 **Process Industries:** Diagnose whether a control variable $X$ has linear or non-linear effect on output $Y$.
- 🔧 **Quality Engineering:** Detect which experimental run is contaminated and warrants re-measurement.
- 🔧 **Reliability Analysis:** Verify assumptions before predicting equipment failure times.
- 🔧 **Design of Experiments (DOE):** Validate fitted response surface models before optimizing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing for Lack of Fit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Testing for Lack of Fit # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lack of Fit (LoF)** is a formal hypothesis test used in regression analysis and Design of Experiments (DOE) to determine whether the chosen (fitted) mathematical model adequately represents the true functional relationship between the independent variable(s) and the response, **or whether significant systematic variation remains un-explained by the model**. The Lack of Fit test decomposes the **pure experimental error** (from replicated observations) from the **model error** (residuals), allowing the experimenter to conclude whether the proposed model is **adequate** or whether a **higher-order model** is required. In the notation of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Montgomery's framework), the test hypotheses are: $$ H_0 : \text{The regression model is adequate (no lack of fit)} $$ $$ H_1 : \text{The regression model is inadequate (lack of fit exists)} $$ The decision statistic is the **F-ratio**: $$ F_0 = \frac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}} \sim F_{\alpha,\;df_{LOF},\;df_{PE}} $$ --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Tailor's Measuring Tape:** > > Imagine a tailor uses a **straight ruler** to measure curved fabric pieces. The ruler cannot follow the curve perfectly, so it always leaves a small gap. If the gaps are **small and random**, the straight-edge model is acceptable. If the gaps form a **systematic pattern** (always bulging in the middle), the ruler is the wrong tool — you need a curved template. > > In statistical terms: > - **Straight ruler** = Linear regression model > - **Gaps** = Residuals > - **Random small gaps** = Pure experimental error (acceptable) > - **Systematic patterned gaps** = Lack of Fit (model is wrong) The **Lack of Fit test** is the statistical "ruler check" that separates random measurement noise from genuine model misspecification. --- ## 1.3 Why Is This Test Critical? | Scenario | Implication for the Engineer | |----------|------------------------------| | Model passes LoF test | Predictions are reliable; use the fitted model for optimization | | Model fails LoF test | Predictions are biased; refit with a higher-order polynomial or transformed variables | | No replicates available | LoF test **cannot be performed** — must add replication or use pure replication designs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** Significance level is conventionally **α = 0.05** for KTU board examinations, unless explicitly stated otherwise in the problem statement. --- ## 1.4 Pre-requisites for Performing the Test > [!WARNING] > The Lack of Fit test **CANNOT** be conducted unless the experimental design contains **at least one replicate** (i.e., two or more observations at the same level of the independent variable(s)). Without replicates, the pure error cannot be estimated. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of Total Variability (The Core Logic) The total sum of squares ($SS_T$) is partitioned sequentially as follows: $$ SS_T = SS_R + SS_E $$ Where: - $SS_T$ = Total Sum of Squares (corrected for the mean) - $SS_R$ = Regression Sum of Squares - $SS_E$ = Error / Residual Sum of Squares The **Error Sum of Squares** is further split **only when replicates exist**: $$ SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE} $$ Where: - $SS_{LOF}$ = Sum of Squares due to Lack of Fit - $SS_{PE}$ = Sum of Squares due to Pure Error (from replicates) --- ## 2.2 The Hierarchical Decomposition Tree $$ SS_T = \underbrace{SS_R}_{\text{explained by model}} + \underbrace{SS_{LOF}}_{\text{model misspecification}} + \underbrace{SS_{PE}}_{\text{true replication noise}} $$ ### Logical Steps (Bulleted Reasoning) 1. **Replicate observations at the same $x$-level** allow us to estimate the **pure error** — variation due to repeated measurements under identical conditions. 2. If the **fitted model passes through the mean of replicates** correctly, the only remaining error is pure random noise ($SS_{PE}$). 3. If the **fitted model systematically deviates** from the means of replicates, this deviation is $SS_{LOF}$. 4. The **ratio** $MS_{LOF} / MS_{PE}$ tests whether the systematic deviation exceeds random noise. --- ## 2.3 Detailed Formula Derivation Let there be $c$ **distinct levels** of $x$, with $n_i$ replicates at level $i$, giving a total of $N = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i$ observations. Let $\bar{y}_i$ be the mean of the $n_i$ observations at level $i$, and $\hat{y}_i$ be the predicted value from the fitted model at $x_i$. ### A. Pure Error Sum of Squares $$ SS_{PE} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i)^2 $$ $$ df_{PE} = N - c $$ ### B. Lack of Fit Sum of Squares $$ SS_{LOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\bar{y}_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 $$ $$ df_{LOF} = c - p $$ where $p$ = number of parameters in the fitted model (including the intercept $\beta_0$). ### C. Error / Residual Sum of Squares (Verification) $$ SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (y_{ij} - \hat{y}_i)^2 $$ $$ df_E = N - p $$ ### D. Mean Squares $$ MS_{LOF} = \frac{SS_{LOF}}{df_{LOF}}, \quad MS_{PE} = \frac{SS_{PE}}{df_{PE}} $$ ### E. Test Statistic $$ F_0 = \frac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}} $$ **Decision Rule:** $$ \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } F_0 > F_{\alpha,\;df_{LOF},\;df_{PE}} $$ --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Degrees of Freedom | |---|----------|---------|--------------------| | 1 | Total SS | $SS_T = \sum_{i,j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$ | $N - 1$ | | 2 | Regression SS | $SS_R = \sum_{i,j}(\hat{y}_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2$ | $p - 1$ | | 3 | Error / Residual SS | $SS_E = SS_T - SS_R$ | $N - p$ | | 4 | Pure Error SS | $SS_{PE} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i)^2$ | $N - c$ | | 5 | Lack of Fit SS | $SS_{LOF} = \sum_{i} n_i(\bar{y}_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $c - p$ | | 6 | Verification | $SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE}$ | $N - p$ | | 7 | $MS_{LOF}$ | $SS_{LOF}/df_{LOF}$ | — | | 8 | $MS_{PE}$ | $SS_{PE}/df_{PE}$ | — | | 9 | F-statistic | $F_0 = MS_{LOF}/MS_{PE}$ | $(df_{LOF},\;df_{PE})$ | | 10 | Critical Value | $F_{\alpha,\;c-p,\;N-c}$ | — | > [!NOTE] > **Key Identities to Memorize:** > - $df_{LOF} = c - p$ > - $df_{PE} = N - c$ > - $df_{LOF} + df_{PE} = N - p = df_E$ --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application of LoF Test | |-------------------|--------------------------| | **Chemical Engineering** | Verifying whether a first-order reaction rate law fits batch reactor data | | **Mechanical Engineering** | Checking whether a linear stress-strain model is adequate for composite materials | | **Civil Engineering** | Testing adequacy of linear creep models in concrete under sustained loads | | **Biomedical Engineering** | Validating pharmacokinetic dose-response curves | | **Industrial Engineering** | Confirming linearity of yield-loss functions in Six-Sigma projects | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Worked Derivation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Setup:** A chemical engineer studies the effect of **temperature ($x$, °C)** on **reaction yield ($y$, %)**. Five temperature levels are tested with varying replication. A **first-order (linear) model** $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$ is proposed. Test for lack of fit at $\alpha = 0.05$. ### Data Table | Level ($i$) | Temp $x_i$ (°C) | Observations $y_{ij}$ | $n_i$ | Mean $\bar{y}_i$ | Fitted $\hat{y}_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 100 | 5.2, 5.5 | 2 | 5.35 | 5.10 | | 2 | 120 | 7.1 | 1 | 7.10 | 7.30 | | 3 | 140 | 9.5, 9.2, 9.8 | 3 | 9.50 | 9.50 | | 4 | 160 | 11.3 | 1 | 11.30 | 11.70 | | 5 | 180 | 13.4, 13.7 | 2 | 13.55 | 13.90 | | **Total** | — | — | **N = 9** | — | — | The fitted regression line (from prior least-squares computation): $\hat{y} = -0.5 + 0.08x$. **Parameters:** $N = 9$, $c = 5$, $p = 2$, so $df_{PE} = 9 - 5 = 4$ and $df_{LOF} = 5 - 2 = 3$. --- ### Step 1: Compute the Grand Mean $\bar{y}_{..}$ $$ \bar{y}_{..} = \frac{\sum_{i,j} y_{ij}}{N} = \frac{5.2 + 5.5 + 7.1 + 9.5 + 9.2 + 9.8 + 11.3 + 13.4 + 13.7}{9} $$ $$ \bar{y}_{..} = \frac{84.7}{9} = 9.4111 $$ --- ### Step 2: Compute Pure Error Sum of Squares $SS_{PE}$ $$ SS_{PE} = \sum_{i=1}^{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i)^2 $$ **Level 1 ($n_1 = 2$):** $$ (5.2 - 5.35)^2 + (5.5 - 5.35)^2 = (-0.15)^2 + (0.15)^2 = 0.0225 + 0.0225 = 0.0450 $$ **Level 2 ($n_2 = 1$):** Contributes **0** (single observation = its own mean). **Level 3 ($n_3 = 3$):** $$ (9.5 - 9.5)^2 + (9.2 - 9.5)^2 + (9.8 - 9.5)^2 = 0 + 0.09 + 0.09 = 0.1800 $$ **Level 4 ($n_4 = 1$):** Contributes **0**. **Level 5 ($n_5 = 2$):** $$ (13.4 - 13.55)^2 + (13.7 - 13.55)^2 = (-0.15)^2 + (0.15)^2 = 0.0450 $$ **Summing all levels:** $$ SS_{PE} = 0.0450 + 0 + 0.1800 + 0 + 0.0450 = 0.2700 $$ $$ df_{PE} = N - c = 9 - 5 = 4 $$ $$ MS_{PE} = \frac{SS_{PE}}{df_{PE}} = \frac{0.2700}{4} = 0.0675 $$ --- ### Step 3: Compute Lack of Fit Sum of Squares $SS_{LOF}$ $$ SS_{LOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\bar{y}_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 $$ **Level 1:** $n_1(\bar{y}_1 - \hat{y}_1)^2 = 2 \times (5.35 - 5.10)^2 = 2 \times (0.25)^2 = 2 \times 0.0625 = 0.1250$ **Level 2:** $n_2(\bar{y}_2 - \hat{y}_2)^2 = 1 \times (7.10 - 7.30)^2 = 1 \times (-0.20)^2 = 0.0400$ **Level 3:** $n_3(\bar{y}_3 - \hat{y}_3)^2 = 3 \times (9.50 - 9.50)^2 = 3 \times 0 = 0.0000$ **Level 4:** $n_4(\bar{y}_4 - \hat{y}_4)^2 = 1 \times (11.30 - 11.70)^2 = 1 \times (-0.40)^2 = 0.1600$ **Level 5:** $n_5(\bar{y}_5 - \hat{y}_5)^2 = 2 \times (13.55 - 13.90)^2 = 2 \times (-0.35)^2 = 2 \times 0.1225 = 0.2450$ **Total:** $$ SS_{LOF} = 0.1250 + 0.0400 + 0.0000 + 0.1600 + 0.2450 = 0.5700 $$ $$ df_{LOF} = c - p = 5 - 2 = 3 $$ $$ MS_{LOF} = \frac{SS_{LOF}}{df_{LOF}} = \frac{0.5700}{3} = 0.1900 $$ --- ### Step 4: Compute F-Statistic $$ F_0 = \frac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}} = \frac{0.1900}{0.0675} = 2.8148 $$ --- ### Step 5: Critical Value From F-tables at $\alpha = 0.05$: $$ F_{0.05,\;3,\;4} = 6.59 $$ --- ### Step 6: Decision $$ F_0 = 2.8148 < F_{0.05, 3, 4} = 6.59 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** **Fail to reject $H_0$.** The linear model is **adequate** — there is no statistical evidence of lack of fit at the 5% significance level. --- ## 3.2 Complete ANOVA Summary Table | Source of Variation | SS | df | MS | F | |---|---|---|---|---| | Regression | 60.96 | 1 | 60.96 | — | | Lack of Fit | 0.5700 | 3 | 0.1900 | **2.8148** | | Pure Error | 0.2700 | 4 | 0.0675 | — | | **Total (Error)** | **0.8400** | **7** | 0.1200 | — | | **Total (Corrected)** | **61.80** | **8** | — | — | > [!NOTE] > **Verification Check:** $SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE} = 0.5700 + 0.2700 = 0.8400$ ✓ --- ## 3.3 Decision Logic Flowchart Summary ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Are replicates present? │ └────────────┬─────────────┘ │ │ NO YES │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ LoF test NOT │ │ Compute SS_PE and │ │ possible. Add │ │ SS_LOF separately. │ │ replicates. │ │ Compute F_0. │ └────────────────┘ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ ┌─────────▼─────────┐ │ Compare F_0 with │ │ F_(α, df_LOF, │ │ df_PE) │ └─────────┬─────────┘ │ │ F_0 > F_crit F_0 ≤ F_crit │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ Reject H_0: │ │ Fail to │ │ Model lacks │ │ reject H_0: │ │ fit. Try │ │ Model is │ │ higher-order │ │ adequate. │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ ``` # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Partitioning of Total Sum of Squares ```mermaid graph TD A["SS_TOTAL
(Total Variability)"] --> B["SS_REGRESSION
(Explained by Model)"] A --> C["SS_ERROR
(Residual Variability)"] C --> D["SS_LOF
(Lack of Fit)"] C --> E["SS_PE
(Pure Error from Replicates)"] D --> F["df = c minus p"] E --> G["df = N minus c"] F --> H["MS_LOF = SS_LOF / df_LOF"] G --> I["MS_PE = SS_PE / df_PE"] H --> J["F_0 = MS_LOF / MS_PE"] I --> J J --> K{"F_0 greater than F_crit ?"} K -->|Yes| L["Reject H0 : Model Lacks Fit"] K -->|No| M["Fail to Reject H0 : Model is Adequate"] ``` ## 4.2 Procedural Flow for Conducting the Lack of Fit Test ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Step 1: Verify Replicates Exist in Design"] --> P2["Step 2: Fit Proposed Model using Least Squares"] P2 --> P3["Step 3: Compute Means y_bar_i at each x_level"] P3 --> P4["Step 4: Compute Fitted Values y_hat_i"] P4 --> P5["Step 5: Calculate SS_PE from Replicates"] P5 --> P6["Step 6: Calculate SS_LOF from Means minus Fitted"] P6 --> P7["Step 7: Compute MS_LOF and MS_PE"] P7 --> P8["Step 8: Compute F_0 Statistic"] P8 --> P9["Step 9: Look up F critical at alpha"] P9 --> P10["Step 10: Make Decision and Conclude"] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Decision System ```mermaid graph LR subgraph INPUT_BLOCK ["INPUT DATA BLOCK"] I1["Raw Observations y_ij"] I2["Levels x_i and Replicates n_i"] I3["Candidate Model Specification"] end subgraph COMPUTATION_BLOCK ["COMPUTATION ENGINE"] C1["Regression Module
Computes y_hat_i"] C2["Aggregation Module
Computes y_bar_i"] C3["SS Partition Module
SS_PE and SS_LOF"] C4["F Ratio Generator
F_0 = MS_LOF / MS_PE"] end subgraph DECISION_BLOCK ["DECISION SUBSYSTEM"] D1["F Distribution Lookup"] D2["Comparison Operator
F_0 versus F_alpha"] D3["Conclusion Generator"] end I1 --> C2 I2 --> C2 I3 --> C1 I1 --> C1 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 C4 --> D2 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The architecture mirrors the standard ANOVA partitioning pipeline. Data flows from the **INPUT_BLOCK** through the **COMPUTATION_BLOCK** (which mimics the algebraic decomposition $SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE}$), and terminates at the **DECISION_BLOCK**, which produces the final inferential output. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define "Lack of Fit" in the context of regression analysis. Why is it important to test for lack of fit?** **Model Answer:** Lack of fit refers to the situation in which the chosen regression model fails to adequately capture the functional relationship between the independent variable(s) and the response variable. It is tested using the **F-test for Lack of Fit**, which compares the mean square due to lack of fit ($MS_{LOF}$) with the mean square due to pure error ($MS_{PE}$). **Importance:** 1. It distinguishes between **random experimental error** and **systematic model misspecification**. 2. It guides the experimenter in deciding whether to retain the current model or refit with a **higher-order polynomial**. 3. It improves the **validity of inferences** (confidence intervals, prediction intervals) derived from the regression equation. 4. Without testing for lack of fit, an inadequate model may be accepted, leading to **biased predictions** and flawed engineering decisions. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State the hypotheses for the Lack of Fit test and write the test statistic formula.** **Model Answer:** **Hypotheses:** $$ H_0 : \text{The model is adequate (no lack of fit)} $$ $$ H_1 : \text{The model is inadequate (lack of fit is present)} $$ **Test Statistic:** $$ F_0 = \frac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}} = \frac{SS_{LOF}/(c-p)}{SS_{PE}/(N-c)} $$ where $N$ = total observations, $c$ = distinct levels of $x$, $p$ = number of model parameters. **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 > F_{\alpha,\;(c-p),\;(N-c)}$. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE full question (A or B). Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **A process engineer investigates the effect of catalyst concentration (x, in g/L) on the yield (y, in %) of a chemical reaction. The following data are obtained. A linear model is proposed. Test for lack of fit at α = 0.05.** | x | Replicates $y_{ij}$ | |---|---| | 1.0 | 4.2, 4.5 | | 2.0 | 6.1 | | 3.0 | 8.4, 8.0, 8.6 | | 4.0 | 10.5 | | 5.0 | 12.8, 13.2 | **The fitted regression equation is $\hat{y} = 2.0 + 2.2x$.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute the Pure Error Sum of Squares and Lack of Fit Sum of Squares. **Solution:** **Given:** $N = 9$, $c = 5$, $p = 2$. **Level means:** - $\bar{y}_1 = (4.2 + 4.5)/2 = 4.35$ - $\bar{y}_2 = 6.10$ - $\bar{y}_3 = (8.4 + 8.0 + 8.6)/3 = 8.333$ - $\bar{y}_4 = 10.50$ - $\bar{y}_5 = (12.8 + 13.2)/2 = 13.00$ **Fitted values** ($\hat{y} = 2.0 + 2.2x$): - $\hat{y}_1 = 2.0 + 2.2(1.0) = 4.20$ - $\hat{y}_2 = 2.0 + 2.2(2.0) = 6.40$ - $\hat{y}_3 = 2.0 + 2.2(3.0) = 8.60$ - $\hat{y}_4 = 2.0 + 2.2(4.0) = 10.80$ - $\hat{y}_5 = 2.0 + 2.2(5.0) = 13.00$ **Pure Error SS:** $$ SS_{PE} = (4.2-4.35)^2 + (4.5-4.35)^2 + 0 + (8.4-8.333)^2 + (8.0-8.333)^2 + (8.6-8.333)^2 + 0 + (12.8-13.0)^2 + (13.2-13.0)^2 $$ $$ SS_{PE} = 0.0225 + 0.0225 + 0.00444 + 0.11111 + 0.07111 + 0.04 + 0.04 = 0.31167 $$ $$ df_{PE} = N - c = 9 - 5 = 4 $$ **Lack of Fit SS:** $$ SS_{LOF} = 2(4.35-4.20)^2 + 1(6.10-6.40)^2 + 3(8.333-8.60)^2 + 1(10.50-10.80)^2 + 2(13.00-13.00)^2 $$ $$ SS_{LOF} = 2(0.0225) + 1(0.09) + 3(0.0711) + 1(0.09) + 0 = 0.045 + 0.09 + 0.2133 + 0.09 = 0.4383 $$ $$ df_{LOF} = c - p = 5 - 2 = 3 $$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Perform the F-test and draw conclusions. **Solution:** $$ MS_{PE} = \frac{0.31167}{4} = 0.07792 $$ $$ MS_{LOF} = \frac{0.4383}{3} = 0.14610 $$ $$ F_0 = \frac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}} = \frac{0.14610}{0.07792} = 1.875 $$ **Critical value** at $\alpha = 0.05$: $$ F_{0.05,\;3,\;4} = 6.59 $$ **Decision:** $$ F_0 = 1.875 < 6.59 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** **Fail to reject $H_0$.** At the 5% significance level, the linear model is **adequate** — there is no evidence of lack of fit. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the decomposition of the total sum of squares in a regression model with replicated observations. How is the Lack of Fit component identified? **Model Answer:** When a regression model is fitted to data with replicates, the total variability $SS_T$ is partitioned into: **Step 1:** Total sum of squares reflects all variation around the grand mean. $$ SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{..})^2 $$ **Step 2:** This total is split into the part explained by the regression model and the unexplained residual. $$ SS_T = SS_R + SS_E $$ **Step 3:** Because replicates exist at some $x$-levels, $SS_E$ can be further decomposed. $$ SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE} $$ **Identification of Components:** - $SS_{PE}$ measures variation **among observations within the same $x$-level** — it isolates the **pure experimental error** (irreducible noise). - $SS_{LOF}$ measures the deviation of the **mean response at each level** from the value **predicted by the fitted model** — it isolates **systematic model misspecification**. **Step 4:** The degrees of freedom split as: $$ df_E = N - p = (c - p) + (N - c) = df_{LOF} + df_{PE} $$ This decomposition is the foundation of the formal F-test for lack of fit. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: What are the limitations of the Lack of Fit test? What alternatives exist when replicates are not available? **Model Answer:** **Limitations:** 1. **Requires replicates:** The test cannot be performed unless there are two or more observations at the same level of $x$. 2. **Sensitive to outliers:** A single rogue observation can inflate $SS_{PE}$ and mask the true lack of fit. 3. **Unequal replication:** Tests become less powerful when replication is unbalanced across levels. 4. **Specific to model form:** A test that fails (model adequate) does not guarantee the model is the "best" — only that there is no statistical evidence against it. **Alternatives When No Replicates Are Available:** | Alternative | Principle | |-------------|-----------| | **Pure replication design** | Run additional repeated observations at selected $x$-levels to estimate $MS_{PE}$. | | **Sequential sums of squares** | Compare nested models (e.g., linear vs. quadratic) using partial F-tests. | | **Adjusted R-squared** | Penalizes model complexity; a plateau suggests adequate fit. | | **Residual analysis** | Plot residuals vs. fitted values; patterns indicate systematic lack of fit. | | **Lack of Fit via replication in CRD/RBD** | Use replicate runs in randomized designs to obtain an independent error estimate. | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing degrees of freedom:** Writing $df_{LOF} = N - p$ instead of $c - p$. **Always remember:** LoF measures deviation among **distinct levels**, not individual observations. > > 2. **Forgetting the verification identity:** Students often compute $SS_{LOF}$ and $SS_{PE}$ separately but fail to verify that $SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE}$. Always include this check. > > 3. **Misstating the hypotheses:** Writing "$H_0$ : Model is wrong" — the null hypothesis should always state **"the model is adequate"** (status quo). > > 4. **Wrong direction of F-test:** The F-ratio is $MS_{LOF}/MS_{PE}$ — **never** the reverse. The convention places the effect of interest in the numerator. > > 5. **Ignoring the replication requirement:** Losing 2–3 marks by not stating that **lack of fit cannot be tested without replicates** when asked about limitations. > > 6. **Arithmetic slip in $SS_{LOF}$:** Forgetting to multiply by $n_i$. The correct term is $n_i(\bar{y}_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$, **not** $(\bar{y}_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Glance Summary — Testing for Lack of Fit** - [ ] **Definition:** LoF test checks if the regression model is **adequate** to describe the data. - [ ] **Prerequisite:** Replicates must exist at one or more $x$-levels. - [ ] **Hypotheses:** $H_0$: model adequate; $H_1$: model inadequate. - [ ] **$SS_E$ Decomposition:** $SS_E = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE}$ - [ ] **Pure Error:** $SS_{PE} = \sum\sum(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i)^2$ with $df = N - c$ - [ ] **Lack of Fit:** $SS_{LOF} = \sum n_i(\bar{y}_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ with $df = c - p$ - [ ] **F-statistic:** $F_0 = MS_{LOF}/MS_{PE}$ - [ ] **Critical Value:** $F_{\alpha,\;(c-p),\;(N-c)}$ - [ ] **Decision:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 > F_{crit}$ (model lacks fit, refit). - [ ] **Degrees of Freedom Identity:** $df_{LOF} + df_{PE} = N - p$ - [ ] **Engineering Insight:** A small $F_0$ means the model captures the trend; a large $F_0$ means hidden curvature. - [ ] **Verification Step:** Always check $SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE} = SS_E$ before concluding. - [ ] **No replicates → No LoF test.** Use model-comparison or residual plots instead.

topics.title: Response Surface Methodology: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Response Surface Methodology: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques that are useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes in which a response of interest is influenced by several quantitative independent variables. The methodology was formally introduced by **George E. P. Box and K. B. Wilson in 1951**, and it integrates the principles of *Design of Experiments (DOE)*, *Empirical Model Building*, and *Optimization* into a single unified framework.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, RSM is the strategic sequel to **first-order (2<sup>k</sup> and 2<sup>k-p</sup>) designs**. While factorial designs locate the region of optimality, RSM is the navigational instrument used to **chart the curvature** within that region and pinpoint the **stationary point** (maximum, minimum, or saddle point) with mathematical precision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: Climbing a hill in fog.**
> Imagine a mountaineer lost in dense fog, standing on an unknown terrain that rises and falls. He cannot see the peak, but he can measure his *altitude* (the **response**, e.g., yield of a chemical reaction) at his *position* (the **independent variables**, e.g., temperature and pressure). By taking a few strategic altitude readings, he can:
> 1. First, build a simple **flat plane approximation** of the terrain (a *first-order model*) and walk in the direction the plane tilts most steeply upward — this is the **Method of Steepest Ascent**.
> 2. Once the flat plane no longer fits (because he is near the peak and the ground is curved), he switches to a **parabolic approximation** (a *second-order model* with squares and cross-products) to find the true summit — this is the **Response Surface** itself.
>
> RSM is precisely this: an empirical strategy to *find the peak* of an unknown response surface when running the actual process is expensive or slow.

### 1.3 Why RSM is Required in Engineering

| Stage of Process Knowledge | Appropriate Tool | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Screening many factors | 2<sup>k</sup> Factorial | Locates direction only; assumes linearity |
| Optimization near optimum | **RSM** | Fits curvature; locates true optimum |
| Final confirmation | Confirmation runs | Validates model predictions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RSM bridges the gap between *screening experiments* (Module 1–2) and *industrial optimization*. It is heavily weighted in the **PECHT865 Module 4** syllabus, which prescribes coverage of first-order models, the **method of steepest ascent**, and second-order response surface models.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in RSM

- **R<sup>2</sup> (Coefficient of Multiple Determination):** proportion of response variation explained by the model. Acceptable threshold is **R<sup>2</sup> ≥ 0.80** for screening, **R<sup>2</sup> ≥ 0.90** for optimization work.
- **R<sup>2</sup><sub>(adj)</sub>:** adjusted for the number of predictors; penalises over-fitting.
- **R<sup>2</sup><sub>(pred)</sub>:** predicted R<sup>2</sup> used in cross-validation of the model.
- **Lack-of-Fit p-value:** must be **> 0.05** for the model to be deemed adequate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Stage Strategic Architecture of RSM

**Stage I — First-Order Approximation (Linear Region)**
Used when the experimenter is **far from the optimum** and the response behaves almost linearly. A 2<sup>k</sup> factorial with *n* centre points is run.

**Stage II — Second-Order Approximation (Curvature Region)**
Used when the experimenter is **near the optimum** where curvature is significant. A *Central Composite Design (CCD)* or *Box-Behnken Design (BBD)* is employed so that quadratic and interaction terms can be estimated.

### 2.2 The First-Order Model

The general form for *k* quantitative factors is:

$$y = \beta_{0} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{i} x_{i} + \varepsilon$$

In matrix notation, this compact expression is:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) estimate of the parameter vector is obtained from the famous **Normal Equation**:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\, \mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$

The fitted (predicted) response at any point **x** is then:

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$$

### 2.3 The Second-Order Model (The Actual Response Surface)

Once curvature is detected, the polynomial is upgraded by adding squared and two-factor interaction terms:

$$y = \beta_{0} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{i}x_{i} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{ii}x_{i}^{2} + \sum_{i<j}\sum \beta_{ij}x_{i}x_{j} + \varepsilon$$

For the very common case of **k = 2** factors (which is the most frequently asked KTU 2-mark and 7-mark question), the model reduces to:

$$y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{1} + \beta_{2}x_{2} + \beta_{11}x_{1}^{2} + \beta_{22}x_{2}^{2} + \beta_{12}x_{1}x_{2} + \varepsilon$$

### 2.4 Method of Steepest Ascent (Path of Maximum Increase)

The direction of steepest ascent is given by the *gradient* of the first-order model. The proportional step size in coded units is taken along the **first-order regression coefficients normalised** to the largest coefficient:

$$\Delta x_{i} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_{i}}{\vert\hat{\beta}_{\max}\vert} \cdot \lambda$$

where **λ** is a user-chosen step in the variable with the largest absolute coefficient (typically chosen so that **λ = 1** coded unit, and the actual physical step = λ × spacing of the factor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why normalised?** Because coded variables are dimensionless; normalising prevents the steepest ascent path from being artificially stretched along a variable that simply has a wider coded range.

### 2.5 Locating the Stationary Point (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The stationary point **x<sub>s</sub>** of a second-order model is found by setting the gradient equal to the zero vector. In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$$

where **b** is the vector of linear coefficients and **B** is the symmetric matrix of pure quadratic and interaction coefficients:

$$\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \beta_{11} & \beta_{12}/2 \\ \beta_{12}/2 & \beta_{22} \end{bmatrix} \quad\text{(for k=2)}$$

The **canonical analysis** then classifies the stationary point using the eigenvalues of **B**:
- Both eigenvalues **negative** → **Maximum**
- Both eigenvalues **positive** → **Minimum**
- Opposite signs → **Saddle point** (re-optimise)

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-order model | $y=\beta_{0}+\sum\beta_{i}x_{i}+\varepsilon$ | Far from optimum |
| 2 | OLS estimate | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}=(\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ | Core of RSM estimation |
| 3 | Second-order model (k=2) | $y=\beta_{0}+\beta_{1}x_{1}+\beta_{2}x_{2}+\beta_{11}x_{1}^{2}+\beta_{22}x_{2}^{2}+\beta_{12}x_{1}x_{2}+\varepsilon$ | Includes curvature |
| 4 | Steepest ascent step | $\Delta x_{i}=\dfrac{\hat{\beta}_{i}}{\vert\hat{\beta}_{\max}\vert}\lambda$ | λ is a chosen step |
| 5 | Stationary point (k=2) | $\mathbf{x}_{s}=-\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | Solution of $\nabla\hat{y}=0$ |
| 6 | Predicted response at optimum | $\hat{y}_{s}=\beta_{0}+\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b}$ | Used for confirmation runs |
| 7 | R<sup>2</sup> | $R^{2}=1-\dfrac{SS_{Res}}{SS_{T}}$ | $\geq 0.90$ preferred |
| 8 | Eigenvalue test | signs of $\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2}$ of **B** | Max / Min / Saddle |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Chemical Engineering:** maximising yield of an organic synthesis by tuning *temperature*, *catalyst loading*, and *reaction time*.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** minimising surface roughness in CNC turning by optimising *spindle speed*, *feed*, and *depth of cut*.
> - **Civil Engineering:** maximising compressive strength of concrete by optimising *water–cement ratio*, *curing period*, and *admixture dosage*.
> - **Electronics:** minimising bit-error-rate of a communication channel by tuning *signal power* and *bandwidth*.
> - **Biotechnology:** maximising enzyme production by tuning *pH*, *temperature*, and *substrate concentration*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the OLS Estimator (Why β̂ has that form)

We want to **minimise the sum of squared residuals** $S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^{T}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$.

Differentiate with respect to **β** and set the gradient to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}$$

Rearranging yields the famous **Normal Equation**:

$$\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$

Assuming $\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X}$ is non-singular, pre-multiply both sides by $(\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}$:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$

**Engineering intuition:** OLS projects the response vector **y** onto the column space of the design matrix **X**. The matrix $(\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{T}$ is called the **projection (hat) matrix** because $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> A 2-factor second-order RSM experiment yields the fitted model (in **coded** variables):
> $$\hat{y} = 45.0 + 3.5x_{1} + 2.0x_{2} - 1.2x_{1}^{2} - 0.8x_{2}^{2} + 0.5x_{1}x_{2}$$
> **Find:** (a) the direction of steepest ascent, and (b) the stationary point and classify it.

---

**Part (a) — Steepest Ascent Direction**

The first-order part of the model gives coefficients $\hat{\beta}_{1}=3.5$ and $\hat{\beta}_{2}=2.0$. The largest in magnitude is $\hat{\beta}_{1}=3.5$, so the unit step is along $x_{1}$.

Choosing λ = 1 coded unit along $x_{1}$, the steps are:

$$\Delta x_{1} = \frac{3.5}{3.5}\times 1 = 1.00 \quad \text{(coded unit)}$$

$$\Delta x_{2} = \frac{2.0}{3.5}\times 1 \approx 0.5714 \quad \text{(coded unit)}$$

So the steepest ascent path is the ray from the origin with slope $\Delta x_{2}/\Delta x_{1} \approx 0.5714$.

---

**Part (b) — Stationary Point**

Identify the linear coefficient vector **b** and the quadratic matrix **B**:

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.2 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & -0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the inverse of **B**:

$$\det(\mathbf{B}) = (-1.2)(-0.8) - (0.25)(0.25) = 0.96 - 0.0625 = 0.8975$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.8975}\begin{bmatrix} -0.8 & -0.25 \\ -0.25 & -1.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.8914 & -0.2785 \\ -0.2785 & -1.3370 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the stationary point:

$$\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = -\frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -0.8914 & -0.2785 \\ -0.2785 & -1.3370 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 3.5 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplication of matrix with vector:

$$x_{1,s} = -\frac{1}{2}\bigl[(-0.8914)(3.5) + (-0.2785)(2.0)\bigr] = -\frac{1}{2}\bigl[-3.1199 - 0.5570\bigr] = -\frac{1}{2}(-3.6769)$$

$$x_{1,s} \approx 1.8385$$

$$x_{2,s} = -\frac{1}{2}\bigl[(-0.2785)(3.5) + (-1.3370)(2.0)\bigr] = -\frac{1}{2}\bigl[-0.9748 - 2.6740\bigr] = -\frac{1}{2}(-3.6488)$$

$$x_{2,s} \approx 1.8244$$

The stationary point in coded units is therefore:

$$\mathbf{x}_{s} \approx (1.84,\; 1.82)$$

---

**Classification via Eigenvalues**

Eigenvalues of **B** are obtained from $\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$(-1.2 - \lambda)(-0.8 - \lambda) - 0.0625 = 0$$

$$\lambda^{2} + 2.0\lambda + 0.96 - 0.0625 = 0$$

$$\lambda^{2} + 2.0\lambda + 0.8975 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{-2.0 \pm \sqrt{4.0 - 3.5900}}{2} = \frac{-2.0 \pm \sqrt{0.4100}}{2} = \frac{-2.0 \pm 0.6403}{2}$$

$$\lambda_{1} \approx -0.6799, \quad \lambda_{2} \approx -1.3201$$

**Both eigenvalues are negative → the stationary point is a MAXIMUM.**

---

**Predicted Response at the Stationary Point**

Using $\hat{y}_{s} = \beta_{0} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b}$:

$$\hat{y}_{s} = 45.0 + \frac{1}{2}(1.8385 \times 3.5 + 1.8244 \times 2.0) = 45.0 + \frac{1}{2}(6.4348 + 3.6488)$$

$$\hat{y}_{s} = 45.0 + 5.0418 \approx 50.04$$

**Conclusion:** The model predicts a **maximum response of ≈ 50.04** at the coded point **(1.84, 1.82)**, which is then de-coded into actual engineering units (e.g., 168 °C, 2.5 bar) for confirmation runs.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fit 2<sup>k</sup> + centre points | First-order coefficients $\hat{\beta}_{i}$ |
| 2 | Lack-of-Fit test + curvature check | Decision: continue steepest ascent OR fit second-order |
| 3 | Steepest ascent | Sequence of new points to explore |
| 4 | Fit CCD or BBD around new region | Second-order coefficients |
| 5 | Solve $\nabla\hat{y}=0$ | Stationary point $\mathbf{x}_{s}$ |
| 6 | Eigenvalue test on **B** | Max / Min / Saddle classification |
| 7 | Confirmation runs | Validates predicted optimum |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RSM Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Engineering Problem Definition] --> B[Identify Quantitative Factors and Responses]
    B --> C{Screening Complete?}
    C -- No --> D[Run 2k or 2k-p Factorial with Center Points]
    C -- Yes --> E[Stage I First Order Model]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Is Curvature Significant?}
    F -- No --> G[Apply Method of Steepest Ascent]
    G --> H[New Operating Region Located]
    H --> I[Run CCD or Box Behnken Design]
    F -- Yes --> I
    I --> J[Stage II Second Order Model Fitting]
    J --> K[ANOVA and R Square Validation]
    K --> L{Model Adequate?}
    L -- No --> M[Refit with Variable Transformation]
    M --> J
    L -- Yes --> N[Solve Gradient equals Zero for Stationary Point]
    N --> O[Eigenvalue Analysis of B Matrix]
    O --> P{Stationary Point Type}
    P -- Maximum --> Q[Confirmation Runs at Optimum]
    P -- Minimum --> Q
    P -- Saddle --> R[Re-Optimize by Ridge Analysis]
    R --> N
    Q --> S[Industrial Scale Implementation]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map — Where RSM Fits in DOE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DOE Family] --> B[Comparative Experiments]
    A --> C[Factorial Designs 2k 2k-p]
    A --> D[Response Surface Methodology RSM]
    A --> E[Mixture Designs]
    D --> D1[First Order Models]
    D --> D2[Steepest Ascent]
    D --> D3[Second Order CCD]
    D --> D4[Box Behnken]
    D --> D5[Canonical Analysis]
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D3 --> D5
```

### 4.3 Geometric Interpretation of the Stationary Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Second Order Response Surface] --> R[Contour Plot in x1 x2 plane]
    R --> S{Shape of Contours}
    S -- Concentric Closed Ellipses --> M1[Maximum or Minimum]
    S -- Hyperbolic Curves --> M2[Saddle Point]
    S -- Parallel Lines --> M3[Ridge System]
    M1 --> T1{B both eigenvalues same sign}
    T1 -- Negative --> X1[Maximum]
    T1 -- Positive --> X2[Minimum]
    M2 --> Y1[Re-optimize via Ridge Analysis]
```

### 4.4 Relationship Between First-Order and Second-Order Designs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[2k Factorial with center points] --> Q1[Test Curvature via Center Points]
    Q1 --> R1{Is Curvature Significant at alpha 0.05}
    R1 -- No --> S1[Steepest Ascent Path]
    S1 --> T1[New Region]
    T1 --> U1[Augment with Axial Points to form CCD]
    R1 -- Yes --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Fit Full Second Order Model]
    V1 --> W1[Stationary Point and Canonical Analysis]
```
