## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define Response Surface Methodology. State any two of its applications in chemical engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Definition (2 marks):* Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques used for developing, improving, and optimising processes in which a response of interest is influenced by several quantitative independent variables. It was introduced by Box and Wilson in 1951 and combines the principles of experimental design, regression modelling, and optimisation.

*Applications (½ + ½ = 1 mark):*
1. Maximising the **yield** of a chemical reaction by optimising temperature, pressure, and catalyst loading.
2. Minimising the **particle size** of a pharmaceutical granule by tuning impeller speed, binder concentration, and granulation time.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Distinguish between a first-order model and a second-order model in RSM. When is each used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | First-Order Model | Second-Order Model |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Linear in factors: $y=\beta_{0}+\sum\beta_{i}x_{i}+\varepsilon$ | Includes curvature: $y=\beta_{0}+\sum\beta_{i}x_{i}+\sum\beta_{ii}x_{i}^{2}+\sum\beta_{ij}x_{i}x_{j}+\varepsilon$ |
| Region | **Far** from the optimum where response is approximately linear | **Near** the optimum where curvature is significant |
| Design used | 2<sup>k</sup> or 2<sup>k-p</sup> factorial with centre points | Central Composite Design (CCD) or Box–Behnken Design |

*When used (1 mark):* First-order when the experimenter is exploring a new region; second-order when curvature is detected (by comparing factorial corners to centre points) and a stationary point must be precisely located.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A two-factor RSM experiment was conducted in a chemical reactor to study the effect of *temperature* ($x_1$, coded) and *catalyst concentration* ($x_2$, coded) on the percentage yield ($y$). The fitted second-order model is:**
$$\hat{y} = 78.0 + 4.2x_1 + 3.0x_2 - 1.5x_1^{2} - 1.0x_2^{2} + 0.6x_1x_2$$

**(a) Determine the direction of steepest ascent. (7 marks)**
**(b) Find the stationary point, classify it using eigenvalues, and compute the predicted maximum yield. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Direction of Steepest Ascent (7 marks)**

The first-order part of the model is $\hat{y} = 78.0 + 4.2x_1 + 3.0x_2$, giving coefficients $\hat{\beta}_1=4.2$ and $\hat{\beta}_2=3.0$.

The **largest absolute coefficient** is $\hat{\beta}_1=4.2$, so the unit step (λ = 1 coded unit) is taken along $x_1$.

The proportional steps are:

$$\Delta x_1 = \frac{4.2}{4.2}\times 1 = 1.00 \text{ coded unit}$$

$$\Delta x_2 = \frac{3.0}{4.2}\times 1 = 0.7143 \text{ coded unit}$$

**Direction of steepest ascent (3 marks):** The path is a ray from the centre of the current design region in the direction of the vector $(\Delta x_1, \Delta x_2) = (1.00,\; 0.7143)$. The slope of this ray in the $x_1$–$x_2$ plane is:

$$\frac{\Delta x_2}{\Delta x_1} = \frac{0.7143}{1.00} = 0.7143$$

**Practical translation (1 mark):** If the coded range was, say, $x_1 \in [-1, +1]$ for $140\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $180\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $x_2 \in [-1, +1]$ for 1% to 3% catalyst, then one coded unit along $x_1$ equals 20 °C and one coded unit along $x_2$ equals 1%, so a single physical step along the steepest ascent is $(20\,^\circ\text{C},\; 0.714\%)$.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Compute new factor levels for the next run: $x_1^{\text{new}} = x_1^{\text{current}} + 1.00$, $x_2^{\text{new}} = x_2^{\text{current}} + 0.7143$.
2. Run the experiment, observe the response.
3. Continue the steps until the response no longer increases; this is the new region of interest for the second-order design.

---

**Part (b) — Stationary Point, Classification, Maximum Yield (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the coefficient vector and matrix (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.2 \\ 3.0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.3 & -1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the inverse of B (2 marks):**

$$\det(\mathbf{B}) = (-1.5)(-1.0) - (0.3)(0.3) = 1.5 - 0.09 = 1.41$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \frac{1}{1.41}\begin{bmatrix} -1.0 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.7092 & -0.2128 \\ -0.2128 & -1.0638 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute the stationary point (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{x}_s = -\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -0.7092 & -0.2128 \\ -0.2128 & -1.0638 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4.2 \\ 3.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$x_{1,s} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(-0.7092)(4.2) + (-0.2128)(3.0)\bigr] = -\tfrac{1}{2}(-2.9786 - 0.6384) = -\tfrac{1}{2}(-3.6170) \approx 1.8085$$

$$x_{2,s} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(-0.2128)(4.2) + (-1.0638)(3.0)\bigr] = -\tfrac{1}{2}(-0.8938 - 3.1914) = -\tfrac{1}{2}(-4.0852) \approx 2.0426$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}_s \approx (1.81,\; 2.04)}$$

**Step 4: Eigenvalue analysis (1 mark):**

Solve $\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$(-1.5 - \lambda)(-1.0 - \lambda) - 0.09 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 + 2.5\lambda + 1.5 - 0.09 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda^2 + 2.5\lambda + 1.41 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{-2.5 \pm \sqrt{6.25 - 5.64}}{2} = \frac{-2.5 \pm \sqrt{0.61}}{2} = \frac{-2.5 \pm 0.7810}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 \approx -0.8595, \quad \lambda_2 \approx -1.6405$$

Both eigenvalues are **negative** → the stationary point is a **MAXIMUM**.

**Step 5: Predicted maximum yield (1 mark):**

$$\hat{y}_s = 78.0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(x_{1,s}\hat{\beta}_1 + x_{2,s}\hat{\beta}_2) = 78.0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(1.8085 \times 4.2 + 2.0426 \times 3.0)$$

$$\hat{y}_s = 78.0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(7.5957 + 6.1278) = 78.0 + 6.8618 \approx \boxed{84.86\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) List the sequential steps involved in Response Surface Methodology. Explain with a flowchart how a first-order model is upgraded to a second-order model when curvature is detected. (7 marks)**

**(b) A 2<sup>2</sup> factorial design with 5 centre points was run in a foundry to study the effect of pouring temperature ($x_1$) and mould hardness ($x_2$) on the tensile strength (MPa) of a cast iron component. The fitted first-order model is $\hat{y} = 320 + 12x_1 + 8x_2$ (coded). Suggest the steepest ascent path, assuming the unit step is taken along the variable with the larger coefficient, and recommend 3 follow-up experimental points. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Sequential Steps in RSM (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 – Problem definition and objective:** Clearly specify the response variable (e.g., yield) and the quantitative factors (e.g., temperature, pressure). Define the optimisation goal: maximise, minimise, or hit a target. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 – Selection of factors and levels:** Choose the *k* factors and their *low (−1)* and *high (+1)* levels. Factors should be quantitative and controllable. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 – Choice of experimental design:** A 2<sup>k</sup> factorial (or 2<sup>k-p</sup> fractional) with *n<sub>c</sub>* centre points is most common for the first-order stage. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 – Conduct experiments and record data:** Runs are randomised to protect against lurking variables. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 – Fit a first-order model and check curvature:** Compute the average response at the factorial points $\bar{y}_F$ and at the centre points $\bar{y}_C$. Test $H_0: \beta_{ii}=0$ using $t$-test. If significant curvature is detected, proceed to Step 7. If not, proceed to Step 6. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 6 – Method of steepest ascent:** Move along the direction $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$ normalised to the largest coefficient, perform sequential experiments until the response declines. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 7 – Fit a second-order model:** In the new region, augment the design with **axial (star) points** to form a **Central Composite Design (CCD)** — this allows estimation of all quadratic and interaction terms. Fit $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii}x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij}x_ix_j$. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 8 – Locate the stationary point and classify:** Solve $\nabla \hat{y} = 0$ to obtain $\mathbf{x}_s$, then perform canonical analysis (eigenvalues of **B** matrix) to classify as maximum, minimum, or saddle. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 9 – Confirmation runs:** Perform experiments at $\mathbf{x}_s$ (or ridge path) to validate the model. *(0.5 mark)*

**Flowchart of first-order → second-order transition:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start with 2k Factorial and Center Points] --> Q[Fit First Order Model]
    Q --> R{Curvature Significant?}
    R -- No --> S[Steepest Ascent]
    S --> T[New Operating Region]
    T --> U[Augment with Axial Points]
    R -- Yes --> U
    U --> V[Fit Second Order Model CCD]
    V --> W[Solve Gradient equals Zero]
    W --> X[Canonical Analysis and Optimum]
```

---

**Part (b) — Steepest Ascent in the Foundry (7 marks)**

**Given:**
First-order model: $\hat{y} = 320 + 12x_1 + 8x_2$ (coded units)

First-order coefficients: $\hat{\beta}_1 = 12$, $\hat{\beta}_2 = 8$.

**Step 1: Identify the largest coefficient (1 mark):**
The largest in magnitude is $\hat{\beta}_1 = 12$. So the unit step is taken along $x_1$.

**Step 2: Compute the step size (2 marks):**
Choosing λ = 1 coded unit along $x_1$:

$$\Delta x_1 = \frac{12}{12}\times 1 = 1.00 \text{ coded unit}$$

$$\Delta x_2 = \frac{8}{12}\times 1 = 0.6667 \text{ coded unit}$$

**Step 3: Direction of steepest ascent (1 mark):**
The direction vector is $(1.00,\; 0.6667)$, i.e., the slope in the $x_1$–$x_2$ plane is $0.6667/1.00 = 0.6667$.

**Step 4: Recommend 3 follow-up points (3 marks):**
Starting from the centre of the current design $(0, 0)$, three experimental points along the path are:

| Point | $x_1$ (coded) | $x_2$ (coded) | Actual interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0 + 1.00 = 1.00$ | $0 + 0.667 = 0.67$ | Higher pouring temp, slightly higher mould hardness |
| 2 | $0 + 2.00 = 2.00$ | $0 + 1.333 = 1.33$ | Even higher temp and hardness |
| 3 | $0 + 3.00 = 3.00$ | $0 + 2.000 = 2.00$ | Further along the path |

**Decision rule (included for full marks):** Continue the steepest ascent as long as the response $y$ keeps increasing. The first point where the response decreases indicates the new region of interest, around which a second-order design (CCD) will be built.

**Practical translation (optional, for completeness):** If $x_1 \in [-1, +1]$ corresponds to pouring temperature 1380 °C to 1420 °C, and $x_2 \in [-1, +1]$ corresponds to mould hardness 80 HRB to 100 HRB, then one coded unit of $x_1$ = 20 °C, and the steps become $(20\,^\circ\text{C},\; 13.3\,\text{HRB})$, $(40\,^\circ\text{C},\; 26.6\,\text{HRB})$, and $(60\,^\circ\text{C},\; 40\,\text{HRB})$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in RSM questions:**
> 1. **Misnaming the steepest ascent direction.** Students often give the raw coefficients $(12,\; 8)$ instead of the *normalised* direction $(1.00,\; 0.667)$. KTU examiners expect the **normalised** form: **−1 mark penalty** is typical.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of ½ in $\mathbf{x}_s = -\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.** A frequent algebraic slip. Recheck your derivation.
> 3. **Using B with entries $\beta_{12}$ instead of $\beta_{12}/2$ in the cross-term position.** The matrix form of the second-order model is $\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$, so the off-diagonal entries are half the regression coefficient. **−1 mark** if wrong.
> 4. **Confusing eigenvalue signs.** Both eigenvalues *negative* → maximum; both *positive* → minimum; opposite signs → saddle. **−1 to −2 marks** for misclassification.
> 5. **Skipping the confirmation-run step.** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests whether students know that RSM is an *iterative empirical* procedure — the optimum is *predicted*, then *verified*.
> 6. **Lack-of-Fit test is mandatory.** Any fitted model must be reported with R<sup>2</sup>, R<sup>2</sup><sub>(adj)</sub>, and the lack-of-fit p-value. A model with significant lack-of-fit (p < 0.05) is **inadequate** — full marks require all three diagnostics.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — RSM: Introduction**
> - **Originators:** Box and Wilson, **1951**.
> - **Purpose:** Empirical modelling and optimisation of processes with multiple quantitative factors.
> - **Two stages:** *First-order* (linear, 2<sup>k</sup> + centre) → *Second-order* (quadratic, CCD or BBD).
> - **First-order model:** $y=\beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$.
> - **Second-order model:** $y=\beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii}x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij}x_i x_j + \varepsilon$.
> - **OLS estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ — the engine of all RSM fitting.
> - **Curvature detection:** Compare $\bar{y}_F$ (factorial corners) with $\bar{y}_C$ (centre points) using a *t*-test.
> - **Steepest ascent direction:** Normalise coefficients by the largest; steps are $\Delta x_i = \hat{\beta}_i / \vert\hat{\beta}_{\max}\vert \cdot \lambda$.
> - **Stationary point formula (k=2):** $\mathbf{x}_s = -\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
> - **Eigenvalue rule:** both negative → max; both positive → min; opposite signs → saddle.
> - **Predicted optimum response:** $\hat{y}_s = \beta_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T\mathbf{b}$.
> - **Key designs:** Central Composite Design (CCD) and Box–Behnken Design (BBD) — *memorise the CCD structure* ($\alpha = 2^{k/4}$ for rotatability).
> - **Diagnostics:** R<sup>2</sup> ≥ 0.90, R<sup>2</sup><sub>(adj)</sub> close to R<sup>2</sup>, lack-of-fit p > 0.05.
> - **Confirmation runs are mandatory** — never end an RSM study without them.
> - **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** Expect a 7-mark problem on locating and classifying a stationary point, plus a 3-mark definition or distinction question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Steepest Ascent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Method of Steepest Ascent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Method of Steepest Ascent (MSA)** is a sequential experimental optimization procedure employed within **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** to efficiently traverse the experimental region from a current operating point toward the neighborhood of the optimal response. It is the natural follow-up procedure after a first-order (linear) model has been fit to data collected via a **2<sup>k</sup> Factorial Design** (with or without center points), and the analysis has revealed that the **first-order model is adequate** (i.e., curvature is absent) and we are operating far from the region of the optimum.

Mathematically, the method exploits the **gradient vector** of the fitted first-order response surface. Movement is made in the direction of this gradient, scaled by a chosen step size, because the gradient always points in the direction of maximum instantaneous increase of the response function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The Method of Steepest Ascent sits at the intersection of **Module 4 (Response Surface Methodology)** and the prior factorial design modules. It is a *first-order* optimization tool — used **before** a second-order (quadratic) model is entertained.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountainside at night, and you want to climb to the highest peak. You cannot see far, but you can feel the **slope of the ground directly under your feet**. The steepest direction your foot feels is the direction in which the height increases most rapidly. So you take a step in that direction, reassess the local slope, and keep walking. You keep doing this until the ground starts to level off or slope downward — at that point, you know you are near the summit and must change strategy (switch to a more detailed quadratic model).

- **The mountain** = the true (unknown) response surface $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Your feet's slope reading** = the estimated gradient $\nabla \hat{y}$ from the first-order model.
- **The step you take** = the experimental run along the steepest-ascent direction.
- **The summit (or a flat region)** = the optimum, where curvature must be modeled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Intuition:** In a chemical reactor study, the "current point" might be $T = 80^\circ\text{C}$, $p = 2\text{ atm}$ with 70% yield. Steepest ascent tells the engineer: *"increase temperature by 2°C for every 1 atm of pressure to push yield upward fastest."*

### 1.3 Why Is the Method Needed?

After running a $2^k$ design, the experimenter often finds:
1. The first-order model fits (no significant curvature).
2. Some main effects are non-zero (i.e., the response is changing).
3. The current point is **not** the optimum (since main effects still exist).

The natural question is: **"Where do I run my next experiment?"** Running a *new* factorial design centered elsewhere wastes information. Instead, MSA uses the *same* fitted linear model to point the way, performing a **sequence of single experiments** that march toward the optimum.

| Situation | Recommended Tool |
|---|---|
| First-order adequate, **far from optimum** | **Method of Steepest Ascent** |
| First-order adequate, **near optimum** (no further gain) | Re-fit $2^k$ at new center, then MSA again |
| Curvature present | Move to **second-order model** (Central Composite / Box–Behnken) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fitted First-Order Model

Assume $k$ quantitative input variables and a single response $y$. The fitted first-order model is:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$$

where the intercept $\hat{\beta}_0$ and slopes $\hat{\beta}_i$ are estimated by **least squares** using the $2^k$ (or $2^{k-p}$ fractional) design data.

The **gradient** (vector of partial derivatives) of this linear surface is constant — it does not depend on the location $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\nabla \hat{y} = \left( \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k} \right) = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a constant gradient?** Because the surface is a *hyperplane*. Its slope is the same everywhere — so the steepest-ascent direction is the same everywhere on the plane. This is why MSA is a *linear* strategy.

### 2.2 The Direction of Steepest Ascent

For a function $f(\mathbf{x})$, the unit vector along the direction of steepest ascent is the **normalized gradient**:

$$\mathbf{d} = \frac{\nabla \hat{y}}{\lVert \nabla \hat{y} \rVert}$$

For the linear model, this direction is:

$$\mathbf{d} \propto (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$$

The **sign convention** is:
- **Positive coefficient** $\hat{\beta}_i > 0$ → move in the **positive** $x_i$ direction.
- **Negative coefficient** $\hat{\beta}_i < 0$ → move in the **negative** $x_i$ direction.

The **method of steepest descent** is the analog for minimization (move opposite to the gradient); the two are mathematically identical up to sign.

### 2.3 Step Size (λ) Selection — Engineering Procedure

In the **coded variable space** (where $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$), it is conventional to:

1. Choose the variable having the **largest absolute regression coefficient** $\vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max}$.
2. Fix a step of **one coded unit** ($\Delta x_i = 1$) for that variable.
3. Compute proportional steps for all other variables:

$$\Delta x_j = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\hat{\beta}_i} \cdot \Delta x_i \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$

4. Convert these coded steps to **natural (uncoded) units** using the change transformation:

$$\Delta x_j^{\text{natural}} = \Delta x_j^{\text{coded}} \cdot \left( \frac{x_{j,\text{high}} - x_{j,\text{low}}}{2} \right)$$

5. Generate a **path of experimental points**:

$$x_{i,\text{new}} = x_{i,\text{current}} + \lambda \cdot \Delta x_i^{\text{natural}}, \quad \lambda = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

### 2.4 Stopping Rule

The experimenter walks along the path, conducting one experiment per step, and plots the response $\hat{y}$ versus step number $\lambda$. The procedure terminates when:

- The response **decreases** (or shows no improvement) over consecutive steps.
- This signals that we have passed the optimum and the **first-order model is no longer adequate** — curvature has set in.
- The new best point becomes the **center of a new $2^k$ design**, and a **second-order model** is then fit.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Fitted first-order model | $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ | Linear hyperplane in $k$ variables |
| 2 | Gradient of fitted model | $\nabla \hat{y} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$ | Constant vector |
| 3 | Unit steepest-ascent direction | $\mathbf{d} = \nabla \hat{y} \,/\, \lVert \nabla \hat{y} \rVert$ | $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ is the Euclidean norm |
| 4 | Proportional coded step (variable $j$) | $\Delta x_j = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\hat{\beta}_{\max}} \cdot \Delta x_{\max}$ | Use $\Delta x_{\max} = 1$ in coded units |
| 5 | Natural-unit step | $\Delta x_j^{\text{nat}} = \Delta x_j^{\text{coded}} \cdot \dfrac{x_{j,\text{high}} - x_{j,\text{low}}}{2}$ | Half the variable's range |
| 6 | New experimental point | $x_{j,\text{new}} = x_{j,\text{current}} + \lambda \cdot \Delta x_j^{\text{nat}}$ | $\lambda = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ |
| 7 | Predicted response at new point | $\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_i \hat{\beta}_i \, x_{i,\text{new}}$ | Used as a sanity check |
| 8 | Stopping rule | $\hat{y}(\lambda+1) \le \hat{y}(\lambda)$ for two consecutive steps | Indicates curvature region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Recovery Tip:** Many students lose marks by writing the *natural-unit* step formula incorrectly. Always remember: natural change = (coded change) × (half the range of the variable in natural units).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Engineering:** Optimizing yield by simultaneously adjusting temperature, pressure, catalyst loading.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Maximizing dissolution rate by tuning excipient ratios.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Maximizing wafer throughput by tuning etch time, RF power, gas flow.
- **Food Technology:** Maximizing consumer-acceptance score by tuning baking time, sugar level, oven temperature.
- **Mechanical / Materials:** Maximizing fatigue life by tuning heat-treatment temperature and time.

In all of these, the **first-order model is locally valid**, so MSA gives the fastest path (in expectation) to the optimum region with the fewest additional runs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Gradient Gives the Direction of Steepest Ascent

Consider the fitted model $\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$. We want to find a unit vector $\mathbf{d} = (d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k)$ that **maximizes the directional derivative** of $\hat{y}$ at a point $\mathbf{x}_0$.

The directional derivative of $\hat{y}$ in the direction $\mathbf{d}$ is:

$$D_{\mathbf{d}} \hat{y} = \nabla \hat{y} \cdot \mathbf{d} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i d_i$$

The optimization problem is:

$$\max_{\mathbf{d}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i d_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \lVert \mathbf{d} \rVert^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i^2 = 1$$

**Step 1 — Set up the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(d_1, \ldots, d_k, \mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i d_i - \mu \left( \sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i^2 - 1 \right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $d_i$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_i} = \hat{\beta}_i - 2 \mu d_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{2 \mu}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constraint $\sum d_i^2 = 1$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{\hat{\beta}_i^2}{4 \mu^2} = \frac{1}{4 \mu^2} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2 = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2} = \frac{\lVert \nabla \hat{y} \rVert}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the expression for $d_i$:**

$$d_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{2 \mu} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\lVert \nabla \hat{y} \rVert}$$

Hence the optimal unit direction is:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{d}^* = \frac{(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)}{\sqrt{\hat{\beta}_1^2 + \hat{\beta}_2^2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_k^2}}}$$

This proves that the **gradient direction (normalized)** is the unique direction of steepest ascent on a linear surface. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example (2-Variable Case) — Full KTU-Style Solution

**Problem Statement (synthesized, KTU-flavor):**

A chemical engineer investigates the effect of **reaction time** $x_1$ (hours) and **catalyst concentration** $x_2$ (mass-%) on the **percentage yield** $y$ of a target compound. A $2^2$ factorial design with two center points is run. The fitted first-order model (in *coded* variables $X_1$, $X_2$) is:

$$\hat{y} = 65.0 + 4.5 X_1 + 2.0 X_2$$

The natural-variable ranges used in the design are:
- $x_1 \in [1, 3]$ hours (so $x_1$ low $= 1$, $x_1$ high $= 3$)
- $x_2 \in [5, 15]$ mass-% (so $x_2$ low $= 5$, $x_2$ high $= 15$)

The center of the design is at $(x_1, x_2) = (2, 10)$. There is no evidence of curvature. Construct the path of steepest ascent.

---

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the gradient (regression coefficients).**

$$\nabla \hat{y} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2) = (4.5, 2.0)$$

The ratio of slopes is $4.5 : 2.0$. The variable with the largest absolute coefficient is $X_1$ (since $\vert 4.5 \vert > \vert 2.0 \vert$).

**Step 2 — Set the coded step for the dominant variable.**

Choose $\Delta X_1 = 1$ (one coded unit). Then:

$$\Delta X_2 = \frac{\hat{\beta}_2}{\hat{\beta}_1} \cdot \Delta X_1 = \frac{2.0}{4.5} \times 1 = 0.4444$$

So in **coded** units, the direction of steepest ascent is $(1, 0.4444)$.

**Step 3 — Convert coded steps to natural (engineering) units.**

Half-ranges of the variables:
- $x_1$: half-range $= (3 - 1)/2 = 1$ hour
- $x_2$: half-range $= (15 - 5)/2 = 5$ mass-%

Natural steps:
- $\Delta x_1^{\text{nat}} = 1 \times 1 = 1$ hour
- $\Delta x_2^{\text{nat}} = 0.4444 \times 5 = 2.222$ mass-%

**Step 4 — Construct the path.**

Starting from the design center $(x_1, x_2) = (2, 10)$, the experimental points along the path are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda = 0: \quad & (x_1, x_2) = (2.000, \ 10.000), \quad \hat{y} = 65.0 \\
\lambda = 1: \quad & (x_1, x_2) = (3.000, \ 12.222), \quad \hat{y} = 65.0 + 4.5(1) + 2.0(0.4444) = 70.39 \\
\lambda = 2: \quad & (x_1, x_2) = (4.000, \ 14.444), \quad \hat{y} = 65.0 + 4.5(2) + 2.0(0.8889) = 75.78 \\
\lambda = 3: \quad & (x_1, x_2) = (5.000, \ 16.667), \quad \hat{y} = 65.0 + 4.5(3) + 2.0(1.3333) = 81.17 \\
\lambda = 4: \quad & (x_1, x_2) = (6.000, \ 18.889), \quad \hat{y} = 65.0 + 4.5(4) + 2.0(1.7778) = 86.56
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Predicted response column (model-based).**

| Step $\lambda$ | $x_1$ (hr) | $x_2$ (mass-%) | $\hat{y}$ (model) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 2.000 | 10.000 | 65.00 |
| 1 | 3.000 | 12.222 | 70.39 |
| 2 | 4.000 | 14.444 | 75.78 |
| 3 | 5.000 | 16.667 | 81.17 |
| 4 | 6.000 | 18.889 | 86.56 |

**Step 6 — Engineering judgment on step size.**

The path above uses one coded unit of $X_1$ per step. In practice, this may be too large. The engineer inspects the experimental region: $x_1$ is allowed to vary in $[1, 3]$ in the original design; a step of 1 hour is the **full half-range** of the design. To stay within physically reasonable bounds, the engineer might instead take $\lambda = 0.25$ steps:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta x_1^{\text{nat, scaled}} &= 0.25 \times 1 = 0.25 \text{ hr} \\
\Delta x_2^{\text{nat, scaled}} &= 0.25 \times 2.222 = 0.5556 \text{ mass-}\%
\end{aligned}$$

The engineer runs the experiments, plots observed $y$ vs. $\lambda$, and stops when $y$ no longer increases.

**Step 7 — Stopping and pivot to second-order model.**

Suppose the observed $y$ peaks at $\lambda = 3$ (i.e., the third new run) and then declines. The center of a **new $2^2$ factorial design** is set at that point, and a **second-order model** (with interaction and quadratic terms) is fit, leading eventually to a Central Composite Design for the final optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow of the Method of Steepest Ascent

The figure below depicts the complete procedural flow of the Method of Steepest Ascent as employed in practice. Note: every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with a letter to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts, and all node labels are raw uppercase text (no markdown formatting).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START: 2K FACTORIAL DESIGN RUN] --> A2[FIT FIRST ORDER MODEL]
    A2 --> A3{CURVATURE TEST: SIGNIFICANT?}
    A3 -->|YES| A4[SWITCH TO SECOND ORDER MODEL: CCD OR BOX BEHNKEN]
    A3 -->|NO| A5[CONFIRM FIRST ORDER ADEQUATE]
    A5 --> A6[COMPUTE GRADIENT: REGRESSION COEFFICIENTS]
    A6 --> A7[IDENTIFY DOMINANT VARIABLE: LARGEST ABS BETA]
    A7 --> A8[CHOOSE CODED STEP: DELTA X MAX EQUALS 1]
    A8 --> A9[COMPUTE PROPORTIONAL CODED STEPS FOR ALL OTHER VARIABLES]
    A9 --> A10[CONVERT CODED STEPS TO NATURAL UNITS]
    A10 --> A11[GENERATE PATH POINTS: X NEW EQUALS X CURRENT PLUS LAMBDA TIMES DELTA]
    A11 --> A12[RUN EXPERIMENT AT LAMBDA EQUALS 1]
    A12 --> A13{OBSERVED Y INCREASES?}
    A13 -->|YES| A14[MOVE TO NEXT LAMBDA: LAMBDA EQUALS LAMBDA PLUS 1]
    A14 --> A13
    A13 -->|NO: Y DECREASES OR PLATEAUS| A15[STOP: NEAR OPTIMUM OR CURVATURE REGION]
    A15 --> A16[NEW DESIGN CENTER AT BEST POINT]
    A16 --> A17[FIT SECOND ORDER RESPONSE SURFACE]
    A17 --> A18[ESTIMATE OPTIMUM VIA CANONICAL OR RIDGE ANALYSIS]
    A18 --> A19[END: CONFIRM WITH CONFIRMATORY RUNS]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Picture (Schematic)

The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** showing how the gradient, the design center, and the steepest-ascent path relate to one another in the input space, since a true 3D perspective drawing cannot be rendered in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[DESIGN CENTER POINT X0] --> B2[FITTED FIRST ORDER HYPERPLANE]
    B2 --> B3[GRADIENT VECTOR: BETA 1, BETA 2, ..., BETA K]
    B3 --> B4[NORMALIZED DIRECTION: D STAR EQUALS BETA DIVIDED BY NORM BETA]
    B4 --> B5[STEP SIZE: LAMBDA TIMES NATURAL DELTA X]
    B5 --> B6[NEW POINT: X1 AT LAMBDA EQUALS 1]
    B6 --> B7[NEW POINT: X2 AT LAMBDA EQUALS 2]
    B7 --> B8[NEW POINT: X3 AT LAMBDA EQUALS 3]
    B8 --> B9{PEAK OBSERVED RESPONSE}
    B9 --> B10[BEST POINT BECOMES NEW DESIGN CENTER]
    B10 --> B11[FIT SECOND ORDER MODEL]
    B11 --> B12[OPTIMUM ESTIMATION]
```

### 4.3 Step-Size Selection Logic (Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[CHOOSE DOMINANT VARIABLE J MAX WITH LARGEST ABS BETA J] --> C2[SET DELTA X J MAX CODED EQUALS 1]
    C2 --> C3[FOR EACH OTHER VARIABLE J: COMPUTE DELTA X J CODED EQUALS BETA J DIVIDED BY BETA J MAX]
    C3 --> C4[MULTIPLY EACH CODED DELTA BY HALF RANGE TO GET NATURAL DELTA]
    C4 --> C5[OPTIONAL: SCALE PATH BY 0.25 OR 0.5 FACTOR TO STAY WITHIN DESIGN REGION]
    C5 --> C6[OUTPUT: TABLE OF X1 NEW, X2 NEW, ..., XK NEW FOR LAMBDA EQUALS 1, 2, 3, ...]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**"Define the Method of Steepest Ascent. Under what conditions in Response Surface Methodology is this method employed?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Method of Steepest Ascent** is a sequential procedure in Response Surface Methodology used to move an experimenter from the current operating region toward the optimum of the response variable, by performing experiments along the **gradient direction** of a fitted first-order response surface.

It is employed when **all three** of the following conditions are satisfied:
1. A **first-order model** $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ has been fit using a $2^k$ (or fractional) factorial design.
2. The **lack-of-fit (curvature) test is non-significant**, indicating the first-order model is adequate locally.
3. The experimenter is **far from the optimum**, i.e., main effects are large and the response is still changing with movement in the design space.

When these conditions hold, MSA gives the most rapid (in terms of expected improvement per run) trajectory to the optimum region.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**"What is the relationship between the regression coefficients of a first-order model and the direction of steepest ascent? State the formula for the unit direction vector."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a fitted first-order model $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$, the **gradient vector** of the response surface is the vector of regression coefficients:

$$\nabla \hat{y} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$$

The unit vector pointing in the direction of steepest ascent is the gradient normalized to unit length:

$$\mathbf{d}^* = \frac{(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)}{\sqrt{\hat{\beta}_1^2 + \hat{\beta}_2^2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_k^2}}$$

The sign of each $\hat{\beta}_i$ determines the **sense** of the movement (positive coefficient → increase $x_i$; negative coefficient → decrease $x_i$), while the **ratio** of the coefficients determines the **relative** magnitudes of the steps in each variable.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the direction of steepest ascent for a fitted first-order response surface model with $k$ input variables. State clearly the optimization problem and show all steps of the constrained maximization.

**(b) [7 marks]** A $2^2$ factorial design with two center points was conducted to study the effect of **temperature** $x_1$ ($^\circ$C) and **pressure** $x_2$ (bar) on the **conversion percentage** $y$ of a chemical reaction. The fitted first-order model in **coded** variables is:

$$\hat{y} = 72.5 + 3.2 X_1 - 1.5 X_2$$

The natural-variable ranges are: $x_1 \in [80, 100]$ °C, $x_2 \in [1, 3]$ bar. The design center is $(x_1, x_2) = (90, 2)$. Construct the steepest-ascent path for $\lambda = 1, 2, 3$ and list the new experimental conditions in natural units.

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the model and the goal (1 mark):**

The fitted first-order model is $\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$. We want to find a unit vector $\mathbf{d} = (d_1, \ldots, d_k)$ that maximizes the directional derivative at any point.

**Step 2 — Set up the constrained optimization (1 mark):**

$$\max_{\mathbf{d}} D_{\mathbf{d}} \hat{y} = \max_{\mathbf{d}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i d_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i^2 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Form the Lagrangian (1 mark):**

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i d_i - \mu \left( \sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i^2 - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_i} = \hat{\beta}_i - 2 \mu d_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{2 \mu}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the unit-length constraint (1 mark):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i^2 = \frac{1}{4 \mu^2} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final formula (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{d}^* = \frac{(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)}{\sqrt{\hat{\beta}_1^2 + \hat{\beta}_2^2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_k^2}}}$$

This is the unique unit direction of steepest ascent on a linear response surface.

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify regression coefficients and dominant variable (1 mark):**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = +3.2, \quad \hat{\beta}_2 = -1.5$$

The dominant variable is $X_1$ (larger $\vert \hat{\beta} \vert = 3.2$).

**Step 2 — Set coded step of dominant variable (1 mark):**

$$\Delta X_1^{\text{coded}} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Proportional coded step for $X_2$ (1 mark):**

$$\Delta X_2^{\text{coded}} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_2}{\hat{\beta}_1} \times \Delta X_1^{\text{coded}} = \frac{-1.5}{3.2} \times 1 = -0.46875$$

(Negative sign: $X_2$ should *decrease* as $X_1$ increases.)

**Step 4 — Convert to natural units (1 mark):**

Half-ranges:
- $x_1$: $(100 - 80)/2 = 10$ °C
- $x_2$: $(3 - 1)/2 = 1$ bar

Natural steps:
- $\Delta x_1^{\text{nat}} = 1 \times 10 = 10$ °C
- $\Delta x_2^{\text{nat}} = -0.46875 \times 1 = -0.46875$ bar

**Step 5 — Construct the path (3 marks):**

| $\lambda$ | $x_1$ (°C) | $x_2$ (bar) | $\hat{y}$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 90.000 | 2.0000 | 72.50 |
| 1 | 100.000 | 1.5313 | 72.50 + 3.2(1) + (-1.5)(-0.4688) = 76.70 |
| 2 | 110.000 | 1.0625 | 80.90 |
| 3 | 120.000 | 0.5938 | 85.10 |

**Engineering Note (1 mark):** Beyond $\lambda = 1$, the temperature exceeds the original design's upper bound (100°C) and the pressure approaches the lower bound (1 bar). The engineer should *rescale* the path (e.g., use $\lambda$ as a *fractional* multiplier of the natural step) to keep experiments within the feasible operating region, OR continue the path with the understanding that extrapolation is permissible for RSM.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the complete procedural steps of the Method of Steepest Ascent with the help of a flowchart. Discuss the criteria for terminating the procedure and the subsequent action to be taken.

**(b) [7 marks]** A $2^3$ factorial design was conducted to study the effect of three factors on the **tensile strength** (MPa) of a polymer composite. The fitted first-order model in coded variables is:

$$\hat{y} = 45.0 + 2.8 X_1 + 1.6 X_2 - 0.9 X_3$$

Construct the direction of steepest ascent in (i) coded units and (ii) natural units, given the variable ranges:
- $x_1 \in [100, 200]$ (fiber loading, mass parts)
- $x_2 \in [5, 15]$ (curing time, hours)
- $x_3 \in [20, 40]$ (molding temperature, °C)

Also compute the predicted response at $\lambda = 2$ along the path.

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Pre-requisites (1 mark):** Fit a first-order model using a $2^k$ (or $2^{k-p}$) factorial design. Verify lack of significant curvature (e.g., by including center points and testing $\beta_{ii}$ via the curvature $F$-test).

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient (1 mark):** The gradient of $\hat{y}$ is the vector $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$.

**Step 3 — Choose step size (1 mark):** Pick the variable $j_{\max}$ with the largest $\vert \hat{\beta}_j \vert$. Set its coded step to 1. Compute the proportional coded step for every other variable $j$:

$$\Delta X_j^{\text{coded}} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\hat{\beta}_{j_{\max}}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to natural units and generate the path (1 mark):** Use $\Delta x_j^{\text{nat}} = \Delta X_j^{\text{coded}} \cdot (x_{j,\text{high}} - x_{j,\text{low}})/2$. New point: $x_{j,\text{new}} = x_{j,\text{center}} + \lambda \cdot \Delta x_j^{\text{nat}}$ for $\lambda = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$.

**Step 5 — Termination criterion (1 mark):** Run experiments along the path. Stop when the observed response no longer increases (decreases or stays flat) for two consecutive steps, indicating that the linear model is no longer adequate — the **region of curvature** has been entered.

**Step 6 — Subsequent action (2 marks):**
- The point yielding the highest observed response becomes the **new center** of a $2^k$ design.
- Fit a **second-order model** $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j$.
- Use a **Central Composite Design (CCD)** or **Box–Behnken Design (BBD)** for efficient second-order fitting.
- Locate the optimum via **canonical analysis** (eigen-decomposition of the quadratic form) or **ridge analysis**.
- Finally, perform **confirmation runs** at the estimated optimum to validate the model.

*Flowchart:* (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes — the same procedural flow applies.)

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the coefficients and dominant variable (1 mark):**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = 2.8, \quad \hat{\beta}_2 = 1.6, \quad \hat{\beta}_3 = -0.9$$

Dominant variable: $X_1$ (largest $\vert \hat{\beta} \vert = 2.8$).

**Step 2 — Coded direction of steepest ascent (2 marks):**

With $\Delta X_1^{\text{coded}} = 1$:

$$\Delta X_2^{\text{coded}} = \frac{1.6}{2.8} = 0.5714$$

$$\Delta X_3^{\text{coded}} = \frac{-0.9}{2.8} = -0.3214$$

Coded direction vector: $(1, \ 0.5714, \ -0.3214)$. The first two factors increase, the third decreases.

**Step 3 — Half-ranges of variables (1 mark):**

- $x_1$: $(200 - 100)/2 = 50$
- $x_2$: $(15 - 5)/2 = 5$
- $x_3$: $(40 - 20)/2 = 10$

**Step 4 — Natural-unit steps (1 mark):**

- $\Delta x_1^{\text{nat}} = 1 \times 50 = 50$
- $\Delta x_2^{\text{nat}} = 0.5714 \times 5 = 2.857$
- $\Delta x_3^{\text{nat}} = -0.3214 \times 10 = -3.214$

**Step 5 — Path and predicted response at $\lambda = 2$ (2 marks):**

At $\lambda = 2$, assuming center is the design center, the new point is:

$$(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (x_{1,0} + 100, \ x_{2,0} + 5.714, \ x_{3,0} - 6.429)$$

(In coded form: $(X_1, X_2, X_3) = (2, \ 1.1428, \ -0.6428)$)

Predicted response:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}(\lambda=2) &= 45.0 + 2.8(2) + 1.6(1.1428) + (-0.9)(-0.6428) \\
&= 45.0 + 5.6 + 1.8285 + 0.5785 \\
&= 53.007 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Compared to the design center prediction $\hat{y}(0) = 45.0$ MPa, the model predicts a **substantial improvement of 8.007 MPa** by moving two steps up the steepest-ascent path — confirming the practical utility of the procedure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $\hat{\beta}_j$:** A negative coefficient (e.g., $\hat{\beta}_3 = -0.9$) means the variable should be *decreased*, not increased. Many students wrongly add a positive step.
> 2. **Skipping the coded-to-natural conversion:** Writing the path only in coded units (e.g., $(1, 0.5714, -0.3214)$) without converting to natural units loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the unit-length constraint in the derivation:** Part (a) of long-answer questions almost always demands the **constrained optimization** with the Lagrangian; do not skip the constraint $\sum d_i^2 = 1$.
> 4. **Using the wrong reference variable:** The dominant variable (largest $\vert \hat{\beta} \vert$) is the one that *defines* $\Delta X = 1$. Forgetting this and normalizing by the smallest coefficient reverses the entire path.
> 5. **Not stating the termination rule:** "When the response decreases" is incomplete — examiners expect "for two consecutive steps" or "when lack of fit becomes significant."
> 6. **Confusing steepest ascent with steepest descent:** Steepest *ascent* is for **maximization**; steepest *descent* is for **minimization**. Both share the same mathematics up to a sign.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** The Method of Steepest Ascent is a *first-order* sequential optimization procedure that moves the experiment along the **gradient** of a fitted linear response surface.
- **Pre-requisites:** (i) $2^k$ (or $2^{k-p}$) factorial design run, (ii) first-order model fit, (iii) lack-of-fit / curvature test non-significant, (iv) operating far from optimum.
- **Gradient of a linear model:** $\nabla \hat{y} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$ — a *constant* vector.
- **Unit direction of steepest ascent:** $\mathbf{d}^* = \nabla \hat{y} \,/\, \lVert \nabla \hat{y} \rVert$.
- **Step-size rule in coded units:** Set the step of the variable with the largest $\vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert$ to 1; other variables take proportional steps $\hat{\beta}_j / \hat{\beta}_{\max}$.
- **Conversion to natural units:** Multiply each coded step by the half-range of the corresponding variable.
- **Path equation:** $x_{j,\text{new}}(\lambda) = x_{j,\text{center}} + \lambda \cdot \Delta x_j^{\text{nat}}$.
- **Stopping rule:** Stop when observed response fails to increase for two consecutive $\lambda$ values.
- **Subsequent action:** Re-center the design at the best observed point and fit a **second-order model** (Central Composite or Box–Behnken).
- **Final stage:** Use **canonical** or **ridge analysis** on the second-order surface to locate the optimum, then perform **confirmation runs**.
- **Engineering watch-points:** Avoid extrapolating far outside the original design region; rescale the step factor if necessary (e.g., use $\lambda \in \{0.25, 0.5\}$); respect physical constraints (safety, equipment limits).
- **Mathematical result to remember cold:** The derivation gives $\mathbf{d}^* = (\hat{\beta}_1, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k) / \sqrt{\sum \hat{\beta}_i^2}$ — derived via Lagrangian optimization with the unit-length constraint.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response Surface Designs for First-Order Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT865 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Response Surface Designs for First-Order Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — RESPONSE SURFACE DESIGNS FOR FIRST-ORDER MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Response Surface Methodology (RSM)

**Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques that are useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes. RSM is formally defined as a sequential experimentation strategy for empirical model building and optimization, in which the objective is to approximate the true but unknown functional relationship between a response (output variable) and a set of independent input variables (factors) over a region of interest.

In the context of **first-order models**, RSM uses a planar (linear) approximation of the underlying true response function. The first-order model is the simplest polynomial approximation and serves as a "stepping stone" in the sequential strategy, particularly when the experimenter is located far from the optimum (away from the region of maximum or minimum response).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** First-order response surface designs are used to **screen factors** and determine the direction of steepest ascent (or descent) towards the optimum, NOT to find the exact optimum itself.

### 1.2 The First-Order Model — Formal Statement

The general **first-order model** in $k$ quantitative factors is given by:

$$y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$$

Where:
- $y$ = the observable response (scalar output)
- $x_i$ = the $i^{\text{th}}$ independent variable (controlled input), with $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$
- $\beta_0$ = the intercept (true mean response when all $x_i = 0$)
- $\beta_i$ = the linear (first-order) regression coefficient for factor $i$
- $\varepsilon$ = random error term, assumed $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ independently

In matrix notation, this becomes:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mountain Climber" Intuition

Imagine you are a **lost hiker on a foggy mountain** and you want to find the highest peak (the maximum response, e.g., maximum yield, maximum purity, or maximum strength). You cannot see the entire mountain because of the fog (the underlying function is unknown and complex). You can only feel the ground beneath your feet (you can run experiments at specific points).

Your strategy would be:
1. **First, find the general direction of ascent** — This is the role of the first-order model. If the slope of the ground in a given direction is steep and positive, you should walk that way.
2. **Walk in that direction** — This is the "method of steepest ascent."
3. **When the slope levels off, the terrain becomes curved** — At this stage, you switch to a second-order (quadratic) model to find the peak.

The first-order model is essentially a **flat inclined plane** fitted through the local region. It tells you the *direction* of improvement, not the *location* of the summit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** A chemical engineer trying to maximize the yield of a reactor, a mechanical engineer optimizing a CNC machining parameter set, or a metallurgist tuning an alloy's hardness — all begin with a first-order model to screen the most influential factors and walk towards better operating conditions.

### 1.4 Suitable Designs for First-Order Models

For fitting a first-order model, the design must satisfy a few conditions. The most common designs used in KTU-relevant RSM applications are:

- **$2^{k}$ Factorial Design** — $k$ factors, each at 2 levels. Provides $2^k$ design points.
- **$2^{k-1}$ Fractional Factorial Design** — Half fraction of the full factorial, used when $k$ is large (typically $k \geq 4$).
- **Plackett-Burman Design** — A specialized screening design for $k$ factors in $N = 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, \ldots$ runs (multiples of 4).

The defining geometric property of these designs is that the **prediction variance** is constant and equal at all points equidistant from the design center — i.e., they are **rotatable** or at least **symmetric** first-order designs.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick recall (KTU pattern):** Whenever the question states "first-order model," always think of $2^k$ or $2^{k-1}$ factorial designs with possible center points added for estimating pure error and checking curvature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Use a First-Order Model? (The "Why")

A first-order model is preferred in the **initial stages** of response surface exploration for three primary reasons:

1. **Simplicity** — Only $(k+1)$ parameters need to be estimated.
2. **Sparsity of runs** — Designs like $2^{k-1}$ fractional factorials require very few experimental runs.
3. **Screening ability** — It allows the experimenter to identify which factors have a substantial linear effect on the response and which are inactive.

The implicit assumption is that within the *current region of experimentation*, the true response function is approximately planar. This holds when the region is small or the function is locally smooth.

### 2.2 Assumptions Underlying the First-Order Model

The standard linear-model assumptions must hold:

- Errors $\varepsilon_i$ are **independent**, **identically distributed**, with $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
- The factor levels $x_i$ are **measured without error** (deterministic).
- The model is **correctly specified** in form (i.e., interactions and curvature are negligible).
- The variance $\sigma^2$ is **constant** across runs (homoscedasticity).

### 2.3 The Method of Least Squares

The unknown parameters $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_k)^T$ are estimated by **minimizing** the sum of squared residuals:

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^T \boldsymbol{\beta})^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

Taking the gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and setting it equal to zero yields the **normal equations**:

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

The **least-squares estimator** is therefore:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

### 2.4 The Method of Steepest Ascent (Descent)

Once the first-order model is fitted, the gradient of the fitted response surface is:

$$\nabla \hat{y} = \left( \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k} \right) = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$$

The **direction of steepest ascent** is proportional to the vector of regression coefficients:

$$\mathbf{d} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)^T$$

In practice, one factor is chosen as the **"step size reference"** (say, factor $j$), and all other factors are scaled proportionally:

$$\Delta x_i = \left( \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_j} \right) \cdot \Delta x_j, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k, \quad i \neq j$$

The experimenter then moves along this direction, conducting a series of experiments, until the response no longer improves. The new optimum region is then re-explored with a fresh design (often a second-order design).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|
| First-order model | $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$ | Linear in parameters |
| Matrix form | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | $\mathbf{X}$ is $n \times (k+1)$ |
| LSE of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Requires $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ to be non-singular |
| Fitted response at $\mathbf{x}_0$ | $\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{x}_0^T \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | $\mathbf{x}_0 = (1, x_{01}, \ldots, x_{0k})^T$ |
| Variance of $\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_0)$ | $\text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_0)] = \sigma^2 \, \mathbf{x}_0^T (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{x}_0$ | Variance depends on location $\mathbf{x}_0$ |
| Variance of $\hat{\beta}_i$ | $\text{Var}[\hat{\beta}_i] = \sigma^2 \cdot C_{ii}$ | $C_{ii}$ is the $(i+1, i+1)$ diagonal of $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$ |
| Steepest ascent direction | $\mathbf{d} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)^T$ | Sign indicates ascent ($\uparrow$) or descent ($\downarrow$) |
| Step size in factor $i$ | $\Delta x_i = (\hat{\beta}_i / \hat{\beta}_j) \cdot \Delta x_j$ | Choose one factor as reference |
| Total SS | $SS_T = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | $df = n-1$ |
| Regression SS | $SS_R = \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$ | $df = k$ |
| Residual SS | $SS_E = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $df = n - k - 1$ |
| Coefficient of determination | $R^2 = SS_R / SS_T$ | Closer to 1 is better |
| F-test for overall regression | $F_0 = (SS_R / k) / (SS_E / (n - k - 1))$ | Compare with $F_{\alpha, k, n-k-1}$ |
| Pure error SS (with $n_c$ center pts) | $SS_{PE} = \sum_{u=1}^{n_c} (y_{0u} - \bar{y}_0)^2$ | $df = n_c - 1$ |
| Lack of fit SS | $SS_{LOF} = SS_E - SS_{PE}$ | $df = n - k - 1 - (n_c - 1)$ |
| F-test for lack of fit | $F_0 = (SS_{LOF} / df_{LOF}) / (SS_{PE} / df_{PE})$ | Significant LOF ⇒ curvature present |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This system is heavily used in process optimization industries — pharmaceutical tablet formulation, semiconductor fabrication, food processing, polymer synthesis, and CNC parameter tuning. The method of steepest ascent dramatically reduces the number of experiments required to approach the optimum.

### 2.6 Diagnostic Checks Before Proceeding to Steepest Ascent

Before trusting the first-order model and the steepest ascent direction, KTU examiners expect you to check:

1. **$R^2$ and Adjusted $R^2$** — They should be reasonably high ($> 0.7$ is a common rule of thumb).
2. **Significance of individual coefficients** — Use $t$-tests: $t_0 = \hat{\beta}_i / \text{se}(\hat{\beta}_i)$.
3. **Lack-of-Fit Test** — The most critical check. If LOF is significant, the first-order model is **inadequate**, and a higher-order (second-order) model is required.
4. **Residual Analysis** — Plot residuals vs. fitted values, vs. run order, and a normal probability plot of residuals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Least-Squares Estimator

**Starting Point:** We wish to minimize the sum of squared errors

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^T \boldsymbol{\beta})^2$$

**Step 1 — Express in matrix form.** For a dataset of $n$ observations, let

$$\mathbf{y} = \begin{pmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1k} \\ 1 & x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2k} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{nk} \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \vdots \\ \beta_k \end{pmatrix}$$

Then the residual vector is $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$, and

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**Step 2 — Expand the quadratic form.**

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$** and set the gradient to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the normal equations.**

$$\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 5 — Second-order condition (positive definiteness).** The Hessian is $\partial^2 S / \partial \boldsymbol{\beta}^2 = 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$. This must be positive definite for a minimum, which is guaranteed when $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Variance of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$

Since $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$, and $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$,

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \boldsymbol{\beta} + (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

Hence

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

The covariance matrix is

$$\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = E\left[(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta})(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta})^T\right] = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T \cdot \sigma^2 \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$$

After simplification,

$$\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$$

In particular, the variance of the $i^{\text{th}}$ coefficient is $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_i) = \sigma^2 C_{ii}$, where $C_{ii}$ is the $i^{\text{th}}$ diagonal element of $(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$. An unbiased estimator is

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{n - k - 1}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Steepest Ascent Direction

**Problem (KTU-style):** A chemical engineer uses a $2^2$ factorial design with two factors, $x_1$ (temperature in °C, coded) and $x_2$ (pressure in bar, coded), and fits a first-order model. The fitted model is

$$\hat{y} = 50 + 2.5 x_1 + 1.0 x_2$$

The engineer wants to perform steepest ascent, choosing $x_1$ as the reference factor with step $\Delta x_1 = 0.5$ (coded units). Find the step size for $x_2$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the regression coefficients.** $\hat{\beta}_1 = 2.5$, $\hat{\beta}_2 = 1.0$.

**Step 2 — Compute the proportional step.**

$$\Delta x_2 = \frac{\hat{\beta}_2}{\hat{\beta}_1} \cdot \Delta x_1 = \frac{1.0}{2.5} \times 0.5 = 0.2 \text{ (coded units)}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.** For every 0.5-unit increase in $x_1$ (coded), $x_2$ should be increased by 0.2 coded units to walk along the steepest ascent path.

**Step 4 — Convert to natural units (if needed).** Suppose the temperature levels were $-1 = 40°C$, $+1 = 60°C$ (so a coded unit of 1 corresponds to 10°C). Then $\Delta x_1 = 0.5$ coded = $5°C$. If pressure levels were $-1 = 1$ bar, $+1 = 3$ bar, then $\Delta x_2 = 0.2$ coded = $0.2 \times 1 = 0.2$ bar.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Lack of Fit Test for First-Order Model

**Problem:** A $2^2$ factorial with 3 center points is run. The design has 4 corner points and 3 center points (total $n = 7$). The fitted first-order model is $\hat{y} = 75.2 + 4.3 x_1 + 2.1 x_2$. Compute the lack-of-fit test at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Data:**

| Run | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | 68.5 |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | 77.4 |
| 3 | $-1$ | $+1$ | 72.8 |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | 81.7 |
| 5 | $0$ | $0$ | 75.0 |
| 6 | $0$ | $0$ | 75.4 |
| 7 | $0$ | $0$ | 75.2 |

**Step 1 — Compute fitted values and residuals.**

Using $\hat{y} = 75.2 + 4.3 x_1 + 2.1 x_2$:

- Run 1: $\hat{y} = 75.2 - 4.3 - 2.1 = 68.8$, residual = $-0.3$
- Run 2: $\hat{y} = 75.2 + 4.3 - 2.1 = 77.4$, residual = $0.0$
- Run 3: $\hat{y} = 75.2 - 4.3 + 2.1 = 70.8$, residual = $+2.0$
- Run 4: $\hat{y} = 75.2 + 4.3 + 2.1 = 81.6$, residual = $+0.1$
- Runs 5, 6, 7: $\hat{y} = 75.2$, residuals = $-0.2, +0.2, 0.0$

**Step 2 — Compute pure error (from center points).**

$\bar{y}_0 = (75.0 + 75.4 + 75.2)/3 = 75.2$

$$SS_{PE} = (75.0 - 75.2)^2 + (75.4 - 75.2)^2 + (75.2 - 75.2)^2 = 0.04 + 0.04 + 0 = 0.08$$

$df_{PE} = n_c - 1 = 2$

**Step 3 — Compute $SS_E$ (sum of squared residuals).**

$$SS_E = (-0.3)^2 + (0.0)^2 + (2.0)^2 + (0.1)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + (0.2)^2 + (0.0)^2$$

$$SS_E = 0.09 + 0 + 4.00 + 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.04 + 0 = 4.18$$

$df_E = n - k - 1 = 7 - 2 - 1 = 4$

**Step 4 — Compute $SS_{LOF}$.**

$$SS_{LOF} = SS_E - SS_{PE} = 4.18 - 0.08 = 4.10$$

$df_{LOF} = 4 - 2 = 2$

**Step 5 — Compute the F-statistic.**

$$F_0 = \frac{SS_{LOF}/df_{LOF}}{SS_{PE}/df_{PE}} = \frac{4.10/2}{0.08/2} = \frac{2.05}{0.04} = 51.25$$

**Step 6 — Compare with critical value.** $F_{0.05, 2, 2} = 19.00$.

Since $F_0 = 51.25 > 19.00$, we **reject the null hypothesis** of no lack of fit. The first-order model is **inadequate** — there is significant curvature, so a second-order model is required.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this happening?** The Run 3 residual is huge ($+2.0$), which dominates $SS_E$. This single point signals that the response function is not planar — it has a quadratic (curvature) component.

### 3.5 Critical Comparison — When to Stay with First-Order, When to Move Up?

| Decision Criterion | Stay with First-Order | Switch to Second-Order |
|---|---|---|
| Lack-of-Fit F-test | Non-significant | Significant |
| $R^2$ value | Acceptable (e.g., $> 0.80$) | Low after first-order fit |
| Residual plots | Random scatter | Curved pattern |
| Goal | Continue moving along gradient | Pinpoint optimum within region |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Workflow of RSM with First-Order Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Problem and Response Variable] --> B[Screen Factors via Factorial Design 2k or 2k-1]
    B --> C[Add Center Points for Pure Error]
    C --> D[Run the Experiments and Collect Data]
    D --> E[Fit First-Order Model via Least Squares]
    E --> F{Test for Lack of Fit}
    F -- LOF Significant --> G[Augment with Axial Points or Switch to Second-Order Design]
    F -- LOF Not Significant --> H[Compute Gradient of Fitted Surface]
    H --> I[Determine Direction of Steepest Ascent]
    I --> J[Perform Sequential Ascent Experiments]
    J --> K{Response Still Improving?}
    K -- Yes --> J
    K -- No --> L[New Region Located - Re-fit with Second-Order Model]
    L --> M[Find Optimum]
    G --> M
```

### 4.2 Geometric Interpretation — Steepest Ascent Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Starting Region R0] --> A1[First-Order Planar Fit]
    A1 --> A2[Gradient Vector beta1, beta2]
    A2 --> A3[Steepest Ascent Path]
    A3 --> A4[New Region R1]
    A4 --> A5[Second-Order Curved Surface Fit]
    A5 --> A6[Optimum Found]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: First-Order Design Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        X1[Design Matrix X of size n times k plus 1]
        Y1[Response Vector y of size n]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Layer]
        C1[Compute X transpose X]
        C2[Compute X transpose y]
        C3[Invert X transpose X]
        C4[Compute beta hat]
        C5[Compute fitted y hat]
        C6[Compute residuals e]
        C7[Compute SS partitions]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Regression Coefficients]
        O2[Fitted Response Surface]
        O3[Variance and R-squared]
        O4[Steepest Ascent Direction]
    end
    X1 --> C1
    X1 --> C3
    Y1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C4 --> C5
    X1 --> C5
    Y1 --> C6
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7
    C4 --> O1
    C5 --> O2
    C7 --> O3
    C4 --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ and response vector $\mathbf{y}$ are the only inputs. The least-squares computation produces four key outputs: the coefficient vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$, the fitted response $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$, the goodness-of-fit measures ($R^2$, MS_E), and the steepest-ascent direction $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)^T$.

### 4.4 Decision Logic: First-Order Adequacy Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Fit First-Order Model] --> Q{Compute LOF F-statistic}
    Q -- F0 less than Fcrit --> R1[Accept First-Order Model]
    R1 --> R2[Proceed with Steepest Ascent]
    Q -- F0 greater than Fcrit --> S1[Reject First-Order Model]
    S1 --> S2[Augment Design with Axial Points]
    S2 --> S3[Fit Second-Order Model]
    S3 --> S4[Locate Stationary Point]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is a first-order response surface model? State its general form and mention two designs suitable for fitting it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A first-order response surface model is a linear polynomial approximation of the true response function within a localized region of the factor space, used in the initial screening phase of Response Surface Methodology.

The general form in $k$ factors is:

$$y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$$

where $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

Two designs suitable for fitting it are:
1. The $2^k$ full factorial design (with optional center points).
2. The $2^{k-1}$ half-fractional factorial design (used for $k \geq 4$ factors, especially when resources are limited).

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for general form, 1 for the two designs.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the method of steepest ascent. How is the direction of steepest ascent computed from a fitted first-order model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The method of steepest ascent is a sequential procedure used in RSM to move from the current region of experimentation toward the optimum (maximum or minimum) by following the direction in which the fitted first-order model increases (or decreases) most rapidly.

The direction of steepest ascent is given by the vector of estimated first-order regression coefficients:

$$\mathbf{d} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)^T$$

In practice, one factor (say, $x_j$) is chosen as the reference with a pre-defined step $\Delta x_j$, and the other step sizes are computed proportionally:

$$\Delta x_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_j} \cdot \Delta x_j, \quad i \neq j$$

A positive sign indicates ascent; a negative sign indicates descent.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the direction vector, 1 for the proportional step-size formula.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** *A $2^2$ factorial design with 3 center points is conducted to study the effect of temperature ($x_1$) and pressure ($x_2$) on the yield of a chemical process. The fitted first-order model is:*
$$\hat{y} = 82.4 + 3.6 x_1 + 1.4 x_2$$
*The coded levels are: temperature at $\pm 1$ corresponds to $\pm 10°C$ from a center of $80°C$; pressure at $\pm 1$ corresponds to $\pm 1$ bar from a center of $5$ bar.*
*Perform the following:*
*(i) State the least-squares estimator of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ in matrix form and compute the predicted yield at the design center and at the point $(x_1, x_2) = (+1, -1)$.*
*(ii) Compute the direction and step sizes for steepest ascent, choosing $x_1$ as the reference with $\Delta x_1 = 0.5$ coded units. Convert to natural units.*

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Least-squares estimator and predicted yields** (4 marks)

The least-squares estimator is $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$.

From the fitted model, $\hat{\beta}_0 = 82.4$, $\hat{\beta}_1 = 3.6$, $\hat{\beta}_2 = 1.4$.

- **At the design center** $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 0)$:

$$\hat{y}(0, 0) = 82.4 + 3.6(0) + 1.4(0) = 82.4$$

- **At the point** $(x_1, x_2) = (+1, -1)$:

$$\hat{y}(+1, -1) = 82.4 + 3.6(1) + 1.4(-1) = 82.4 + 3.6 - 1.4 = 84.6$$

**(ii) Steepest ascent direction and step sizes** (3 marks)

Direction vector: $\mathbf{d} = (3.6, 1.4)^T$. Since both coefficients are positive, this is a direction of *ascent*.

Step size for $x_2$:

$$\Delta x_2 = \frac{\hat{\beta}_2}{\hat{\beta}_1} \cdot \Delta x_1 = \frac{1.4}{3.6} \times 0.5 = 0.194 \text{ (coded)}$$

Conversion to natural units:
- $\Delta x_1 = 0.5 \text{ coded} \times 10°C = 5°C$
- $\Delta x_2 = 0.194 \text{ coded} \times 1 \text{ bar} = 0.194 \text{ bar}$

**Practical interpretation:** Starting from the center (80°C, 5 bar), one ascent step moves to (85°C, 5.194 bar).

---

**(b)** *Explain the procedure of fitting a first-order model using the method of least squares. Derive the estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ and the covariance matrix $\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$.*

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the model.** (1 mark)

For the first-order model, $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, where $\mathbf{X}$ is an $n \times (k+1)$ matrix with a column of ones and $k$ columns of factor levels.

**Step 2 — Define the objective.** (1 mark)

Minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^T \boldsymbol{\beta})^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**Step 3 — Expand and differentiate.** (2 marks)

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

Setting $\partial S / \partial \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$-2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$.** (1 mark)

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

The second-order condition (positive-definite Hessian $2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$) confirms this is a minimum.

**Step 5 — Covariance matrix.** (2 marks)

Substituting $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \boldsymbol{\beta} + (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

Since $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$:

$$\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T \cdot \sigma^2\mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$$

Hence $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_i) = \sigma^2 C_{ii}$, where $C_{ii}$ is the $i^{\text{th}}$ diagonal element of $(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *A first-order model is to be fit to study the effect of three factors on the tensile strength of an alloy. The data from a $2^3$ factorial design with 2 center points are as follows:*

| Run | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_3$ | $y$ (Strength) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | 320 |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | 380 |
| 3 | $-1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | 350 |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | 410 |
| 5 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | 340 |
| 6 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | 400 |
| 7 | $-1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | 370 |
| 8 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | 430 |
| 9 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | 372 |
| 10 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | 376 |

*(i) Fit the first-order model. State the regression coefficients.*
*(ii) Compute $SS_T$, $SS_R$, $SS_E$, $R^2$, and the F-statistic for overall regression significance at $\alpha = 0.05$.*
*(iii) Comment on whether the first-order model is adequate, given that the critical value is $F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.76$.*

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Fitted first-order model** (3 marks)

By the orthogonality of the $2^3$ design:

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} = \frac{320+380+350+410+340+400+370+430+372+376}{10} = \frac{3748}{10} = 374.8$$

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum x_1 y}{8} = \frac{(-1)(320)+(1)(380)+(-1)(350)+(1)(410)+(-1)(340)+(1)(400)+(-1)(370)+(1)(430)}{8}$$

$$= \frac{-320+380-350+410-340+400-370+430}{8} = \frac{240}{8} = 30$$

$$\hat{\beta}_2 = \frac{\sum x_2 y}{8} = \frac{-320-380+350+410-340-400+370+430}{8} = \frac{120}{8} = 15$$

$$\hat{\beta}_3 = \frac{\sum x_3 y}{8} = \frac{-320-380-350-410+340+400+370+430}{8} = \frac{80}{8} = 10$$

So the fitted model is:

$$\hat{y} = 374.8 + 30 x_1 + 15 x_2 + 10 x_3$$

**(ii) Sum of squares calculations** (3 marks)

- $SS_T = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 2472 + 27 + 612 + 1243 + 1212 + 637 + 23 + 3057 + 8 + 1.4$ ≈ let us recompute cleanly.

Actually, for a $2^3$ design, the total sum of squares is:

$$SS_T = \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}$$

$\sum y_i = 320+380+350+410+340+400+370+430+372+376 = 3748$

$\sum y_i^2 = 320^2+380^2+350^2+410^2+340^2+400^2+370^2+430^2+372^2+376^2$

$= 102400 + 144400 + 122500 + 168100 + 115600 + 160000 + 136900 + 184900 + 138384 + 141376 = 1414560$

$$SS_T = 1414560 - \frac{(3748)^2}{10} = 1414560 - 1404750.4 = 9809.6$$

$SS_R = 8(\hat{\beta}_1^2 + \hat{\beta}_2^2 + \hat{\beta}_3^2) = 8(900 + 225 + 100) = 8(1225) = 9800$

$SS_E = SS_T - SS_R = 9809.6 - 9800 = 9.6$

$R^2 = 9800 / 9809.6 = 0.9990$

$F_0 = \frac{9800/3}{9.6/6} = \frac{3266.67}{1.6} = 2041.67$

**(iii) Conclusion** (1 mark)

Since $F_0 = 2041.67 \gg 4.76$, the regression is highly significant. However, the small $SS_E = 9.6$ must be split into pure error and lack of fit before declaring adequacy — see next part.

**Note:** Center points mean $\bar{y}_0 = (372+376)/2 = 374$. $SS_{PE} = (372-374)^2 + (376-374)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$, $df_{PE} = 1$. $SS_{LOF} = 9.6 - 8 = 1.6$, $df_{LOF} = 5$. $F_{LOF} = (1.6/5) / (8/1) = 0.04$. Since $0.04 < F_{0.05, 5, 1}$, the lack of fit is **not significant**, and the first-order model is **adequate**. (This additional note adds value for full marks.)

---

**(b)** *Discuss the geometric interpretation of the first-order response surface. Explain the role of center points in testing for lack of fit, and outline the sequential strategy of moving from a first-order to a second-order design.*

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Geometric interpretation of the first-order surface** (2 marks)

A first-order response surface in $k$ factors is a *k-dimensional hyperplane* in $(k+1)$-dimensional space. Its gradient (vector of slopes) is constant everywhere, and the level contours (lines of constant $\hat{y}$) are equally spaced parallel hyperplanes perpendicular to the gradient vector. Geometrically, the method of steepest ascent moves in a straight line orthogonal to these contour hyperplanes.

**Role of center points in lack-of-fit testing** (3 marks)

Center points (replicates at $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$) are critical for testing lack of fit because:

1. They provide a **pure estimate of experimental error** $\sigma^2$ from replicate variation alone.
2. The total residual $SS_E$ can be partitioned into:
   - $SS_{PE}$ (pure error) — variation among center-point replicates
   - $SS_{LOF}$ (lack of fit) — the remainder
3. The F-ratio $F_0 = (SS_{LOF}/df_{LOF}) / (SS_{PE}/df_{PE})$ tests whether the first-order model adequately fits the data. A **significant F** indicates **curvature** is present, signaling that the first-order model is inadequate and a second-order design (e.g., central composite design or Box-Behnken design) should be used.

**Sequential strategy: first-order to second-order** (2 marks)

1. **Phase 1 — Screening:** Use $2^{k-1}$ fractional factorial to identify active factors.
2. **Phase 2 — First-order modeling:** Fit a linear model; if lack of fit is not significant, use the gradient to perform steepest ascent.
3. **Phase 3 — Locate region of optimum:** Continue ascent until response levels off.
4. **Phase 4 — Second-order modeling:** In the new region, augment the design with axial/star points to fit a quadratic model and find the stationary point.

This sequential approach minimizes experimental effort by escalating complexity only when needed.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (KTU Board Examiner Insight):**
> 1. **Confusing steepest ascent with steepest descent** — Always check the sign of the coefficients. A *positive* coefficient on a factor you wish to *maximize* indicates ascent; if you want to *minimize* the response (e.g., impurity), the same direction becomes descent.
> 2. **Forgetting center points when computing lack of fit** — A pure $2^k$ design has *zero* degrees of freedom for pure error. You cannot test LOF without replicates (typically center points). This is a top reason students lose 2-3 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Incorrectly partitioning $SS_E$** — $SS_{LOF} = SS_E - SS_{PE}$ and $df_{LOF} = (n - k - 1) - (n_c - 1)$. Do not confuse these formulas.
> 4. **Sign error in the regression coefficient** — In a $2^k$ design, $\hat{\beta}_i = (\sum x_i y)/n$ (not divided by $2^k$ alone, but properly normalized). Always verify the contrast formula.
> 5. **Skipping the conversion to natural units** in steepest ascent — Examiners love to deduct marks if you stop at coded units without converting to physically meaningful step sizes.
> 6. **Misinterpretation of non-significant LOF** — Non-significant LOF means the first-order model is *adequate* in the current region; it does **not** mean the optimum has been found. Continue the sequential procedure.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — First-Order Response Surface Designs**
